pages:
Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - The Theme Doctrine
Every investigation has a theme. The theme is the single coherent operational narrative that explains what
happened, why it was an offence, and why the evidence proves it. It is what a Sergeant briefing a Superintendent
says in the first sentence. It is the spine the cover report is built around. It is the lens through which the
prosecutor reads the file. The theme is not the chronology - chronology is what happened in what order; the
theme is the meaning of what happened.

1.1 Why a Theme Matters
A file without a theme is a stack of statements. The reader has to assemble the meaning themselves, and they
often will not, or will assemble it differently from the investigator. A file with a theme tells the reader, from
the first page, what they are looking at and what to expect. Every subsequent document confirms and extends
the theme. The investigation, on the page, becomes a coherent argument rather than a collection of paper.
In court, the theme is the prosecution's opening. Counsel for the prosecution stands and tells the jury, in a
paragraph, what this case is about. That paragraph is built directly from the cover report's theme. If the theme
has been clearly identified and communicated up the file, counsel inherits a coherent case; if not, counsel
constructs the theme from scratch, sometimes successfully and sometimes not.

1.2 Identifying the Theme - The THEME Mnemonic
Working through the THEME mnemonic at the start of every significant cover report or file submission forces
the theme out of the material and into words:

T - Threshold
What does the prosecution have to prove? The elements of the offence are the threshold. Identify them precisely
- actus reus, mens rea, capacity, jurisdiction. Everything in the file should connect to one of these elements.

H - Heart
What is the heart of this case? In one sentence, what is the most compelling single thread? Examples:
Theft case: 'CCTV shows the suspect taking the goods; the suspect was found in possession of the same goods
200 metres away.'
Assault s.3 case: 'The injured party gave a clear account; medical evidence confirms the injuries; an independent
witness identified the suspect.'
Coercive control case: 'The injured party's diary records the pattern over twelve months; three independent
witnesses describe the controlling conduct; the suspect's text messages confirm it in his own words.'
Drugs s.15 case: 'The quantity, packaging, scales, cash and intelligence together show possession was for
supply.'

E - Evidence
What evidence supports the heart? Each strand of evidence should be traceable to the heart of the case. Evidence
that doesn't connect either needs reconsidering or needs framing differently.

M - Motive
Why did the suspect act? Motive is not an element of most offences and not always provable, but where it exists
and is supportable it strengthens the theme enormously. In coercive control, motive is the case; in drug supply,
motive is the commercial picture; in DV homicide, motive is the sustained context.

E - Exposure
Where is the case weak? The honest investigator identifies the exposure before the defence does. Weak
identification? A gap in continuity? A reluctant witness? A contested caution? Acknowledge it in the cover
report; explain how it is addressed; never hope the prosecutor will not notice.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.3 Working Themes by Offence Category
Theft / Property
The recurring theme is possession plus dishonest intent. The compelling thread is usually the link between the
offender and the property - hands-on contact, possession after the fact, identification at the scene, association
with the means of taking (the vehicle, the tools, the accomplices).

Assault / NFOAP
The recurring theme is the application of force and the resulting harm, with intent or recklessness about the
harm. The compelling thread is usually the injured party's account corroborated by injury, witness, CCTV, and
the suspect's own conduct or admissions.

Domestic Violence / Coercive Control
The recurring theme in coercive control is the pattern over time. A single incident may be the trigger for the
report, but the case is built from the accumulated context. The compelling thread is the pattern made visible through the IP's account, third-party witnesses, communications, and the suspect's own words capturing the
control.

Drugs - Possession for Supply
The recurring theme is that the seized material was for sale or supply, not personal use. The compelling thread
is the combination of factors that exclude personal use - quantity beyond personal threshold, packaging
consistent with dealing, the apparatus (scales, deal bags), cash in dealer denominations, the location, the
intelligence picture.

Sexual Offences
The recurring theme is the absence of consent or the inability to consent, with reasonable belief in consent
absent on the facts known to the suspect. The compelling thread is the complainant's account, supported by the
early disclosure, the conduct of the parties, forensic material where present, and any communications or
behaviour around the incident.

Public Order
The recurring theme is conduct in a public place that engaged the statutory standard - intoxication causing
concern, threatening or abusive language or behaviour, disorderly conduct. The compelling thread is the
member's own observation in the moment, corroborated by BWV and any other witness.

Road Traffic - Drink Driving
The recurring theme is impairment plus driving, established by the statutory testing procedure. The compelling
thread is the procedural compliance with section 10 through section 14 of the Road Traffic Act 2010 - the form,
the calibration, the chain. Substance plus procedure is the case.

1.4 The Theme as Discipline
The theme is the test that every paragraph of the cover report has to pass. If a paragraph does not contribute to
the theme, it does not belong in the cover report (it may belong in the file, but not in the synthesis). If a piece
of evidence does not connect to the theme, either the theme is wrong or the evidence is irrelevant - either way,
the discipline is to find the answer before submission.
The theme also disciplines investigative effort during the case. With the theme identified early, the investigator
can see what is missing - the gap in the evidence chain, the un-interviewed witness, the un-recovered exhibit and address it before submission. Without the theme, gaps remain invisible until the file is reviewed.

1.5 Theme Failures

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chronology without narrative - a cover report that lists events in order but does not say what they mean.
Missing the heart - a cover report that buries the strongest evidence in the middle of a long list.
Multiple competing themes - a case where two or three different narratives are presented without choosing,
leaving the reader unsure which case is being made.
Theme without exposure - a cover report that omits the weak points; the defence finds them, and the prosecutor
was not prepared.
Theme imposed on the witness - the witness's account is shaped to fit the theme rather than the theme shaped
to fit the account; a serious integrity failure with a real evidential cost.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Cover Reports: Architecture and Theory
A cover report is the synthesising document that accompanies an investigation file. It is read by the supervising
Sergeant, the District Officer, sometimes the Superintendent, and ultimately by the prosecutor in the DPP's
Directing Section or the State Solicitor. The cover report tells those readers what the case is, what is in the file,
why the elements of the offence are met, and what the recommendation is. It is the bridge between investigation
and prosecution.

2.1 Purpose
To present the case coherently to readers who were not at the scene.
To synthesise material that is otherwise spread across multiple statements, exhibits, and technical reports.
To analyse - showing the prosecutor how the evidence meets each element of the offence.
To recommend - what charge, what court, what considerations for disposal.
To preserve - the cover report is the record of how the case stood at the moment of submission, useful for years
afterwards.

2.2 Audience
The cover report has multiple audiences, layered:
Immediate supervisor (Sergeant) - reviews for completeness and quality before forwarding.
District Officer (Inspector / Superintendent) - approves the recommendation, may direct further investigation.
Prosecutor (DPP or State Solicitor) - decides charge, court, and prosecution strategy.
Court of trial - the cover report itself rarely goes to court, but the prosecutor's opening and case strategy flow
directly from it.
The cover report must work for all audiences simultaneously. A document that satisfies the Sergeant but loses
the prosecutor has failed.

2.3 The COVER Architecture
Every well-built cover report follows the COVER architecture:

C - Charge clearly stated
The first substantive content names the proposed charge and its statutory basis. The prosecutor reading the
cover report should know within fifteen seconds what offence is being put forward.

O - Outline of the offence and how it engages the elements
A short narrative of what happened, written in operational language, with explicit reference to how each element
of the offence is engaged. Not chronology dumped onto the page - the narrative version of the theme.

V - Voice of the evidence
The evidence list: statements (witness, IP, Garda, specialist), exhibits, technical reports (forensic, medical,
FCI), CCTV, BWV, communications data. Each item identified, dated, and cross-referenced to the file's exhibit
/ witness numbering.

E - Examination
The analytical heart of the cover report. Each element of the offence taken in turn; the evidence supporting that
element identified; any weaknesses acknowledged and addressed; defences anticipated; case law applied.

R - Recommendation
The proposed disposition: charge, court of trial, any specific directions sought, any sentencing considerations.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The recommendation is the investigator's professional view; the DPP may agree or differ, but the
recommendation should be substantiated and defendable.

2.4 The Standard Elements
Beyond the COVER architecture, certain administrative elements are standard:

2.5 The Theme in a Cover Report
The theme (Chapter 1) is the organising spine of the cover report. The brief case summary states the theme.
The outline narrative develops it. The examination tests it against each element. The recommendation flows
from it. A cover report whose summary, outline and examination all point in the same direction has integrity;
one that pulls in different directions loses the reader.

2.6 Common Defects in Cover Reports
Chronology dumped without narrative analysis - reading like a notebook expanded.
Charge stated but elements not analysed - the prosecutor cannot see how the case meets the offence.
Weak points concealed rather than addressed - the prosecutor finds them on first reading and loses confidence.
Exhibit and witness references missing - the prosecutor cannot navigate from the report to the underlying
material.
Case law not engaged - the obvious authority (Walsh, JC, Avadenei) not referenced where it matters.
Recommendation not substantiated - 'I recommend prosecution' without saying why this rather than caution /
charge sheet only / summons only.
Tone inappropriate - too informal, too argumentative, too speculative. The cover report should read as a
measured operational analysis, not as advocacy.
Length disproportionate - either a paragraph that omits the analysis, or a sprawling document that buries it.

2.7 Length and Style
There is no fixed length. A summary disposal cover report (public order, minor theft) may be one to two pages.
An indictable file (s.4 NFOAP, s.15 MDA, s.39 coercive control) typically runs to three to six pages of cover
report, supporting an underlying file that may run to hundreds of pages. A complex investigation (homicide,
organised crime) may have a cover report of fifteen to thirty pages or longer.
Style: third person, past tense, plain language, sentences of moderate length, paragraphs of moderate length,
headings used to navigate. No argumentation against the suspect - the function is to present the case for the
prosecutor's decision, not to advocate. No personal observation about the suspect's character beyond what is
operationally relevant.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - Cover Report Types and Templates
Different stages and purposes of investigation call for different types of cover report. Each has its own
architecture within the broader COVER framework. This chapter sets out the principal types with template
structures.

3.1 The Initial / Interim Cover Report
Submitted at an early stage of investigation, usually after first interview or arrest.
Purpose: to put the supervisor on notice of the case, capture the position at the time, and request resources /
directions for the next stages.
Tone: descriptive rather than recommendatory; the case is not yet ready for a charging decision.
Structure: short summary; what is in hand; what remains to be done; resource / direction request.

3.2 The DPP Prosecution File Cover Report
Submitted when the investigation is complete and a charging decision is sought.
Purpose: to enable the prosecutor to decide charge, court and disposal.
Full COVER architecture; all standard elements; substantive examination.
This is the principal form treated in this chapter.

3.3 The Supplementary Cover Report
Submitted after the principal file, to add material that emerged subsequently.
Purpose: to bring the prosecutor up to date - new statement, new exhibit, forensic return, additional charge
consideration.
Structure: brief recap of the case; specific new material; analytical effect on the case; updated recommendation
if any.
Cross-reference back to the principal file is essential; the supplementary is read alongside, not instead of.

3.4 The Intelligence Cover Report
Used where the investigation has intelligence dimensions - covert, sensitive, source-protective.
Two-file structure typical: a main file with the disclosable case; a separate sensitive cover and source file
accessible only on the need-to-know principle.
The sterile corridor (Volume 10 Chapter 8) is the operational backbone - the disclosable file rests on
independent evidentially-clean material; the sensitive cover explains the intelligence basis where required.
Co-ordination with the prosecutor's office on PII analysis is typically required before submission.

3.5 The Exhibit / Continuity Cover Report
Used where exhibits and continuity are particularly central to the case (drug seizures, firearms, electronic
devices).
Includes detailed PEMS continuity chain, seizure documentation, technical examination reports.
Often built as an appendix or accompanying document to the principal cover, not a standalone.

3.6 The Missing Persons / Sudden Death Review Cover Report
Used for ongoing matters that have not (yet) progressed to prosecution.
Purpose: to record the investigation's state for supervisor review, family engagement, and Coroner's processes.
Structure: case summary; chronology of investigative steps; status; family engagement record; outstanding
actions; review schedule.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.7 Template Structure - DPP Prosecution Cover Report
A typical full cover report follows this template structure. The detail in each section varies with case complexity.
Items in square brackets are placeholders to be filled.

Header
[STATION] Garda Station
[DISTRICT], [DIVISION]
File Reference: [PULSE INCIDENT NUMBER]
Date of Report: [DD/MM/YYYY]
To: District Officer, [DISTRICT]

Subject
Re: [SUSPECT NAME] - [PROPOSED CHARGE] - [LOCATION] - [DATE OF OFFENCE]

1. Suspect Particulars
Name: [FICTITIOUS NAME]
Date of Birth: [DD/MM/YYYY]
Address: [SAMPLE ADDRESS]
Status: [In custody / On bail / Charge by summons proposed]
PULSE Person ID: [PID]

2. Brief Summary
[One paragraph - the theme of the case. What happened, what offence, what the principal evidence is. The
reader who reads nothing else should know the case from this paragraph.]

3. Proposed Charge
[Statutory section and Act, with brief description. E.g.: 'Section 3 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person
Act 1997 - assault causing harm.']

4. Narrative of the Offence
[Plain-language narrative of what happened, written in third person past tense. References to witnesses and
exhibits as 'witness 1', 'exhibit A' etc.]

5. Evidence Schedule
Statements:
- Statement 1: [Witness name / IP / Garda member, date, brief description]
- Statement 2: ...
Exhibits:
- Exhibit A: [Description, date seized, PEMS reference]
- Exhibit B: ...
Technical reports: [Forensic, medical, FCI, FSI, MBRS as applicable]
CCTV / BWV / digital: [Description, location, period]
PULSE references: [Incident, intelligence as appropriate]

6. Examination - How the Evidence Meets the Elements
[The analytical heart. Each element of the offence taken in turn; evidence connected to each element;
weaknesses acknowledged.]

7. Anticipated Defence

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

[What the defence is likely to be; how the case addresses it; case law where relevant.]

8. Antecedents
[The suspect's relevant prior convictions and ongoing matters.]

9. Case Law Considerations
[Where relevant authorities are engaged - Walsh on arrest, JC on admissibility, Avadenei on statutory form,
Damache on warrant, etc. Brief application to the case.]

10. Recommendation
[The proposed disposition - charge, court (District / Circuit), summons / charge sheet, any specific directions
sought.]

11. Signatures
Investigator: [Rank, Name, Badge No., Station, Signature, Date]
Supervisor Endorsement: [Rank, Name, Comments, Signature, Date]

3.8 Common Cover Report Phrasing
Certain phrasings are common across cover reports and are useful to internalise:
'On [date] at approximately [time], at [location], the alleged offence occurred in the following circumstances...'
'The matter came to Garda attention when [origin of the report] at [time].'
'The first member at the scene was [rank and name], who [actions taken].'
'A statement was taken from the injured party on [date], reference [Statement 1 in file].'
'CCTV from [location, e.g. Spar Talbot Street] was recovered. It depicts...'
'The suspect was arrested under [statutory power] at [time, location] by [member]. The arresting member's
grounds were [summary].'
'During interview, conducted at [station] in the presence of [solicitor], the suspect [account / admissions /
denials]. The full interview is at Statement [reference].'
'The element of [element name] is supported by [specific evidence].'
'It is recommended that the matter proceed by way of [charge sheet / summons], for prosecution at the [District
/ Circuit] Court.'

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 4 - Cover Report Worked Examples
This chapter sets out five fully-worked cover reports across the principal offence categories. All names,
addresses, reference numbers, dates and circumstances are fictitious and illustrative only. The detail level
matches what a DPP file would contain; the structure follows Chapter 3's template. Reading these alongside
Chapters 1 and 2 makes the architecture concrete.
Fictitious examples
Every name, address, file reference, and circumstance in this chapter is invented. The names used (John Murphy,
Mary Walsh, Patrick O'Brien and so on) are stock placeholder names. Any resemblance to real persons or events
is coincidental. The purpose is illustration of structure and theme - the substantive case law and procedure are
accurate; the facts are not.

4.1 Worked Example - Theft From Shop (s.4 Theft and Fraud Offences Act
2001)
File Reference
Mountjoy Garda Station - DMR North Central
File Ref: PI-2026/SAMPLE-001
Report Date: 15/05/2026
To: Superintendent, DMR North Central

Subject
Re: John Murphy (DOB 04/08/1996) - Theft contrary to s.4 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act
2001 - Easons Talbot Street - 02/05/2026

1. Suspect Particulars
Name: John Murphy
DOB: 04/08/1996
Address: 14 Sample Street, Dublin 1 (fictitious)
Status: Released on station bail; summons proposed
PULSE Person ID: P-FICT-12345

2. Brief Summary
On 02/05/2026 at approximately 14:30 the suspect entered a city centre retail premises, selected six items of
stationery to the value of EUR 87.50, and left without payment. CCTV captured the entire incident from
approach to exit. The suspect was identified by a Garda member from the recording on the same day and was
located 200 metres from the premises in possession of the items, still bearing the shop's security tags. He
admitted the conduct at interview.

3. Proposed Charge
Section 4 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 - theft.

4. Narrative of the Offence
At approximately 14:30 on 02/05/2026, John Murphy entered Easons Talbot Street. He moved through the
stationery section, selected six items (described in the exhibit schedule), placed them into a shoulder bag he
was carrying, and walked past the till point and out through the main door without making any attempt to pay.
The store's CCTV system recorded the conduct from entry to exit.
The store's security personnel did not engage the suspect on exit. The matter came to Garda attention when the
store manager, Witness 1, made a report at Store and Forward Easons at 14:45 by telephone to the Garda station.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Member A attended the premises at 14:55, viewed the CCTV, and obtained a copy.
At 15:20 Garda Member A, while returning to the station with the CCTV exhibit, observed a male matching
the description and clothing depicted in the recording at the junction of Talbot Street and Marlborough Street.
The male was identified to the satisfaction of the member by direct comparison with the CCTV stills. The
member approached, identified himself, and arrested the male under section 4 of the Criminal Law Act 1997
for the suspected offence of theft contrary to section 4 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act
2001.
On searching the suspect's person (s.6 Criminal Justice Act 1984, search incident to arrest), the six items
depicted on the CCTV were located inside the suspect's shoulder bag, with the store's security tags still attached.
The items were seized as exhibits.
The suspect was conveyed to Mountjoy Garda Station, where he was processed at 15:45 under section 4 of the
Criminal Justice Act 1984. He consulted a solicitor; on commencement of interview at 17:20, after caution and
statutory warnings, he made a full admission of having taken the items without payment.

5. Evidence Schedule
Statement 1: Witness 1 (store manager) - dated 02/05/2026 - identifies the items taken, the absence of payment,
and the CCTV recovery.
Statement 2: Garda Member A - dated 02/05/2026 - records attendance at the premises, CCTV recovery,
identification, arrest, search and conveyance.
Statement 3: Sergeant B (Member-in-Charge) - dated 02/05/2026 - records custody process, rights notification,
solicitor consultation.
Statement 4: Garda Member C (interviewer) - dated 02/05/2026 - records interview, caution, admission.
Exhibit A: CCTV recording (DVD) - Easons Talbot Street, 14:25 to 14:32 on 02/05/2026 - depicting entry,
item selection, exit without payment.
Exhibit B: Six items of stationery (described in seizure record) bearing security tags - seized from suspect's
shoulder bag at 15:25 on 02/05/2026 - PEMS Ref FICT-001.
Exhibit C: Receipt from store confirming retail value EUR 87.50 - dated 02/05/2026.
Exhibit D: Audio recording of interview - 02/05/2026, 17:20-17:48.

6. Examination - How the Evidence Meets the Elements
The elements of theft under s.4 are: (a) dishonest appropriation, (b) of property belonging to another, (c) without
consent, (d) with intent permanently to deprive.
Appropriation: CCTV captures the suspect selecting and bagging the items - exhibits B and A.
Property of another: Witness 1 confirms ownership; receipt (Exhibit C) confirms commercial stock.
Without consent: CCTV captures exit past the till without payment; Witness 1 confirms no transaction recorded.
Dishonest: the act of bypassing the till and exiting with concealed items satisfies the test; the admission at
interview is corroborative.
Intent permanently to deprive: the suspect did not return to the premises; was located moving away with the
items; admitted the intent at interview.

7. Anticipated Defence
No substantive defence is anticipated given the CCTV, the recovery of the items still tagged on arrest, and the
admission. Possible procedural challenges would be limited; the identification flowed from direct visual
comparison with high-quality CCTV stills and is documented.

8. Antecedents
The suspect has two previous convictions for theft contrary to s.4 of the 2001 Act, in 2021 and 2024, both in
the Dublin Metropolitan District Court, disposed of by fine and Probation Act respectively. He is not subject to
current bail conditions or pending matters.

9. Case Law Considerations

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - the arresting member personally identified the suspect from the
CCTV recording before arrest. Grounds are articulated in his statement and notebook.
The People (DPP) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 - identification is direct visual comparison of the suspect with
CCTV stills by the same member, recorded contemporaneously. ADVOKATE-style considerations recorded
in Statement 2.

10. Recommendation
It is recommended that the matter proceed by way of summons in the District Court. The suspect's admission,
the documentary evidence and the antecedents support a contested-summary prosecution. The value of the
property places the case within District Court jurisdiction.

11. Signatures
Investigator: Garda Member A, Badge XX, Mountjoy GS, [signature], 15/05/2026
Supervisor Endorsement: Sergeant D, [comment], [signature], 16/05/2026

4.2 Worked Example - Assault Causing Harm (s.3 NFOAP 1997)
Subject
Re: Patrick O'Brien (DOB 11/03/1990) - Assault causing harm contrary to s.3 Non-Fatal Offences Against the
Person Act 1997 - Outside premises at North Strand - 14/03/2026

Brief Summary
In the early hours of 14/03/2026 the suspect struck an injured party twice to the face outside a licensed premises
on North Strand following a verbal exchange. The injured party fell, struck his head on the kerb, and sustained
a fracture to the right zygomatic arch and lacerations requiring sutures. The assault was witnessed by two
independent witnesses and recorded in part by external CCTV. The suspect was identified, arrested two streets
away, and made significant admissions at interview.

Proposed Charge
Section 3 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 - assault causing harm.

Narrative of the Offence
At approximately 02:15 on 14/03/2026, outside [licensed premises name on North Strand], the injured party
(Mary Walsh, DOB 22/09/1988) was in conversation with friends when she was approached by the suspect,
who is unknown to her, following an earlier verbal exchange inside the premises. The suspect, without warning,
struck the injured party twice to the right side of the face with a closed fist. The injured party fell, striking the
right side of her head on the kerb edge.
Two independent witnesses, Witness 1 and Witness 2, observed the assault from a distance of approximately
three metres. Both gave a description of the suspect (male, mid-thirties, dark jacket, distinctive cap). External
CCTV from the premises captured the approach and the first blow but did not fully capture the second blow
due to camera angle.
Garda Members E and F responded to the scene at 02:21 following a 999 call by Witness 1. The injured party
was unconscious for approximately twenty seconds; she was conscious but disoriented on Garda arrival, with
visible facial injuries and bleeding from the head wound. Ambulance was called and arrived at 02:32.
Garda Member G, on mobile patrol, was directed to the area by the radio dispatch. At 02:28 he observed a male
matching the description supplied by Witness 1 walking westbound on Amiens Street. The male was identified
by the distinctive cap. Garda Member G stopped the male, who gave the name [...]. Following confirmation by
radio that the description matched the suspect from the scene, the male was arrested under section 4 of the
Criminal Law Act 1997 for the suspected offence of assault causing harm under s.3 NFOAP 1997. The arresting
member's grounds were: (a) the description matched, (b) the distinctive cap matched, (c) the male was in the
immediate vicinity of the offence, (d) within minutes of the offence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The suspect was conveyed to Mountjoy GS and processed at 02:55 under s.4 CJA 1984. He consulted a solicitor
at 04:10. Interview commenced at 05:25 after caution. The suspect admitted being in the area; admitted the
verbal exchange; admitted striking the injured party once, while contesting the second blow.

Evidence Schedule
Statement 1: Mary Walsh (IP) - dated 16/03/2026 - account of the conversation, the approach, the first blow,
partial recollection of the second blow and the fall.
Statement 2: Witness 1 - dated 14/03/2026 - account of observation, description of suspect, 999 call.
Statement 3: Witness 2 - dated 14/03/2026 - corroborative account.
Statement 4: Garda Member E - dated 14/03/2026 - first response, scene observations, IP condition.
Statement 5: Garda Member F - dated 14/03/2026 - corroborative response.
Statement 6: Garda Member G - dated 14/03/2026 - identification, arrest, conveyance.
Statement 7: Sergeant H (MIC) - dated 14/03/2026 - custody process.
Statement 8: Garda Member I (interviewer) - dated 14/03/2026 - interview and admissions.
Statement 9: Medical statement from Dr [SPECIALIST] - dated 24/03/2026 - injuries, mechanism, prognosis.
Exhibit A: External CCTV from premises - 02:14-02:18 on 14/03/2026.
Exhibit B: 999 call audio - dispatched at 02:18.
Exhibit C: BWV from Garda Member E - 02:21-02:45.
Exhibit D: BWV from Garda Member G - 02:28-02:55 (arrest sequence).
Exhibit E: Photographs of IP's injuries - taken by Garda Photographer at 04:30 on 14/03/2026.
Exhibit F: Suspect's distinctive cap, seized on arrest - PEMS Ref FICT-002.
Exhibit G: Audio recording of interview - 05:25-06:10 on 14/03/2026.

Examination - Elements
The elements of s.3 NFOAP are: (a) assault (i.e. application or threat of force without consent), (b) causing
harm (defined in s.1 NFOAP as harm to body or mind including pain and unconsciousness), (c) intentional or
reckless.
Assault - direct application of force to the IP's face, captured on CCTV (first blow) and described by two
independent witnesses (both blows). Suspect admits first blow.
Causing harm - medical statement describes fracture to zygomatic arch, lacerations requiring sutures, and brief
loss of consciousness; well within the s.1 definition of harm.
Mens rea - intentional. Striking another person to the face with a closed fist twice satisfies intent. No suggestion
of self-defence on the evidence; suspect was the aggressor approaching the IP, not the other way around.

Anticipated Defence
Likely contest: defence may seek to limit liability to one blow rather than two. Address: Witness 1 and Witness
2 both observed two blows; CCTV captures the approach and first blow; medical evidence is consistent with
two impacts (one to face, then head striking kerb on falling).
Possible self-defence claim: not supported by the evidence; the suspect approached the IP, not vice versa.
Witnesses describe the suspect as the aggressor. CCTV supports.
Identification challenge unlikely: arrest within minutes of the offence, distinctive cap matched, suspect admits
being in the area and the verbal exchange.

Antecedents
The suspect has one previous conviction for s.2 NFOAP in 2023 (Dublin Metropolitan District Court, fine). No
other convictions; no current bail conditions.

Case Law Considerations
Walsh [1980] - arresting member's grounds (matching description, distinctive cap, vicinity, time proximity) are
articulated and recorded contemporaneously.
Gormley v DPP [2014] IESC 17 - solicitor consultation afforded pre-interview; admissions follow consultation

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

and caution.
Madden [1977] IR 336 - voluntariness of interview confirmed; suspect was not intoxicated to the point of being
unfit; rest provided; the admissions are clear.
Buck [2002] - no statutory inferences sought; case rests on direct evidence.

Recommendation
It is recommended that the matter proceed by way of charge sheet, contested at trial on indictment. Given the
injuries (fracture and unconsciousness), the case is suited to Circuit Court rather than District Court summary
disposal. A book of evidence will be prepared for service.

4.3 Worked Example - Coercive Control (s.39 Domestic Violence Act 2018)
Subject
Re: Patrick Murphy (DOB 17/06/1985) - Coercive control contrary to s.39 Domestic Violence Act 2018 Period 01/06/2024 to 28/02/2026 - 14 Sample Street, Dublin

Brief Summary
Over a period of twenty-one months the suspect engaged in a sustained course of controlling and coercive
conduct toward his partner, the injured party. The conduct included financial control, isolation from family,
monitoring of communications and movements, repeated belittlement, threats and occasional physical violence.
The pattern is established through the IP's detailed account, contemporaneous diary entries kept by the IP, three
independent witness statements (the IP's sister, a neighbour and a former colleague), text and WhatsApp
records, and the IP's GP records. A protective Safety Order has been obtained on emergency application.

Proposed Charge
Section 39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 - coercive control.

Narrative of the Offence
[Narrative summarising the pattern; key incidents identified by date; the cumulative picture rather than any
single incident; the IP's account anchored by the diary and corroborated by third parties.]

Evidence Schedule
Statement 1: Mary Walsh (IP) - dated 01/03/2026 - comprehensive account of the relationship and pattern.
Statement 2: Witness 1 (IP's sister) - dated 03/03/2026 - corroborates isolation pattern and observed control.
Statement 3: Witness 2 (neighbour) - dated 05/03/2026 - corroborates observed control, raised voices and tearful
state.
Statement 4: Witness 3 (former colleague) - dated 06/03/2026 - corroborates monitoring of communications
and isolation.
Statement 5: Dr [SPECIALIST] (GP) - dated 12/03/2026 - records of attendances and disclosures over the
period.
Statement 6: Garda Member J (responding member to incident 12/02/2026) - dated 12/02/2026.
Statement 7: Garda Member K (case investigator) - dated 14/03/2026 - investigation summary.
Statement 8: Sergeant L (MIC) - dated 15/03/2026 - arrest and custody.
Exhibit A: IP's diary, 28 entries over the period - PEMS Ref FICT-003.
Exhibit B: Extraction of suspect's text messages to IP, 01/06/2024 to 28/02/2026 - GCCB report Ref FICT-X.
Exhibit C: WhatsApp message screenshots provided by IP - 47 specific messages.
Exhibit D: GP medical records (consented release by IP under data access).
Exhibit E: BWV from incident attendance 12/02/2026.
Exhibit F: Audio recording of interview - 16/03/2026.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Examination - Elements
Section 39 requires: (a) a course of conduct (i.e. more than one occasion), (b) of a controlling or coercive nature,
(c) toward an intimate partner or former partner, (d) that has a serious effect on the relevant person (causing
fear of violence, or causing serious alarm or distress that has a substantial adverse effect on day-to-day
activities), (e) where the suspect knew or ought to have known the effect.
Course of conduct: the diary entries identify 28 specific incidents over the period; the IP's account, witness
corroboration and message records establish a sustained pattern.
Controlling and coercive nature: financial control (sole access to bank accounts, restriction of IP's earnings),
isolation (preventing contact with family, monitoring of phone), monitoring (location tracking, demand for
account access), belittlement and threats (text messages and witness corroboration), occasional physical
violence (incidents documented in diary and GP records).
Intimate partner: the parties were cohabiting partners throughout the period.
Serious effect: the IP describes constant fear, withdrawal from work and family, weight loss, sleep disturbance
and anxiety; the GP records confirm presentations consistent with the account.
Knowledge: the suspect's own messages threaten and demean the IP and reference the pattern; this is conduct
undertaken with knowledge of effect.

Anticipated Defence
Likely contest: the defence is expected to characterise the conduct as 'difficult relationship' rather than coercive
control; to challenge the IP's reliability; to point to gaps in third-party corroboration.
Address: the diary's contemporaneity (entries are dated and granular), the independent witnesses (three
sources), the suspect's own messages in his own words, and the medical record over time - the cumulative
picture answers the 'just a bad relationship' framing.
Possible recanting risk: this is a coercive-control case where the IP has historically returned to the relationship;
she has indicated commitment to the prosecution and supports are in place, but the case needs to be capable of
standing on the corroborative evidence. Section 16 CJA 2006 considerations have been flagged for the
prosecutor.

Antecedents
The suspect has no previous convictions. One prior domestic incident in 2022 was not progressed at the IP's
request; that incident is referenced in the file as context.

Case Law Considerations
Opuz v Turkey (2009) 50 EHRR 28 - the State's positive obligation under Articles 2, 3 and 8 ECHR engages
where there is sustained DV. The Garda response has documented every prior contact (Vol 4 framework).
Volume 4 Chapter 2 case law on s.39 prosecutions - early Irish authorities reinforce the need for the cumulative
picture; the diary, witness, message and medical strands are explicitly designed to deliver that picture.
Section 16 CJA 2006 (hostile witness / earlier inconsistent statement framework) flagged for the prosecutor's
awareness, given the prior recanting history.

Recommendation
It is recommended that the matter proceed by way of charge sheet for prosecution at the Circuit Court. The
cumulative evidence supports the threshold; the Safety Order is already in place; the IP is supported by
Women's Aid and is engaged with the prosecution.

4.4 Worked Example - Possession for Sale or Supply (s.15 Misuse of Drugs
Act 1977)
Subject
Re: Stephen Doyle (DOB 21/11/1992) - Possession for sale or supply contrary to s.15 Misuse of Drugs Act
1977 - 14 Sample Apartments, North Strand - 18/04/2026

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Brief Summary
Following intelligence-led investigation and surveillance, a search warrant under s.26 MDA 1977 was executed
at the suspect's apartment on 18/04/2026. The search recovered 312 grams of suspected cocaine in 28 deal bags,
47 grams of suspected cannabis herb, an electronic weighing scale, deal bags consistent with the packaging,
EUR 4,750 in cash in mixed denominations, and a mobile phone subsequently extracted by GCCB. The total
estimated street value is EUR 24,800. The combination of quantity, packaging, scales, cash, phone evidence
and surveillance product supports possession for sale or supply.

Proposed Charge
Section 15 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 - possession for sale or supply (cocaine and cannabis).
Consider s.3 simple possession as alternative if s.15 unsustained.

Narrative of the Offence
[Intelligence and surveillance background documented in sensitive file; the disclosable narrative rests on the
warrant execution and what was found.]

Evidence Schedule
Statement 1: Detective Garda M (lead) - dated 18/04/2026 - intelligence, warrant application, search.
Statement 2: Detective Sergeant N - dated 18/04/2026 - search and seizure detail.
Statement 3-7: Detective Gardai O, P, Q, R, S - corroborative search team accounts.
Statement 8: Garda Member T (interviewer) - 19/04/2026.
Statement 9: Sergeant U (MIC) - 18/04/2026.
Statement 10: FSI Analyst - dated 28/05/2026 - certificate confirming cocaine (purity 38%) and cannabis.
Statement 11: GCCB examiner - dated 02/06/2026 - phone extraction report.
Statement 12: Detective Garda V - dated 12/06/2026 - market value statement (cocaine deal bags at EUR 80
each, cannabis at street rate).
Exhibit A: 28 deal bags suspected cocaine, total 312g (gross) - PEMS Ref FICT-101.
Exhibit B: 47g cannabis herb - PEMS Ref FICT-102.
Exhibit C: Electronic weighing scale - PEMS Ref FICT-103.
Exhibit D: 145 unused deal bags (matching pattern) - PEMS Ref FICT-104.
Exhibit E: EUR 4,750 cash in mixed denominations - PEMS Ref FICT-105.
Exhibit F: Mobile phone - PEMS Ref FICT-106.
Exhibit G: Search warrant - dated 17/04/2026, issued by District Judge.
Exhibit H: BWV from search execution.
Exhibit I: Photographs of premises and items in situ before seizure.
Exhibit J: GCCB extraction report - phone communications consistent with dealing.

Examination - Elements (s.15 MDA)
Section 15 requires possession of a controlled drug, with the purpose of selling or otherwise supplying it. The
case is built on the cumulative picture (Volume 5 Chapter 3 framework):
Possession: physical control of the substances within the suspect's apartment is established by the search.
Controlled drugs: FSI certificate confirms cocaine and cannabis.
Purpose of sale or supply: the cumulative picture is built from quantity (312g cocaine in deal bags is well
beyond personal use), packaging (28 individual deal bags pre-prepared), scales, unused deal bags in quantity
(145), cash in dealer denominations (EUR 4,750 mixed), phone communications consistent with dealing
(GCCB extract), and the absence of personal-use paraphernalia consistent with personal-only possession.

s.15A Threshold Consideration
The total seized has an estimated value of EUR 24,800. This exceeds the EUR 13,000 threshold for s.15A MDA
1977. The case will be referred to the DPP with both s.15 and s.15A considered; if s.15A is directed, the

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

mandatory minimum sentence regime applies (subject to the exceptional and specific circumstances exception).

Anticipated Defence
Likely contest: defence may seek to characterise the quantity as bulk personal-use purchase; or may challenge
the chain of custody / search procedure.
Address: 28 individual deal bags pre-prepared, scale, 145 unused bags and cash combine to defeat the personaluse framing; chain of custody is documented from seizure through PEMS through FSI; warrant and execution
are documented and BWV-supported.
Possible Damache challenge: the warrant was issued by District Judge on sworn information; the underlying
intelligence is treated through the sterile-corridor approach with independent surveillance product available.

Antecedents
The suspect has previous convictions for s.3 MDA (simple possession) in 2018 and 2021, both fine disposals.
No s.15 or s.15A history.

Case Law Considerations
Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 - warrant by District Judge, not by Garda; independent issuance preserved.
Walsh [1980] - arresting members' grounds documented; intelligence picture supplemented by surveillance and
the warrant's sworn information.
JC [2015] - any procedural points addressed through documented compliance; inadvertent breaches addressed
openly.
Renald, Ducque, Long, Anderson - the s.15A sentencing line (Volume 5 case law) - flagged for the prosecutor's
consideration on charge selection between s.15 and s.15A.

Recommendation
It is recommended that the matter proceed by way of charge sheet, considered for prosecution at the Circuit
Criminal Court. The case will be submitted to the DPP for direction on whether s.15 or s.15A is the appropriate
charge. The suspect was charged before the District Court on 19/04/2026 and was remanded in custody. A book
of evidence is in preparation.

4.5 Worked Example - Public Order (s.6 POA 1994)
Subject
Re: Thomas Byrne (DOB 09/02/1998) - Threatening or abusive behaviour contrary to s.6 Criminal Justice
(Public Order) Act 1994 - O'Connell Street - 22/04/2026

Brief Summary
At approximately 23:40 on 22/04/2026 the suspect, observably intoxicated, engaged in threatening and abusive
behaviour toward members of the public on a busy thoroughfare. He shouted obscenities, made threatening
gestures toward passers-by, and refused a member's direction to desist. He was arrested under s.8 of the 1994
Act after the warning provisions were complied with. BWV captured the conduct, the warning, the failure to
comply, and the arrest. The case is a short summary file.

Proposed Charge
Section 6 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 - threatening, abusive or insulting behaviour.

Brief Narrative
Garda Members W and X were on foot patrol on O'Connell Street at 23:40 on 22/04/2026 when their attention
was drawn to a male, since identified as the suspect, shouting at passers-by outside a fast-food premises. The
conduct was observed for approximately ninety seconds. The suspect's voice was raised, he was making
aggressive gestures, and members of the public visibly moved to avoid him.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Member W approached, identified himself, and directed the suspect under s.8 of the 1994 Act to desist
and to leave the area. The direction was given clearly, in plain language, and was repeated. The suspect
responded with further abusive language and made no attempt to comply. Garda Member W arrested the suspect
for the offence under s.6 of the 1994 Act at 23:43.
The suspect was conveyed to Mountjoy GS, processed in custody at 00:05 on 23/04/2026. Given his apparent
intoxication, he was held in custody under s.4 CJA 1984 for processing and was released on station bail at 09:30
on 23/04/2026 following a period of sobering and a brief processing interview.

Evidence Schedule
Statement 1: Garda Member W - dated 22/04/2026 - observations, direction, arrest.
Statement 2: Garda Member X - dated 22/04/2026 - corroborative observations.
Statement 3: Sergeant Y (MIC) - dated 23/04/2026 - custody process and release.
Exhibit A: BWV from Garda Member W - 23:39-23:50 on 22/04/2026.
Exhibit B: BWV from Garda Member X - 23:39-23:50 on 22/04/2026.

Examination - Elements
Section 6 requires threatening, abusive or insulting behaviour in a public place with intent to provoke a breach
of the peace or being reckless as to whether a breach of the peace might be occasioned.
Public place: O'Connell Street is plainly a public place within s.3 POA.
Threatening / abusive / insulting behaviour: BWV captures both members observing and the suspect's conduct;
corroborative statements describe the gestures, the volume, and the language.
Intent / recklessness as to breach of the peace: the suspect persisted after warning by Gardai; members of the
public moved to avoid him; the conduct in a busy area at night is reckless to the threshold.

Anticipated Defence
Likely contest minimal given BWV. Possible mitigation rather than defence (intoxication, no recollection,
apology). Section 8 procedural compliance is documented; the warning was given, the failure to comply is
captured.

Antecedents
The suspect has three previous convictions under s.4 POA 1994 (intoxication in public place) and one under
s.6, all disposed of by Probation Act or fine.

Recommendation
Summary prosecution at the District Court by way of charge sheet. Given the antecedents, the matter is not
appropriate for adult cautioning. Probation Service involvement may be appropriate at sentence.

Chapter 5 - Statements: Architecture and Theory
Statements are the substance of the prosecution case. They are what travels with the file to the prosecutor, what
is served on the defence in the book of evidence, and what witnesses adopt or are taken to in court. The cover
report synthesises and analyses; the statements are the underlying truth on which everything rests. This chapter
sets out the architecture of statements and the disciplines that make them stand up.

5.1 Types of Statements

5.2 The Statement as Evidence
A statement made under s.10 of the Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act 1851, or under the modern equivalents in
different procedural settings, is the form in which most evidence is captured for prosecution use. The witness
signs the statement, attesting that it is true; the statement is served in the book of evidence; at trial, the witness
adopts the statement or is taken to it. A statement that is accurate, complete and well-structured carries the
witness through cross-examination; one that is loose, incomplete or contradictory creates exposure.

5.3 The STORY Architecture
Every well-built statement follows the STORY architecture:

S - Setting
Where, when, who is the speaker, in what capacity. The opening establishes context. For a Garda statement: 'I
am a member of An Garda Síochána stationed at Mountjoy Garda Station, DMR North Central. On [date] at
[time] I was on duty in [capacity].' For a witness: 'I am [name], [DOB], of [address]. I am [occupation]. I am
giving this statement in connection with...'

T - Trigger
What brought the speaker into contact with the relevant events? The phone call that diverted the patrol; the
moment the witness noticed the disturbance; the radio dispatch; the colleague's call for assistance. The trigger
anchors the account to a specific identifiable moment.

O - Observation
What was seen, heard, said, done. First-person, sensory, contemporaneous in spirit. 'I saw...', 'I heard...', 'I
said...', 'I did...'. Specific, dated, located. Each step in sequence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

R - Result
What flowed from the observation? An arrest, an exhibit seized, a witness identified, a statement taken, a charge
preferred. The result connects the witness's role to the eventual case.

Y - Yield to verification
The signature, the date, the witnessing. The statement closes with the speaker's signature, date, and (for Garda
statements) badge number and station. The closure makes the statement an evidential document.

5.4 Standard Elements of a Garda Statement

5.5 Standard Elements of a Civilian (Witness/IP) Statement

5.6 The Theme in Statements
The theme of the case (Chapter 1) sits behind the statements as a coherent organising idea, but it must not be
imposed on the witness. The investigator's job in taking a statement is to elicit the witness's complete account
in their own words, structured chronologically and clearly. The theme is the investigator's understanding of
what the totality of the evidence means; the statement is the witness's evidence.
The point matters because a statement that reads as if it were ghost-written to a theme will be cross-examined
as such. The defence will probe for whether the words are the witness's own. A statement that captures the
witness's own language, including their hesitations, qualifications and limits, has a credibility that polished
narrative does not.
That said, the statement should be coherent and complete. The role of the statement-taker is to ensure the
witness has covered everything relevant, structured the account chronologically, and clarified ambiguity. It is
not to impose words, but to elicit the words that are there.

5.7 Common Statement Defects
Gaps - the witness moved from one moment to another without describing the connecting events; the defence
will probe the gap.
Hearsay - the statement contains 'X told me Y said' without attribution; either the original speaker should be
the witness, or the hearsay should be expressly identified.
Opinion - the witness expresses opinions ('he was clearly drunk') without the underlying observations ('he was
unsteady on his feet, his speech was slurred, I smelled alcohol').
Conclusions - the witness offers conclusions on legal points ('he assaulted me') without the underlying conduct
('he struck me in the face with his closed fist').
Missing times - 'later that day' is weak; 'approximately 15:30' is strong.
Missing identifications - 'he then did X' without making clear which 'he' where multiple persons are involved.
Inconsistencies with other material - the statement contradicts BWV, CCTV or other statements without
acknowledging or addressing it.
Statement-taker's voice - the statement reads in the Garda's voice rather than the witness's; the defence will
exploit it.
Length disproportionate - either too short to be of use or so long that the key material is buried.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 6 - Taking Statements from Witnesses and
Injured Parties
Taking a statement is one of the most consequential acts in any investigation. The statement captured well is
the foundation of the prosecution; the statement captured poorly is a problem that will surface in court. This
chapter sets out the operational approach to statement-taking, drawing on the PEACE interview framework
adapted to the statement context.

6.1 Before the Statement
6.1.1 Setting
Quiet, private location - the witness room, the IP's home if appropriate, or a comparable space. Avoid openplan public areas.
Time - allow more time than feels necessary. Witnesses pause, recall, qualify, expand. A rushed statement reads
as rushed.
Tone - calm, supportive, attentive. The statement is the witness's contribution; the member's role is to enable
it.

6.1.2 Preparation
Background known: the basic facts of the offence; the witness's apparent role; any prior accounts (call-out
narrative, scene observations).
Specific topics to cover identified: the elements of the offence; the witness's specific knowledge; any contested
or ambiguous points.
Practicalities: water, breaks, support person if appropriate (particularly for DV / sexual offences / vulnerable
witnesses).
Mode of recording: written (typed by member from witness's account), or fully written by the witness, or videorecorded interview later transcribed (specific categories).

6.2 The PEACE-Aligned Statement Sequence
The PEACE model (Planning and Preparation - Engage and Explain - Account - Closure - Evaluation) was
developed for suspect interviewing but its principles apply directly to witness statement-taking.

6.2.1 Engage and Explain
Introduce yourself; rank, name, station.
Explain what is happening: 'I am going to take a statement from you about what you saw / what happened to
you. I'll ask you to tell me in your own words, and I'll write it down. Then I'll read it back to you, you can
change anything you want to, and you'll sign it.'
Explain the witness's role: 'It's important you tell me everything you remember, even if it seems small. If you
don't remember something, please say so - don't guess.'
Confirm capacity (e.g., voluntary, no inducement, fit to give account).
Establish rapport without compromising independence: a few minutes of human engagement before the
substantive material.

6.2.2 Account - The Free Narrative
Open invitation: 'Tell me, in your own words, what happened.'
Allow uninterrupted narrative as far as possible. Do not interrupt to clarify or correct unless absolutely
necessary.
Listen actively; take notes but do not write the statement yet.
Allow pauses; allow the witness to think; allow emotion.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Do not lead. Avoid suggestions like 'and then he hit you, didn't he?' or 'you must have been scared.' The defence
will scrutinise for leading.

6.2.3 Account - Probing
After the free narrative, return to specific topics for clarification and detail.
Use open questions: 'Tell me more about [moment]'; 'What did you see next?'; 'How did you know that?'
Probe for sensory detail: what was seen, heard, smelled, said, felt. Distance, lighting, duration, position.
Probe for time: 'How long did that last?'; 'About what time was it?'
Probe for identification: 'How did you know it was him?'; 'Had you seen him before?'; 'Was anyone else around?'
Address gaps: 'You mentioned X, then later Y - what happened in between?'
Address inconsistency: 'Earlier you said X; just now you said Y; help me understand.'

6.2.4 Account - Recap and Confirmation
Summarise the account back to the witness.
Confirm each significant point.
Allow the witness to correct, qualify, expand.
Confirm exhibits viewed and identifications made.

6.2.5 The Written Statement
With the account fully understood, the written statement is prepared - typically typed by the member from
notes, or hand-written, depending on local practice.
Drafted in the witness's voice, not the member's. Use the witness's own words, particularly for descriptions of
conduct and quotations.
Chronological, paragraphed for clarity.
All standard elements (Chapter 5.5).
Read back to the witness in full.
Corrections incorporated; the corrected statement read again if substantial changes.
Witness signs each page; member witnesses the signature; date recorded.

6.2.6 Closure
Thank the witness.
Explain next steps: 'You may be contacted again as the investigation develops. You may be required to give
evidence in court if the case proceeds. Court Services and Victim Services [or other relevant supports] are
available.'
Provide contact details for the investigating member.
Document the statement-taking session on PULSE and in the member's notebook (time started, time ended,
location, persons present).

6.2.7 Evaluation
After the witness has gone, review the statement against the case theme: does it cover what is needed; are there
gaps; do further statements need to follow.
Note any operational follow-up flagged in the account (other witnesses to interview, CCTV to recover, exhibits
to seize).

6.3 Sensitive Considerations
6.3.1 Domestic Violence Complainants
Trauma-aware approach - allow time; pause for emotion; do not push through distress.
Specialist Garda involvement (DARI / Divisional DV unit) where appropriate.
Safety planning alongside statement-taking; consider Safety Order / Protection Order application.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Recanting risk consideration - explain the s.16 CJA 2006 framework so the complainant understands that the
early statement matters even if her position changes later.
Support service signposting (Women's Aid, Dublin RCC if sexual element, Men's Aid where applicable).

6.3.2 Sexual Offence Complainants
Specialist victim interviewer (where available) - consider Section 16 CEA 1992 video-recorded interview for
complainants who would benefit.
SATU involvement and forensic timing considerations (Volume 6).
Long, paced, deeply patient approach.
Anonymity considerations - the complainant's identity is protected by statute; reflect that in any reference.
Continuing support and signposting (Dublin RCC, SATU after-care).

6.3.3 Child Witnesses
Section 16 CEA 1992 video-recorded interview by trained specialist.
Tusla notification under Children First Act 2015 where appropriate.
Appropriate adult, age-appropriate language, breaks.
Specific child-protection investigation framework (Volume 6 / Volume 8).

6.3.4 Vulnerable Adults
Section 21 CL(SO)A 2017 considerations where applicable.
Appropriate adult support.
Capacity assessment where indicated.
Pace adapted to the witness.

6.3.5 Reluctant / Intimidated Witnesses
Understand the basis of reluctance - safety fear, distrust, prior bad experience.
Witness anonymity / protection options where available.
Intimidation as itself a criminal matter (s.41 CJA 1999).
Honest engagement: 'I understand this is difficult. Here is what we can do.' Do not promise what cannot be
delivered.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

7.1 Garda Member's Statement - Arrest and Investigation
Statement of Witness
Made under Section 10 of the Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act 1851

Witness Details
Garda Member: Garda A. Member
Badge No.: [BADGE NUMBER]
Station: Mountjoy Garda Station, DMR North Central

Statement
I am a member of An Garda Síochána stationed at Mountjoy Garda Station in the Dublin Metropolitan Region
North Central Division.
On the 14th of March 2026 I was on duty in uniform attached to Mountjoy GS, performing a tour of duty from
22:00 hours on the 13th of March to 06:00 hours on the 14th of March 2026. I was assigned to mobile patrol
with Garda B. Colleague (Badge [...]) in marked Garda vehicle [vehicle ID].
At approximately 02:21 hours on the 14th of March 2026 we received a radio dispatch from Dublin Central
Control to attend a reported assault outside [licensed premises name], North Strand. We were approximately
800 metres from the location and proceeded directly to the scene, arriving at 02:24 hours.
On arrival I observed a female lying on the footpath approximately three metres from the entrance to the
premises. She was conscious but appeared disoriented, with visible facial injuries and bleeding from the right
side of her forehead. I was directed by a male civilian, who later identified himself as Witness 1, to that female.
I identified the female as the injured party, Mary Walsh (DOB 22/09/1988). I requested an ambulance through
Dublin Central Control. The ambulance arrived at 02:32 hours and the injured party was conveyed to the Mater
Hospital.
Witness 1 stated to me in summary that the injured party had been struck twice by a male, who had then walked
away westbound on Amiens Street. He described the male as approximately mid-thirties, wearing a dark jacket
and a distinctive flat cap. I broadcast this description on the radio at 02:25 hours.
I remained at the scene and identified a second witness, Witness 2, who confirmed the account of Witness 1 in
summary. I requested attendance of the Scenes of Crime Office for scene photography, which attended at 02:55
hours.
At 02:35 hours I was advised by radio that Garda C. Colleague had stopped a male matching the description on
Amiens Street. I attended that location at 02:40 hours and confirmed by direct visual comparison that the
stopped male matched the description given by Witnesses 1 and 2.
At 02:43 hours, having heard the radio confirmation of identification, Garda C. Colleague arrested the male,
since identified as Patrick O'Brien (DOB 11/03/1990), under section 4 of the Criminal Law Act 1997 on
suspicion of an offence contrary to section 3 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997. The
arrested male was cautioned in the standard form. He made no immediate reply.
The arrested male was conveyed to Mountjoy GS in marked Garda vehicle [vehicle ID]. I followed in vehicle
[vehicle ID]. We arrived at the station at 02:55 hours.

I returned to the scene at 03:15 hours to liaise with the Scenes of Crime Office, who completed their

7.2 Injured Party Statement - Assault Complainant
Statement of Witness
Made under Section 10 of the Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act 1851

Witness Details
Name: Mary Walsh
Date of Birth: 22/09/1988
Address: [Provided to Garda, redacted for cover - see file]
Occupation: Office Administrator

Statement
I am Mary Walsh, born on the 22nd of September 1988. I work as an office administrator. I live at the address
provided. I am giving this statement in connection with an assault on me on the 14th of March 2026 outside a
licensed premises on North Strand, Dublin.
On the night of the 13th of March 2026 going into the 14th of March 2026 I was out with two friends. We had
been at a few different places during the evening. We ended up at [premises name] on North Strand at about
midnight. I had been drinking but I was not very drunk. I would say I had about four drinks over the course of
the evening.
Inside the premises, sometime around 1:30am as I recall it, there was a verbal exchange between my friend and
a male we did not know. The male was annoyed about something, I am not sure what started it. I tried to calm
things down. The male said something to me that I cannot now remember exactly, but it was aggressive. The
bouncers came over and the male was asked to leave the premises. He left.
My friends and I stayed for a while longer. At some point after 2am, I went outside with one of my friends for
a cigarette. We were standing about three metres from the door of the premises. There were a few other people
standing around.
I saw the same male who had been asked to leave earlier coming back along the footpath toward me. I did not
at first think he was coming for me. He came right up to me. He did not say anything. He just hit me with his
fist on the right side of my face.
I remember the first hit very clearly. It was a closed fist; it came from his right side; it hit me on the right side
of my face, on my cheekbone. I remember the impact and the noise.
I remember being hit a second time. I do not remember the second hit as clearly as the first. I know I was hit
twice because of the witnesses and because I felt two separate impacts. After the second hit I fell. I struck my
head on the kerb on the right side. I think I lost consciousness for a few seconds.
The next thing I remember clearly is people around me, and then a Garda arriving. I remember being asked
questions but I was not able to focus. An ambulance came and I was brought to the Mater Hospital.
At the hospital I was examined. I had cuts to my head that needed stitches; I was told I had a fracture to my
cheekbone. I was kept in overnight. I went home the next day.
Since then I have had headaches. The cut on my head has healed. The cheekbone is still sore at times. I have a
follow-up appointment with the hospital in May.
I have been shown by Garda [...] a photograph of a male and I identified that male as the person who struck me.
I had not seen the male before that night, except inside the premises earlier when he was asked to leave.
I make this statement of my own free will. I understand that this statement may be used in court and that if I
have stated anything in it which I know to be false I am liable to prosecution.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Signed: [Mary Walsh] Date: 16/03/2026
Statement taken by: Garda A. Member, Badge [...], Mountjoy GS, on 16/03/2026 commencing at 11:00 hours
and concluding at 12:30 hours.

7.3 Independent Witness Statement
Statement of Witness
Made under Section 10 of the Petty Sessions (Ireland) Act 1851

Witness Details
Name: David Kelly
Date of Birth: 04/01/1987
Address: [Provided to Garda]
Occupation: Taxi Driver

Statement
I am David Kelly, born on the 4th of January 1987. I am a taxi driver. I am giving this statement about something
I saw on the 14th of March 2026 on North Strand.
I was working a night shift. At about ten past two in the morning on the 14th of March 2026 I had let off a fare
on North Strand near [premises name]. I was looking for my next fare. I was standing at the driver's door of my
taxi on the kerb side, about ten metres from the entrance to the premises.
There were a few people standing outside the premises. I noticed a man walking along the footpath toward the
entrance. He was wearing a dark jacket and a flat cap. I noticed him because of the cap, which was a sort of
brown colour and stood out. He was walking with a purpose. He did not look like someone going home; he was
heading for something.
I saw him approach a woman who was standing with another woman, smoking, near the entrance. He went
right up to her. I did not hear him say anything. He punched her in the face with his right fist. She did not have
time to react. He hit her a second time straight after, again with his right fist. She fell. I heard her head hit the
kerb.
The man with the cap then turned and walked away westbound, back the way he had come. He was not running,
just walking quickly.
I rang 999 immediately. While I was on the phone I went over to the woman. She was on the ground, bleeding
from her head. She seemed conscious but not really aware. I stayed with her until the Gardaí came, which was
only a few minutes.
I told the Gardaí what I had seen, including the description of the man and the direction he had gone. I gave my
details. I have been asked since then to identify the man from a photograph. I was shown a photograph and I
identified the man with the cap.
I make this statement of my own free will. I understand that this statement may be used in court and that if I
have stated anything in it which I know to be false I am liable to prosecution.
Signed: [David Kelly] Date: 14/03/2026

Operationally, the investigating Garda's role with specialist statements is to ensure exhibits are submitted with

Chapter 8 - Investigation Closure and Injured Party
Communications
Investigation does not end at charge or release. The injured party - and where the IP is deceased, the bereaved
family - has a right to know what happened to their case, where it stands, and what their options are. The
communication discipline that surrounds this is operational ethics expressed as workflow. A successful
prosecution that leaves the IP unheard is a partial failure; a case that does not progress to prosecution but is
communicated honestly and supportively to the IP preserves trust in the system. This chapter sets out the
framework, the categories of communication, and templates for each.

8.1 The Statutory and Policy Framework
8.1.1 The Victims' Rights Framework
Criminal Justice (Victims of Crime) Act 2017 - the principal statute on victims' rights in the criminal justice
process.
Victims Charter - the operational standard for the treatment of victims by Gardaí, the DPP, the Courts Service
and other agencies.
Convention rights - Articles 6, 8, 13 ECHR engage where the State response to crime against a victim is in
issue.
Garda policy on victim engagement - operational guidance supplementing the statutory framework.

8.1.2 Rights of the Victim Under the 2017 Act and Charter
Right to be informed about the investigation - status, significant decisions, outcome.
Right to information about supports available.
Right to language assistance (interpreter / translation).
Right to be heard, including through a Victim Impact Statement at sentence.
Right to specific protections during the criminal process (anonymity, special measures, contact arrangements).
Right to be referred to victim support services.
Right to review of certain decisions (e.g. DPP decision not to prosecute, in certain categories).
