8.3 General Principles of IP Communication
8.3.1 The CLOSURE Mnemonic
C - Contact maintained - the IP should never feel forgotten. Even where there is nothing to report, an
acknowledging message preserves the relationship.
L - Loop closed - when the case ends, the IP knows. No 'just heard nothing more' endings.
O - Options explained - the IP understands what they can do next, including any rights of review and support
services.
S - Support signposted - relevant support services identified, contact details provided.
U - Understand the IP's perspective - the case may be one of many on the member's caseload; for the IP it is
their case, often a defining experience.
R - Record the communication - on PULSE / file - what was said, when, by what means, by whom.
E - Empathy - tone matters. Procedural communication delivered with warmth is fundamentally different from
procedural communication delivered as a form letter.

8.3.2 What Can and Cannot Be Shared
CAN: case status (open, charged, in court, concluded); the IP's own statement and the use being made of it; the
IP's rights and options; court dates and outcomes; supports.
CANNOT: investigative detail that could compromise the case (intelligence, ongoing covert work, witness
identities); third-party private information; suspect details before charge in many circumstances (consult
supervisor / prosecutor); operational sensitive material.
ALWAYS HONEST: where there is no news, say so; where the case is going slowly, say so; where the case is
not going to proceed, say so promptly.
NEVER OVER-PROMISE: avoid suggesting an outcome before it occurs; never assure conviction; never
assure a specific charge or court.

8.4 Communication Modes
In-person - the strongest mode for significant communications (initial engagement, charge notification,
outcome). Combines tone with substance.
Telephone - the standard mode for routine updates and 'fill the gaps' contact. Faster than in-person, warmer
than written.
Written (post / email) - useful for formal notifications, for matters of record, and where in-person / phone has
not succeeded. Slower; less personal; cannot read tone.
SMS / messaging - increasingly common for short procedural communications (hearing date confirmations);
not appropriate for substantive updates.
Through Family Liaison Officer (FLO) - mandatory for homicide and similar; the FLO carries communications
as part of sustained engagement (Volume 8 Chapter 4).

8.5 Sample Templates
The templates below are starting structures, not scripts. Each must be adapted to the specific case and the
specific injured party. The tone is more important than the wording. All names are fictitious.

8.5.1 Template - Initial Engagement (Within 24 Hours of Report)
[For first-contact engagement, typically by phone or in person. Tone: acknowledging, calming, informational.]
Initial Contact Template - Sample Text
Hello [Mr/Ms Walsh], my name is [Garda Name] from Mountjoy Garda Station. I'm the member who has been
assigned to your case. I'm following up on the report you made on [date]. First, I want to acknowledge how
difficult what happened must have been. I'm here to take you through the investigation step by step.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

I'd like to arrange to take a full statement from you. We can do this at the Garda station, or at your home, or at
another place where you feel comfortable. The statement is important because it's the foundation of the case. I'll
go through it with you carefully and we'll take whatever time we need.
I want to give you my direct contact details. My phone number is [...]. If you don't get me, leave a message and
I'll come back to you. If something urgent arises outside of working hours, call 999 or the station main line on
[...].
There are support services available to you. [Name relevant service - Women's Aid, Crime Victims Helpline,
Dublin RCC, etc.]. I'll give you their details and you can contact them at any time, with or without telling me.
I'll keep you updated as the investigation progresses. If there's something significant - if someone is arrested, or
if there's a change in direction - I'll be in touch. If nothing significant has happened, I'll still check in with you so
you know where things stand.
Do you have any questions for me now?

8.5.2 Template - 'Fill the Gaps' Update (Periodic, Nothing Major to Report)
[For periodic contact when the case is open but nothing significant has happened. Tone: maintaining contact,
acknowledging the silence, honest about progress.]
Fill the Gaps Template - Sample Text
Hello [Mr/Ms Walsh], it's [Garda Name] from Mountjoy. I wanted to check in with you on your case.
I want to be honest with you - there's no major update to give you today. The case is still open and being worked
on. [Optional: brief mention of what is in train if shareable - e.g., 'We're waiting on a forensic result from FSI,
which can take time'; or 'We've spoken with two more witnesses since I last spoke to you'.]
I know that hearing 'nothing major' can be frustrating when this is so important to you. I want you to know your
case has not been forgotten and is being actively progressed. I'll be in touch again [in approximately X weeks /
when there is news / on our next agreed contact date].
How are you doing in yourself? Is there anything I can help with or anyone you need me to put you in touch
with?
If anything has come to your attention that you think I should know - if you've remembered something, or if
anything has happened to make you concerned - please tell me now or call me. My number is [...].

8.5.3 Template - Suspect Arrested / Charged
[For notifying the IP that the suspect has been arrested or charged. Tone: informational, careful about
expectations, supportive.]
Arrest / Charge Template - Sample Text
Hello [Mr/Ms Walsh], [Garda Name] from Mountjoy here. I have some news on the case.
I want to let you know that we arrested [or charged] a person yesterday in connection with the incident on [date].
I'm not in a position to share every detail at this stage, but I can tell you that the investigation has moved on
significantly.
[If charged: ] The person has been charged with [offence in plain language - e.g., assault causing harm], and the
case will now go through the courts. The first court date is [date] at [court]. You don't need to attend that hearing
- it's an initial appearance. If you want to attend, you can; I can go through what to expect.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

[If on bail: ] The person has been released on bail with conditions. The conditions are designed to protect you.
They include [conditions in summary - no contact, no approach, exclusion zone, etc.]. If the person breaches any
condition, contact me or call 999. The conditions don't expire until the court decides otherwise.
I want to be careful not to over-promise. A charge is a long way from an outcome, and court cases can take time.
I'll keep you updated on every significant date and decision.
What I want you to know now: the case has progressed; your statement is essential to it; we'll be with you through
the process. Do you have questions?

8.5.4 Template - Outcome (Conviction)
[For notifying the IP of a conviction. Tone: clear, factual, supportive, careful about emotional weight.]
Outcome Template - Conviction - Sample Text
Hello [Mr/Ms Walsh], [Garda Name]. I'm calling with news on the court case.
The case concluded yesterday at [court]. The accused was convicted of [offence in plain language] and was
sentenced to [sentence in plain terms - e.g., 'two years' imprisonment with the final six months suspended', or 'a
fine of EUR X with a Probation Service direction'].
I want to acknowledge what this means. You went through the report, the statement, [court attendance if
applicable]. The fact that the case has reached this point owes a great deal to your willingness to engage. I want
you to know that has been recognised.
[Where appropriate: ] The court also made the following orders: [protective orders, restitution, etc.]. These come
into effect immediately. If they are breached you should contact me or call 999.
There's no obligation on you to do anything further. If you would find it helpful to talk through what happens
next or how the system works from here, I'm available. If there are any further matters - if you receive any contact
from the convicted person, for example - tell me immediately.
I'd also encourage you to take care of yourself in the coming days. Court outcomes can bring up feelings that
have been pushed aside during the wait. [Support service] is available if you'd like to speak with someone.
Is there anything I can answer for you now?

8.5.5 Template - Outcome (Case Not Proceeding to Prosecution)
[For notifying the IP that the case will not proceed - either no charge, or DPP no-prosecution direction, or
withdrawal. Tone: honest, respectful, explanatory, focused on the IP's options. Among the most difficult
communications to make well.]
No Prosecution Template - Sample Text
Hello [Mr/Ms Walsh], it's [Garda Name]. I need to talk to you about your case, and I want to do it properly, so
I'd like to ask if I can come to see you, or if you'd prefer to come into the station. [Or: 'I want to give you an
update and explain something difficult, and I'd rather do it on the phone rather than wait to meet, if that's alright.']
I have to tell you that the [DPP / Garda decision] has been not to proceed with the case at this point. I want to
explain what that means and what it doesn't mean.
What it means: the case is not going to court. [Brief reason, where shareable - e.g., 'The DPP took the view that
on the evidence currently available, the prosecution would not have a reasonable prospect of success.' Or: 'A

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

particular evidential difficulty was identified that means we cannot proceed.'] That decision was made after
careful consideration of the file.
What it doesn't mean: it doesn't mean that what you said wasn't believed. It doesn't mean that what happened to
you didn't matter. It doesn't mean the door is closed forever - if new information becomes available, the case can
be reviewed.
I know this is not the outcome you hoped for. I want to be honest with you that I understand that. I also want you
to know what your options are.
[Where relevant: ] You have the right to seek a review of the DPP's decision. The process for that is [explanation
/ written information provided]. There is no time pressure - you can take your time to consider.
[Where relevant: ] You also have the option of civil proceedings, which operate differently from criminal
proceedings. A solicitor can advise you on that, and I can give you contact details for [Legal Aid Board / Free
Legal Advice Centres] if helpful.
Support services remain available. [Name service]. They are there to support you whether or not the case is in
the criminal courts.
I'm going to leave you my direct contact details. If anything comes up - if you remember something, if you're
contacted by anyone connected with this, if you simply want to talk it through - please reach out to me. I'm sorry
I'm not bringing you the news you would have wanted today.

8.5.6 Template - Long-Term Unsolved Case (Periodic Review)
[For cases that remain open but unresolved over time - cold cases, long-term missing persons. The
communication maintains the relationship and confirms the case is still being held open.]
Long-Term Review Template - Sample Text
Hello [Mr/Ms Walsh], [Garda Name] from Mountjoy. I'm calling on what would be the [annual / six-monthly]
review of [the case / [Person Name]'s case].
I want you to know your case has not been forgotten. It remains open. It's been reviewed by [me / the cold case
team / specialist unit] in the past [period] and a decision has been taken that [current status / steps being taken /
awaiting development X].
If new information comes forward at any time, the case is reopened. Sometimes that information comes from
advances in forensic methods. Sometimes it comes from a witness who couldn't speak at the time. Sometimes it
comes from a member of the public coming forward.
If you have heard anything, remembered anything, or want to share anything since we last spoke, this is a good
time to do that. Even something small can matter.
If there's anything I can do for you personally - support service contact, information, just listening - I'm here.
The next review will be in [period]. I'll be in touch then unless something significant happens in the meantime.

8.6 Specific Operational Considerations
8.6.1 Where the IP is Deceased
Family Liaison Officer (FLO) typically allocated (Volume 8 Chapter 4).
Communications with the family follow the FLO framework - sustained, supportive, structured.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Death notification protocol applies for initial notification (Volume 8 Chapter 1.4).
Coroner's inquest engagement throughout.
Specific considerations where homicide is suspected - the family becomes both bereaved and prosecutionsupporting.

8.6.2 Where Multiple IPs are Involved
Each IP is communicated with as an individual - no group emails or generic notifications.
Where there are sensitivities between IPs (multiple complainants in one suspect, fellow witnesses with different
perspectives), communications respect each IP's confidentiality.
Consider designated investigator-IP contact pairing in larger cases.

8.6.3 Where the IP is Vulnerable
Communications adapted to the IP's capacity, language, and support needs.
Appropriate adult / support person involved where helpful.
Specialist Garda (DARI, DSCB, JLO) supports the communication.
Documentation of how communication was structured to meet the IP's needs.

8.6.4 Where the IP Withdraws Engagement
Honest acknowledgement of the IP's right to withdraw.
Explanation of effect on the case, where the IP wishes to know.
Continued availability - the door is not closed; the IP may return to the case at any time.
In DV / coercive control cases, particular sensitivity - withdrawal may itself be the product of the coercive
pattern; specialist consideration of the case's evidential capacity to proceed without the IP's continued
cooperation (Volume 4).

8.7 Recording the Communication
Every substantive communication with an injured party is recorded:
PULSE narrative entry - what was communicated, when, by whom, mode.
File annotation - significant communications referenced in the file as supplementary.
Notebook - for in-person and phone communications, the member's contemporaneous note.
FLO log where applicable (Volume 8 Ch 4).
Outgoing letters and emails preserved in the file.
The record protects both the IP (showing the engagement happened) and the member (showing the
communication discipline).

8.8 The Long View
The injured party's experience of the criminal justice system is shaped, often decisively, by the quality of
communication from the investigating Garda. A case that ends in conviction but leaves the IP feeling unheard
is remembered as a poor experience; a case that does not result in prosecution but is communicated to the IP
honestly, supportively and with respect is remembered as one where the system worked, even though the
outcome was hard. The communication discipline is not a separate task from the investigation; it is part of the
investigation, and it is among the most consequential parts.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 9 - Defence Preparation Doctrine
The defence is not the enemy. The defence is doing its constitutional job - testing the prosecution case, holding
the State to its proof, protecting against wrongful conviction. The properly-prepared Garda witness welcomes
the test, because the test is what gives a conviction its legitimacy. The opposite - a witness who treats crossexamination as combat - loses ground regardless of the truth of the case. This chapter sets out the doctrine of
preparing for the defence's challenge.

9.1 Thinking Like the Defence
The most effective preparation tool a Garda has is the deliberate, disciplined effort to read the case as the
defence will read it. The exercise is uncomfortable - the investigator who has built the case has investment in
its strengths; the defence has investment only in its weaknesses. But the prosecution case that has been pressuretested by the investigator already does not collapse when the defence applies the same pressure later.
The exercise has a structure:

9.2 The Three Categories of Defence Attack
9.2.1 Substantive Attack - The Facts
Challenging what happened. The complainant is wrong; the witness is mistaken; the identification is unreliable;
the suspect's account is the true account. The substantive attack is met by the strength of the evidence - the
witness accounts, the corroborating exhibits, the documentary record.

9.2.2 Procedural Attack - The Process
Challenging how the evidence was obtained. The arrest was not on reasonable suspicion. The search exceeded
the warrant. The interview was conducted without proper solicitor access. The caution was not properly
administered. The statutory form contained an error. The chain of custody was broken. The procedural attack
is met by procedural rigour at every step of the investigation - and by the contemporaneous record showing that
rigour.

9.2.3 Credibility Attack - The Witness
Challenging the reliability of the witness rather than the substance of what they say. Memory, perception, prior
inconsistent statement, motive, bias, prior conduct, comprehension. Met by accurate, well-prepared, composed
testimony, and by the contemporaneous record showing the consistency between what was recorded at the time
and what is now said.

9.3 Anticipation and Pre-Emption
Every cover report should anticipate the defence. The 'Anticipated Defence' section of the cover report (Chapter
3) is where this is captured. Anticipation is not paranoia - it is the realistic assessment of where a competent
defence solicitor or barrister will focus. The standard questions:
If you were the defence, where would you start?
If you had one cross-examination point, which would it be?
If you had to argue the case for the suspect, what would your three best arguments be?
What would the IP have to prove and what is the weakest link in that proof?
The answers form the agenda for additional investigation, additional statements, additional exhibit work. The
properly-prepared case has already addressed what the defence will raise.

9.4 Knowing Your Weak Points Before the Defence Does
Every case has weak points. The skill is not to deny them but to know them. A Garda who can describe their

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

own case's weak points has integrity that the court will recognise; a Garda who claims the case has no
weaknesses loses credibility in the moment of admitting the first one.
The honest answer to a hard cross-examination question is often a partial concession followed by a substantive
point:
'It's correct that the lighting was poor. The identification rests on the distinctive cap and the clothing as well,
and on the proximity in time and place.'
'It's correct that the chain of custody record has a gap of two hours. I am not suggesting that gap doesn't exist;
what I can say about it is...'
'I cannot recall that exchange. I do recall and recorded what I considered material.'
'That is one possibility. The basis on which I formed the contrary view at the time was...'

9.5 The Garda Witness in Court - Posture
Composed. Cross-examination is structured pressure; composure is the answer to it. Pause before answering;
speak deliberately; do not engage in argument with counsel.
Honest. The truth, including its limits. Where you don't remember, say so. Where you weren't there, say so.
Where you made a judgement call, say so.
Within the record. The notebook, statement, custody record, BWV is your foundation. You are not inventing
answers; you are speaking from the record.
Specific. 'I formed the view that there were reasonable grounds because...' - and then the grounds, articulated.
Not 'I just had a feeling.'
Independent. The defence will sometimes try to align you with the prosecution narrative beyond what the
evidence supports. Resist - your role is to give your evidence accurately, not to support a case.
Respectful. To the court, to counsel, to the accused. The witness whose conduct in court is professional is the
witness whose evidence is heard.

9.6 The Three Habits of the Well-Prepared Witness

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 10 - Case Law Application and Defence Attack
Vectors
This chapter takes the principal procedural authorities case by case, sets out how each is most commonly used
by the defence to attack the prosecution case, and identifies how the well-prepared investigator pre-empts the
attack. For each case: the principle in operational terms, the defence application, the preparation, the crossexamination answers.
Working alongside the volume case law
The vLex-format case law in Volumes 1 through 10 sets out citation, facts, holding, reasoning, significance and
operational application for each authority. This chapter does not repeat that material; it focuses on the operational
mechanics of how each case is used in defence cross-examination and how the investigator prepares. Read in
conjunction with the relevant volume's case law appendix.

10.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294 - Reasonable Suspicion for
Arrest
The Principle in Operational Terms
The lawfulness of an arrest depends on the arresting member personally holding reasonable suspicion at the
moment of arrest. Suspicion based on information from a colleague counts only if the arresting member is
aware of the substance of the basis - not merely that 'a colleague said to arrest.' The arrest is the arresting
member's act; the grounds are the arresting member's grounds.

How the Defence Will Attack
'You arrested my client on the direction of another Garda?'
'You did not see the alleged offence yourself?'
'You did not know what your colleague had seen?'
'You were just doing what you were told?'
The destination: an arrest that was not lawfully grounded; everything that flowed from the arrest (search,
custody, interview, exhibits) potentially excluded.

How You Prepare
At the moment of arrest, articulate your grounds in your own mind: 'I am arresting this person because [specific
grounds].'
Where the grounds come substantially from colleague observation: the colleague should communicate the
substance to you before you arrest - description, behaviour observed, basis. 'Garda X said to arrest him' is not
grounds; 'Garda X reported observing the offence and described the male as wearing [...] - I confirmed the
description on observing the male' is grounds.
Record the grounds in your notebook contemporaneously - what observation, what description, what time, what
location.
Make it part of your statement: 'My grounds for arresting [...] were [...].'

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'You hadn't seen anything yourself, had you?' - A: 'I had not personally witnessed the offence. I had received
from Garda X the description of the suspect and the substance of his observations [specify], and on attending
the scene I confirmed [specific match]. Those were my grounds.'
Q: 'You just did what Garda X told you?' - A: 'I exercised my own judgement on the basis of the information I
had. I was satisfied that there were reasonable grounds; the grounds are recorded in my notebook and statement.'
Q: 'You can't say what Garda X actually saw?' - A: 'I can say what Garda X communicated to me. That was
[...]. I am not in a position to give evidence of what Garda X saw directly; Garda X gives that evidence himself.'

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

10.2 The People (DPP) v JC [2015] IESC 31 - The Modern Exclusionary Rule
The Principle in Operational Terms
Evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights is liable to exclusion, subject to a narrowed exception
where the breach was inadvertent and the prosecution can demonstrate it. The 'inadvertent' standard is genuine
and not a saving formula - the test will be searching. The post-JC reality is procedural rigour at every step,
because any breach risks the evidence.

How the Defence Will Attack
Identifying any procedural irregularity - a delay in caution, a slip in the rights notification, a gap in the chain and pressing for exclusion.
'You didn't follow the standard procedure on [step], did you?'
'You knew the procedure required X - and you chose to do Y?'
'If we accept this was a breach of my client's rights, the court is being asked to exclude the evidence.'

How You Prepare
Procedure followed at every step - and recorded contemporaneously.
Where a slip occurred, acknowledge it in the cover report; explain it; let the prosecutor address it openly rather
than discover it under cross-examination.
If a step deviates from standard practice for a defensible operational reason (urgency, safety, evidence
preservation), record the reason at the time.
Read your statement and the custody record in conjunction; identify any procedural friction points in advance.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'You did not give the caution at the moment of arrest?' - A: 'I gave the caution at [specific time/circumstance];
the reason for the timing was [...]. The caution is recorded in my notebook and on BWV at [reference].'
Q: 'The standard procedure would be X - you didn't do that?' - A: 'I followed the procedure as I understood it
required to be followed. The specific step you mention was [done / not applicable / done differently because of
the following circumstances].'
Q: 'You can't say there was no breach, can you?' - A: 'I can say what I did and why. Whether that meets the
standard the court requires is a matter for the court.'

10.3 Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 - Warrant Independence
The Principle in Operational Terms
A search warrant must be issued by an independent person - not by a member of the investigating force. The
warrant grounded by an investigator and issued by the investigator's own command lacks the independent
judicial scrutiny that justifies the intrusion on the dwelling. Post-Damache, search warrants in serious cases
come from District Court judges or other genuinely independent issuers.

How the Defence Will Attack
Examining the warrant - was it issued by a competent independent issuer?
Examining the sworn information - did it disclose the grounds adequately?
Examining the execution - was the warrant complied with as issued?
Probing whether the warrant rested on protected intelligence that should have been more openly disclosed.

How You Prepare
Sworn information drafted carefully - the grounds articulated with sufficient detail to support the issuance; the
source of the grounds identified to the issuer (intelligence handled through the sterile corridor approach,
Volume 10).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Warrant served and executed within the scope authorised.
Production of the warrant on entry; statement of authority; identification of members; copy provided where
required.
All of the above documented in the warrant package, the entry log, the BWV, and the executing member's
statement.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'The warrant was based on intelligence you didn't share with the judge?' - A: 'The sworn information
disclosed the substance of the grounds to the issuing District Judge as the law requires. The basis of the
application is the sworn information; I am not in a position in this courtroom to go beyond what was in the
sworn information on the sources.'
Q: 'You searched beyond the scope of the warrant?' - A: 'The warrant authorised the search of [...]. The items
recovered and seized were found within that scope, namely [...].'

10.4 DPP v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17 and DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC
1 - Solicitor Access
The Principle in Operational Terms
Gormley establishes the right to pre-interview consultation with a solicitor; Doyle establishes the right to a
solicitor's presence in interview where requested. The interview that proceeds without honouring those rights
is at risk of exclusion. The interviewer's discipline is to facilitate the rights actively, not merely to formally
offer them.

How the Defence Will Attack
'My client asked for a solicitor at [time] - the interview started at [time]. There was a gap. Why?'
'My client wanted the solicitor present in interview - the solicitor was not in the room.'
'You commenced the interview before the consultation was complete.'
'You did not allow the solicitor to confer with my client during the interview when requested.'

How You Prepare
Custody record entries showing: solicitor requested at [time]; solicitor contacted at [time]; consultation
commenced at [time]; consultation concluded at [time]; interview commenced at [time].
Where a solicitor was requested to be present in interview: the solicitor's presence (or genuine inability to
attend, with the suspect's informed waiver) documented.
Where the suspect waives any right: the waiver explicit, after explanation, on the recording, in the suspect's
own words.
The MIC's role in protecting the rights - independent of the investigator - documented.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'There was a 90-minute gap between my client requesting a solicitor and the interview - why?' - A: 'The
custody record reflects the contact with the solicitor, the time of attendance, and the duration of the consultation.
The interview commenced after the consultation concluded and the suspect indicated readiness.'
Q: 'The solicitor was not in the room during the interview?' - A: 'The suspect was offered solicitor presence in
interview. He indicated [acceptance / a waiver], which is on the audio recording and in the custody record.'

10.5 The People (DPP) v Madden [1977] IR 336 - Voluntariness
The Principle in Operational Terms
Statements / admissions are admissible only if voluntary - not obtained by threat, inducement, or oppression.
The test is realistic - tiredness, stress and the inherent pressure of custody do not in themselves render a
statement involuntary, but where conduct or conditions tip into oppression, the statement falls.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How the Defence Will Attack
Conditions: 'My client was in custody for X hours; he had not slept; he had not eaten; he was distressed.'
Conduct: 'You raised your voice; you said the only way out was to admit; you suggested matters would go
better if he cooperated.'
Vulnerability: 'My client was intoxicated; had a mental health condition; was in pain.'
Inducement: 'You suggested bail would be more likely if he confessed.'

How You Prepare
Custody record: time in, rest periods, meals, medical attention, welfare checks - all recorded.
Interview audio - the tone, the language, the pace. Standard caution; standard questions; no leading; no threats;
no inducements; no shouting.
Fitness for interview - medical assessment where indicated; not interviewing where condition unfit.
Breaks during interview - documented; not pressed through.
Solicitor presence/availability protective of the suspect, also protective of the prosecution case.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'My client was in custody for 18 hours - he was exhausted.' - A: 'The custody record reflects rest periods,
meals and welfare checks. The interview was conducted at [time]. The audio reflects the conduct of the
interview.'
Q: 'You suggested that admitting would mean bail.' - A: 'I did not suggest that. The audio is the record of the
interview. No such suggestion is on it.'
Q: 'You raised your voice.' - A: 'The audio is the record. I conducted the interview in accordance with standard
procedure.'

10.6 The People (DPP) v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 - Inferences from Silence
The Principle in Operational Terms
Statutory inferences under sections 18, 19 and 19A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 are available only where
strict prerequisites are met - the relevant warning given, in clear terms, recorded; the suspect afforded
reasonable opportunity to consult; the failure to mention something the suspect could reasonably have been
expected to mention. The inferences are a powerful tool when properly used and a fatal flaw when improperly
used.

How the Defence Will Attack
'The warning was not given in clear terms.'
'My client did not understand what was being warned.'
'My client was not given reasonable opportunity to consult.'
'The failure relates to something my client could not reasonably have been expected to know to mention.'
'You did not properly comply with the prerequisites - no inference can be drawn.'

How You Prepare
Inference warning script ready and used in the interview - read clearly, recorded, the suspect's understanding
confirmed.
Solicitor consultation prior - documented.
Specific failure to mention identified to the suspect - 'I am asking you to account for [...]'
Audio recording captures the warning and the response or silence.
Cover report identifies which inference is being relied on and the basis.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'The warning wasn't clear.' - A: 'I read the warning in standard form. The audio is the record. The suspect

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

was asked to confirm understanding.'
Q: 'My client didn't know what he was being asked to account for.' - A: 'I specified the matter on which an
account was sought. The audio reflects the specific question.'

10.7 DPP v Avadenei [2017] IESC 77 - Statutory Form Strict Compliance
The Principle in Operational Terms
The statutory form completed in the Road Traffic Act 2010 testing procedure - and by extension other statutory
testing procedures - must be completed strictly in accordance with the statute. Errors in the form, omissions of
required content, deviations from the prescribed procedure are not curable by substantial compliance. The case
stands on the statutory form being right.

How the Defence Will Attack
'The form omitted [required entry].'
'The form has an error in [field].'
'The procedure required X to be communicated; it was not.'
'The form was not completed contemporaneously.'
'The bilingual / language requirement was not met.'

How You Prepare
Every field completed accurately at the time.
The procedure followed as the statute requires - the required information communicated, the required signatures
obtained, the prescribed sequence followed.
Forms read carefully; deviations from form impossible if the form is the operational artefact.
Where a slip occurred, recognise it; do not attempt to disguise or 'correct' afterwards in a way that creates a
fabrication concern.
Cover report addresses any procedural friction point openly.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'You omitted [field] on the form.' - A: 'The form contains [response - either the field is completed and you
can identify it; or, if it was omitted, an honest acknowledgement followed by the operational explanation].'
Q: 'The procedure required reading the document to the suspect in his language.' - A: 'The procedure was
followed; the audio / contemporaneous record confirms.'

10.8 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250 - Sentencing Structure
The Principle in Operational Terms
Sentencing is structured discretion - the court weighs aggravating and mitigating factors against the gravity of
the offence and the personal circumstances of the offender. The investigator's role at sentence is to provide the
factual basis - accurate antecedents, accurate description of the offending conduct, accurate articulation of
impact on the IP.

How the Defence Will Engage
Mitigation - the personal circumstances of the offender, the prospects of rehabilitation, the offender's expression
of remorse.
Challenging aggravating factors put forward by the prosecution.
Plea credit and timing of plea.

How You Prepare
Antecedent statement accurate and up to date.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Victim Impact Statement obtained where applicable (Vol 4 / Vol 6 / Vol 8).
Aggravating factors articulated in cover report and supported by evidence.
Investigator available to give evidence on the offence circumstances at sentence.

In Court at Sentencing
Factual; not advocacy.
Confirm the description of the offence as captured in the file.
Confirm antecedents accurately.
Be prepared for questions on the circumstances, the impact, the recovery of property, the engagement with the
IP.

10.9 People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 - Identification Warning
The Principle in Operational Terms
Identification evidence carries inherent risks of error; the court warns juries about the special danger. The
investigator's role is to capture identification properly - by ADVOKATE-aligned recording at the time, by
VIPER where appropriate, by photographs in defined conditions, by avoiding contamination from other
identification material.

How the Defence Will Attack
'The lighting was poor.'
'The witness saw the suspect only briefly.'
'The witness was distressed at the time.'
'The witness's description has changed.'
'The identification procedure was suggestive.'

How You Prepare
Initial description from the witness recorded at the earliest opportunity, in the witness's own words.
ADVOKATE factors documented - Amount of time observing, Distance, Visibility, Obstruction, Known/seen
before, Any reason to remember, Time elapsed since, Errors or material discrepancies.
VIPER (video identification) where appropriate; conducted by independent officer; documented.
Avoid contamination - the witness should not see the suspect (or images of the suspect) before any formal
identification procedure other than where the immediate identification (e.g. at scene, by direct comparison) is
the procedure.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'It was dark and the witness only saw him briefly.' - A: 'The witness's account records [specific lighting
conditions and duration]. The identification process is documented in the statements at [reference].'
Q: 'The witness's description has changed.' - A: 'The initial description was [...]; any subsequent description is
recorded in the relevant statements; the consistency or otherwise is a matter for the court.'

10.10 Chain of Custody and PEMS Continuity
The Principle in Operational Terms
Every exhibit must be traceable from seizure through to court - who took it, when, where it was stored, who
received it on transfer, what was done with it. A break in the chain raises a question about whether the exhibit
in court is the exhibit seized.

How the Defence Will Attack
'There is no record of who moved the exhibit between X time and Y time.'

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

'The exhibit was unaccounted for over the weekend.'
'The seal on the bag was broken before examination - was this exhibit tampered with?'
'No member can give evidence of having handled the exhibit at [specific stage].'

How You Prepare
PEMS entry at seizure - immediate, in the seizing member's presence.
Tamper-evident sealing of relevant exhibits; signed by the sealing member.
Every transfer recorded - PEMS log, member signatures.
Storage location recorded.
Examination - sample to FSI / MBRS / GCCB - transit recorded, receiving member identified.

Cross-Examination Answers
Q: 'You cannot account for the exhibit between [time] and [time].' - A: 'The exhibit was stored at [location]
under [security arrangement]. The next handling was [...]. The PEMS record reflects the chain.'
Q: 'The seal was broken on receipt at FSI.' - A: 'The seal was broken by the FSI examiner on receipt for analysis
- that is the protocol. The seal was intact on transit; the FSI record reflects receipt with intact seal.'

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 11 - Cross-Examination Roadmaps by Offence
Category
This chapter gives, for each principal offence category, the predictable lines of cross-examination the Garda
witness should expect, and the prepared answer for each. The roadmaps are not scripts - every case is different
and every defence is different. They are the agenda the well-prepared witness brings into court, knowing where
the questions are likely to go and being ready to give the considered answer rather than the surprised one.
Note on use
These roadmaps are operational anticipation, not legal advice. The well-prepared witness uses them to pre-empt
- asking themselves before court 'how would I answer this?' - rather than to memorise answers. Memorised
answers sound memorised. The honest, prepared answer in the witness's own words is what carries weight. All
scenarios are illustrative; all names are fictitious.

11.1 Theft and Property Offences
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Identification: 'The identification rested on [CCTV / direct observation / recovery in possession of the items /
the admission]. Specifically [...]. The basis is recorded in my statement at [paragraph].'
Recovery: 'The items were recovered from [where] at [time]. They [were / were not] still tagged / packaged
consistent with the unpaid state. The location of recovery is [proximity to the premises].'
Intent: 'My evidence is what I observed and what was found. The conduct observed was [...] - leaving without
payment, concealment, location of recovery. The admission at interview was [...]. The court will assess what
those facts demonstrate.'
Arrest grounds: 'My grounds were [specific - CCTV match, direct observation, identification, recovery]. Those
grounds are recorded contemporaneously.'
Search: 'I conducted a search incident to arrest under [s.6 CJA 1984 or other power]. The scope was [...]. The
items were recovered from [...].'

11.2 Assault and NFOAP Offences
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Identification: 'The identification was [direct, by description matched on stop, by witness identification, by
suspect's own admission of presence]. The grounds are [...].'
IP reliability: 'The IP's account is in her statement. I observed her at the scene. Her account is consistent with
the witnesses, with BWV [where applicable], and with the medical evidence.' (Or, honestly: 'The IP had been
drinking. The independent witnesses corroborate the account on [specific points].')
Inconsistencies: 'Where there are differences in detail between accounts, those differences are matters for the
court to consider. The core elements are corroborated.'
Self-defence: 'On the evidence I observed and gathered, the suspect approached the IP, not the other way
around. The IP was the one struck, not the suspect. There was no use of force by the IP that would warrant selfdefence.'
Injury attribution: 'The medical statement [Statement X] addresses mechanism and timing. The injuries are
consistent with the assault as described.'

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Mens rea: 'My evidence is the conduct - striking the IP twice with a closed fist to the face. The court draws its
own conclusion as to intent.'

11.3 Public Order Offences
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Public place: 'The location was [O'Connell Street / etc.], plainly a public place within the definition.'
Conduct: 'The conduct was [specific - words used, gestures, volume, target]. It is recorded on BWV. Members
of the public were [moving to avoid / visibly concerned / etc.].'
s.8 warning: 'I gave the direction in clear terms at [time]. I repeated it. The suspect's response was [...]. The
arrest followed [...]. BWV captures it.'
Proportionality of arrest: 'I assessed the conduct against the powers available. A direction was given first. The
arrest followed the failure to comply.'
Intoxication: 'The suspect was [observed to be intoxicated to the extent of [...] / responsive to direction / etc.].
The custody record reflects [...] processing.'

11.4 Domestic Violence and Coercive Control
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Reliability: 'The complainant's account is corroborated by [specific corroborating material - third-party
witnesses, contemporaneous communications, medical record, the IP's own diary]. Where there have been prior
interactions with the Gardaí, they are documented and are matters for the court to consider in context.'
Cumulative pattern: 'The case is built on the pattern - twenty-eight specific incidents over twenty-one months.
Each documented. The pattern is the offence under section 39, not any single incident in isolation.'
Diary: 'The diary is the IP's contemporaneous record, exhibited at [...]. The entries are dated. The IP confirms
in her statement when and how she kept it.'
Witnesses: 'Each witness gives their own evidence of what they directly observed. The aggregation of
independent observation is the corroboration.'
Communications: 'The messages exhibited are the suspect's own words. The IP's responses are in context and
the IP addresses them in her statement.'
Neutrality: 'I conducted the investigation in accordance with the Domestic Violence framework. I followed the
evidence as it was. Where there are weaknesses, the cover report identifies them.'

11.5 Drugs Offences - Possession, Supply, s.15A
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Intelligence: 'The warrant was issued by the District Judge on the basis of the sworn information. I am not able
to go beyond the sworn information on the operational sources.'
Warrant: 'The warrant is at Exhibit [...]. It was issued by [District Judge] at [court] on [date]. It authorised the
search of [...].'
Execution: 'On arrival I produced the warrant, identified myself, gave a copy to [...]. The search was conducted

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

by the team under my supervision. BWV captures the execution.'
Chain: 'PEMS records the chain from seizure to FSI examination to court. The continuity is documented at
every link.'
Supply inference: 'The case rests on the cumulative picture - quantity in 28 individual pre-prepared deal bags,
additional 145 unused bags, scale, EUR 4,750 cash, phone communications consistent with dealing. The
individual items might be explicable; the combination is not consistent with personal use.'
Personal use: 'The quantity is 312 grams of cocaine in 28 individually packaged deal bags. The street value is
approximately EUR 24,800. Personal use does not present in pre-packaged deal bags, scales and cash.'
FSI: 'The FSI examination is by [analyst] at Statement [...]. The certificate confirms identification and purity.'
Phone: 'The phone was seized in the apartment, powered on, in the suspect's possession effectively. The GCCB
extraction at Statement [...] details the communications and the basis of attribution.'

11.6 Sexual Offences
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Consent: 'The complainant's account on consent is at Statement [...]. Surrounding evidence - the conduct of the
parties, any communications, the immediate aftermath, the disclosure - bears on the consent issue. The court
determines the question.'
Reasonable belief: 'The reasonable-belief test is for the court. My role was to record the suspect's account at
interview and to investigate the surrounding evidence.'
Delays in disclosure: 'Delayed disclosure in sexual offences is well-recognised. The early disclosure here was
[to whom, when]. The first formal complaint was at [time]. The reasons given by the complainant are in her
statement.'
Forensic: 'The forensic evidence is the SATU report and FSI analysis at Statement [...]. The findings are [...].
The interpretation is within the medical and scientific evidence.'
Investigation neutrality: 'I conducted the investigation in accordance with the sexual offences framework. The
suspect was offered interview, with solicitor consultation. His account is recorded. The evidence followed
where it led.'

11.7 Road Traffic - Drink Driving (s.4 RTA 2010)
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
Reasonable cause: 'The basis for the stop was [routine MAT checkpoint authorised at [time, level] / specific
observation of [driving behaviour, smell of alcohol, demeanour]]. The basis is recorded in my notebook.'
Preliminary test: 'The preliminary test was administered under section 10 [or as applicable]. The procedure
followed [...]. The result was [...].'
Arrest: 'I arrested under [s.39 RTA 2002 / s.14 / etc.] at [time]. My grounds were [...].'
Statutory form: 'The form is at Exhibit [...]. It was completed contemporaneously, signed by [...] at [...]. Every
required field is completed.'
Calibration: 'The device was within calibration at the time of use. The calibration certificate is at Exhibit [...].
The approval is [...].'
Chain: 'The blood specimen was taken by [designated doctor] at [time]. The kit was sealed in the suspect's
presence. The transit to MBRS is at [...]. The MBRS receipt and analysis is at [...]. The certificate is at Exhibit
[...].'
Within-3-hours: 'The specimen was taken at [time], within three hours of [time of driving]. The statutory

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

presumption applies. Any medical or consumption defence raised would be a matter for the court.'

11.8 Fatal Collision (s.53 RTA 1961)
Predictable Cross-Examination Lines
Prepared Answer Framework
First response: 'On arrival I prioritised safety, casualties and scene preservation. Any movement of vehicles or
items was for life-saving purposes only and is documented.'
FCI: 'The FCI is the expert. The reconstruction is in the FCI report at [Statement X]. My role was to preserve
the scene to support the FCI's work.'
Causation: 'My evidence is on the scene and the investigation steps. The causation analysis is in the FCI report
and the pathology.'
Impairment testing: 'The surviving driver was tested under section 10 RTA 2010. The procedure followed [...].
The result was [...].'

11.9 When the Defence Names a Case
Defence counsel may name a specific authority in cross-examination - 'Walsh,' 'JC,' 'Avadenei,' 'Damache.' The
well-prepared witness recognises the case being referenced and answers from the operational application rather
than attempting to discuss the law:
Q: 'Are you familiar with Walsh's case?' - A: 'I am familiar with the requirement for the arresting member's
reasonable suspicion. In this case, my grounds were [...].'
Q: 'You've heard of Avadenei, no doubt.' - A: 'I am familiar with the strict compliance requirement for the
statutory form. In this case, the form was completed [...].'
Q: 'Are you saying you complied with Gormley?' - A: 'I am saying the suspect was afforded consultation with
a solicitor before interview as the custody record confirms.'
The witness need not discuss the case law as law - that is for counsel and the court. The witness's role is to give
evidence on what was done and why; the case law application is for the court to determine.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 12 - Witness Box Survival
The witness box is a structured pressure environment. Counsel for the defence has the floor, controls the topic,
controls the pace, and is professionally trained in adversarial examination. The Garda witness has the truth, the
contemporaneous record, and a duty to give their evidence accurately. The skills that survive cross-examination
are not combative skills; they are the skills of composure, honesty, precision, and discipline.

12.1 The Realities of the Witness Box
You are giving evidence in conditions designed to test it - and tests find weaknesses. Expect difficulty; do not
be surprised by it.
Counsel is not your enemy and not your friend. Counsel is doing a job. Treat counsel professionally regardless
of tone.
The court is the audience that matters. Your evidence is given to the judge and (in jury trials) the jury, not to
counsel. Direct your answers to them.
Time slows down. A pause feels longer than it is. Use the pause.
Memory is imperfect. Honesty about its limits is strength, not weakness.

12.2 The Six Disciplines
12.2.1 Pause Before Answering
Take a beat. Half a second to a full second. The pause gives you time to hear the question, formulate the answer,
and resist the tempo counsel is setting. The witness who answers instantly is often answering the question they
expected rather than the question they were asked.

12.2.2 Answer the Question Asked
Answer the specific question. If counsel asks 'what time was it', the answer is the time, not the surrounding
narrative. If counsel asks 'did you do X', the answer is yes / no / I did but with [...] / I did not. Do not volunteer
additional material; do not start a different topic; do not give a speech.

12.2.3 'I Don't Remember' Is Honest
If you do not remember, say so. The most damaging thing in cross-examination is invented detail to fill the gap.
The defence will probe and find the invention; the credibility goes.
'I do not specifically recall.'
'I cannot say with certainty.'
'My contemporaneous record reflects [...]; beyond what is recorded I do not have independent recollection.'
These are professional answers. They protect you and the case.

12.2.4 Stick to the Record
The notebook, the statement, the custody record, the BWV are your foundation. Where there is a discrepancy
between memory and record, the record is your reference point. Re-read the record before court; in court, you
can ask to refresh memory from your contemporaneous notebook under the relevant rules.

12.2.5 Concede the Concedable
Where the defence makes a point that is factually correct, concede it. A small honest concession early in crossexamination establishes credibility for the larger contested points later. The witness who contests everything
loses on the small contests and weakens on the large ones.
'That is correct. Within the context of [...], my view at the time was [...].'
'It is the case that [...]. The reason I formed the contrary view was [...].'

12.2.6 Composure Throughout

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Speak at a measured pace. Hold your posture. Look at the questioner, then turn to give the answer to the court.
Do not raise your voice. Do not show frustration. Do not match aggression. The composed witness is the
credible witness.

12.3 Specific Cross-Examination Traps
12.3.1 The Compound Question
Counsel: 'Isn't it the case that you arrived at 2:25, didn't see anything yourself, and just arrested whoever your
colleague pointed at?' Each part of that question is a separate proposition; some you accept, some you don't.
Answer: 'There are several points in your question. I arrived at 2:24. I had not personally observed the offence.
I did not arrest at my colleague's direction; I arrested on grounds I held myself, which were [...].'

12.3.2 The Assumption Embedded
Counsel: 'When you decided to ignore the standard procedure...' - the question assumes a fact that has not been
established. Answer: 'I did not ignore the standard procedure. I followed it as it required to be followed.' Then
if asked specifics, deal with specifics.

12.3.3 The Long Question
Counsel: '[Lengthy hypothetical or narrative ending in] - isn't that right?' Long questions invite the witness to
agree to the whole proposition. Pause; identify the actual question; address it. 'Could you break that into separate
questions?' is a fair response.

12.3.4 The Repeated Question
Counsel asks essentially the same question multiple times, hoping for a different answer or to wear the witness
down. The answer is the same answer. 'As I have said, [...].' Without irritation.

12.3.5 The Silence After Your Answer
Counsel may pause after your answer, hoping you will fill the silence with additional material. You do not need
to. Your answer was sufficient. Wait for the next question.

12.3.6 The Personal Question
Counsel may ask about your training, experience, prior cases. Answer factually and briefly. 'I have been a
member for X years. I have investigated cases of this nature on previous occasions. My training included [...].
I do not consider [whatever proposition is being put] inconsistent with my training.'

12.4 Re-Examination
After cross-examination, the prosecution can re-examine to address points raised. The well-prepared witness
has not introduced material that the defence has been able to use; the prosecution's re-examination then is
targeted to clarify rather than to repair. If you have made a slip, do not panic - the re-examination can address
it, and the court understands that witnesses are not perfect.

12.5 After Court
Debrief - with the prosecutor, with your supervisor.
Learn - identify what went well and what was difficult.
Decompress - court is intense; the body and mind need to wind down after.
Record - any operational points emerging from court for future cases.

12.6 The Long View
Garda witnesses are tested in court over years and decades. The reputation built in one trial sustains across the

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

next. The witness who is known by the local bar, the local prosecutors and the local judges to give evidence
accurately and professionally has credibility that is worth more than any specific argument made in any specific
case. That reputation is built one statement, one cross-examination, one composed answer at a time.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

About the Companion Reference
This section appends four companion case law references compiled separately from the main series. The
references operate alongside the volumes - they cover the same operational territory in different formats and at
different depths, providing alternative ways into the material.

The Four References
1. Garda Case Law Quick Reference
Quick-reference table format covering 12 operational areas: Arrest and Detention; Search, Entry and Warrants;
Evidence, Confessions and Admissibility; Use of Force; Road Traffic; Public Order and Assault; Theft, Fraud
and Property; Domestic Violence and Vulnerable Persons; Drugs; Interviewing and Suspect's Rights; Court,
Disclosure and Evidence Preservation; Constitutional and Human Rights. Use for fast lookup when in the
moment - case name, principle, practical application in three columns.

2. Garda Operational Case Law Reference (Detective Grade)
Expanded reference covering 21 sections - the 12 from Quick Reference plus: Organised and Serious Crime;
Homicide and Serious Violence; Sexual Offences; Bail and Remand; Firearms and Offensive Weapons;
Statutory Anchor Cheat Sheet; Inner-City Dublin Operational Playbook; Detective Toolkit and Investigative
Doctrine; Operational Mnemonics and Scripts. Use for deeper engagement and for the scenario-based
playbooks (assault, burglary, domestic incident, gangland, mental health crisis, sudden death - each with first
actions, charges, case law, file craft).

3. Garda Case Law Ultimate Operational Reference
Comprehensive reference with deep treatment of each case: FACTS / PRINCIPLE / RATIO / TEST / HOW IT
UNFOLDS IN COURT / SPECIMEN TESTIMONY / PRACTICAL APPLICATION / WITNESS
HANDLING / COMMON PITFALLS / CROSS-REFERENCES. Organised into six internal volumes: Powers;
Evidence and Interview; Offences; Constitutional and Procedure; Witness Handling; Common Mistakes. Use
for trial preparation, supervisor briefing, file building, cross-examination preparation, and spotting problems
before they become defects.

4. Garda Case Law Deep Operational Reference (Probationer to Senior Investigator)
Deep case-law reference pitched across the full career span - from probationer to senior investigator. Each case
is presented as FACTS / PRINCIPLE / RATIO / TEST, with a parallel column translating the principle into
operational practice at scene, in custody, in interview, and in court. It follows the operational chronology (arrest,
search and entry, detention regimes, evidence and admissibility, use of force, then offence categories, then
cross-cutting topics) and adds: nine Operational Workflows (arrest; drugs search execution; DV callout; CCTV
capture; inference warning sequence; scene preservation; building a s.15 case; stop and search articulation;
PEACE interview strategy); a Statutory Anchors index; standard caution and warning wordings; and
operational mantras. Use for senior-investigator-level depth, detention clock management, the inference
warning sequence, and DPP directions preparation.

How the Companion Reference Relates to Volumes 1-9
Volumes 1-9 are organised by offence category and incident type (theft, assault, public order, DV, drugs, sexual
offences, road traffic, special incidents, cross-cutting). Each volume's Case Law Appendix treats the principal
authorities for that subject area in vLex format (Citation, Facts, Holding, Reasoning, Significance, Operational
Application). The Companion Reference is organised differently - by operational legal area (arrest, search,
evidence, use of force) and by case-as-unit. Both perspectives operate alongside each other:
To find case law for a specific offence type: go to the Volume Case Law Appendix.
To find case law for a specific operational legal area: go to the Companion Reference.
For deep case-by-case treatment for trial preparation: go to the Ultimate Reference.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

For scenario-based playbook for a specific situation type: go to the Detective Reference inner-city playbook.
For fast lookup of a single case principle: go to the Quick Reference.

Verification Reminder
The Companion Reference, like Volumes 1-9, is a working reference. Case law and statutory frameworks
continue to develop. Before relying on any statement for an operational decision, verify on
revisedacts.lawreform.ie, irishstatutebook.ie, vLex Ireland, BAILII, HUDOC (ECtHR), or curia.europa.eu
(CJEU) as appropriate. Some citations in the Companion Reference are working references requiring exact
neutral citation verification on Bailii/courts.ie. The JC (2015) framework restated the exclusionary rule - check
whether older exclusion authorities have been refined before relying.

1. Arrest & Detention
The most operationally consequential area of Irish criminal law. Every arrest is reviewed against these authorities. Master t he
s.4 CJA 1984 clock and the Gormley/Doyle line on solicitor access.
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

DPP v Walsh [1980] IR 294

Common law/statutory arrest valid only where the
arresting Garda personally holds reasonable
suspicion at the moment of arrest. Suspicion cannot
be inherited.

You must form your own grounds. If another
Garda asks you to arrest 'their' suspect, get the
grounds yourself before laying hands on. 'I was
told to arrest him' = unlawful.

Christie v Leachinsky [1947]
AC 573 (HL); adopted in
Irish law

Person arrested must be told (a) that they are under
arrest and (b) the reason, in language understood,
unless obvious or person makes it impossible.

State: 'I am arresting you for [offence] contrary
to [section + Act]. You are not obliged to say
anything…' All three elements: arrest + reason
+ caution.

DPP v Shaw [1982] IR 1

Detention purely for the purpose of questioning,
without statutory authority, is unlawful
imprisonment. Confessions obtained may be
excluded.

Never 'invite' someone to the station and hold
them without arrest. Either they're free to leave
or they're under arrest — there is no middle
ground.

The People (DPP) v
Quilligan (No.1) [1986] IR
495

S.30 OASA arrest only lawful where Garda has
bona fide suspicion of scheduled offence. Cannot
be used as catch-all detention.

S.30 must relate to an actual scheduled offence
(firearms, explosives, OASA offences, etc.).
Don't use s.30 because s.4 'won't be long
enough'.

The People (DPP) v
Quilligan (No.3) [1993] 2 IR
305

Once lawfully arrested under s.30, questioning
may extend to ordinary offences arising from same
facts.

Where arrest is genuinely for scheduled offence,
investigation
can
lawfully
encompass
connected non-scheduled offences.

DPP v O'Brien [2005] IESC
29

Re-arrest for same offence requires fresh evidence
or material change of circumstances.

Cannot release and immediately re-arrest to
reset s.4 clock. Document the new material
justifying re-arrest.

DPP v Finnegan [2019]
IECA 250

Where statutory time limits exceeded, detention
becomes unlawful from that point — but earlier
lawful detention not retrospectively tainted.

Watch the clock obsessively. Once the clock
expires, charge, release, or apply for extension
immediately.

DPP v Boylan [1991] 1 IR
477

Time spent on medical attention, solicitor
consultation, sleep, or arrival/departure may be
excluded from detention period if properly
recorded.

Custody record entries must be timestamped to
the minute. Note start/end of every interruption.
Don't approximate.

DPP v Gormley & White
[2014] IESC 17

Constitutional right to consult solicitor BEFORE
questioning. Questioning before reasonable
solicitor access = breach.

Do not commence interview until solicitor has
been consulted, or waiver is in writing and
recorded. Even if suspect 'wants to talk', wait.

DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1

Right to have solicitor PRESENT during interview
(extension of Gormley).

Facilitate solicitor presence. Brief them on
offence + key disclosures. Record any
refusal/waiver.

DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31

Unconstitutionally obtained evidence MAY be
admitted if breach was inadvertent and not
deliberate/conscious. Burden on prosecution to
show inadvertence.

JC is not a licence to cut corners. Deliberate or
reckless breach = exclusion. Document your
reasoning to show good faith.

DPP v Cash [2010] IESC 1

Technical irregularities in arrest do not necessarily
invalidate it where Garda acted in good faith and
within powers.

Substance over form — but document your
bona fides contemporaneously, not in
retrospect.

DPP v Madden [1977] IR
336

Detention is for investigation of the offence for
which arrested. Cannot detain to investigate other
matters without fresh arrest.

If new offence emerges during detention,
formally arrest for the new offence and update
custody record. Don't 'piggyback'.

DPP v Pringle (1981) 2
Frewen 57

Judges' Rules require caution before any
incriminating questioning. Failure may render
answers inadmissible.

Caution before questioning. Re-caution after
every break and at the start of every new
interview session.

DPP v Coddington [2001] 3
IR 62

Where detention extended under s.4 CJA 1984, the
extension itself must be justified and recorded by
member-in-charge.

Extension authorisation must be in writing,
signed,
timestamped,
and
reasoned.
'Investigation ongoing' alone is insufficient —

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application
show what's been done and what's left.

DPP v Birney [2007] 1 IR
337

Arrest within a dwelling without warrant requires
specific legal authority (e.g. s.6 CJA 1997, s.10
CJA 1984, hot pursuit).

Cannot follow someone into a house to arrest
absent specific statutory power. Knock,
identify, articulate the power.

The State (Trimbole) v
Governor
of
Mountjoy
Prison [1985] IR 550

Use of one power as a pretext to achieve another
unlawful purpose vitiates the arrest.

Don't use a public order arrest as cover to search
for drugs. Bad faith is fatal.

DPP v Tyndall [2005] 1 IR
593

Member-in-charge has independent duty to
safeguard detainee's welfare and rights.

If you are MIC, you are NOT part of the
investigation team. Your duty is to the detainee.
Push back if questioning is excessive.

People (DPP) v
[2021] IECA 211

Limen

Translation/interpreter services must be provided
where suspect's English is insufficient for fair
process.

Mountjoy/inner-city: high volume of nonnational suspects. Engage qualified interpreter
early. Avoid using family/friends.

DPP v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268

Breach of Custody Regulations 1987 does not
automatically exclude statements; trial judge has
discretion based on fairness.

Don't relax — regulation breaches damage
credibility even if statement admitted. Treat
regs as mandatory.

Salduz v Turkey (2008) 49
EHRR 19

ECHR Art 6 — restriction on solicitor access at
investigative stage breaches fair trial right where
prejudicial.

Underpins Gormley. Any restriction on solicitor
access must be exceptional and justified.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

2. Search, Entry & Warrants
Search powers are tightly circumscribed. Dwelling protection under Article 40.5 is constitutional. Damache transformed s.29
OASA warrants. Inner-city raids must withstand JC scrutiny.
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

DPP v Dunne [1994] 2 IR
537

Search warrant must specify the offence with
sufficient particularity and identify the premises
clearly. Defective warrant invalidates search.

Before executing: check address spelling,
offence cited, statutory provision, signature,
date, issuer. One typo can sink the case.

DPP v Laide & Ryan [2005]
1 IR 209

Sworn information grounding warrant must show
actual reasonable grounds, not bare recitation.

Your information must include: source of info
(reliable informant, observation, CCTV),
corroboration, timeline, why this address/time.
Show your working.

DPP v Damache [2012]
IESC 11

S.29 OASA warrant issued by member of the
investigating force breaches separation of powers
— unconstitutional.

S.29 warrants now require District Judge. Never
accept an internal warrant for serious
investigation.

DPP v Cunningham [2012]
IECCA 64

Damache applied — evidence obtained under
defective s.29 warrant excluded.

Audit every warrant before execution. Question
whether it complies with post-Damache regime.

The People (DPP) v Kenny
[1990] 2 IR 110

Deliberate and conscious breach of constitutional
rights re: dwelling = exclusion of evidence
(modified by JC).

Article 40.5 protection is at its highest for
dwellings. Any uncertainty — get the warrant.

DPP v Birney [2007] 1 IR
337

Entry to dwelling requires (i) warrant; (ii) genuine
consent; (iii) hot pursuit of arrestable offence; or
(iv) specific statutory power.

Catalogue your power before crossing the
threshold. 'Implied invitation' to knock is NOT
consent to enter.

DPP v Mallon [2003] 2
ILRM 374

Consent to enter must be informed and freely
given; cannot be obtained by misrepresentation.

If asking permission: say what you want to look
for. Don't say 'just a quick chat' then search.

DPP v Madden [1977] IR
336

Items seized must be within scope of warrant (or
plain view doctrine). Wholesale seizure can render
whole search unlawful.

Plain view = (i) lawfully on premises, (ii) item
in immediate view, (iii) incriminating nature
immediately apparent. Don't open drawers
'looking for' unrelated items.

DPP v McMahon [1986] IR
393

Using one statutory power as cover for another
purpose = trespass ab initio.

Don't enter under licensing inspection power to
gather drugs intelligence. Use the proper power
or get the proper warrant.

DPP v Fagan [2018] IECA
32

Search incident to lawful arrest extends to person,
immediate area, and vehicle if applicable.

Once arrested, you can search for
weapons/evidence on person + within reach.
Search of vehicle requires the person to have
been in/near it.

DPP v Cooney [1997] 3 IR
205

Power to detain for search (e.g. s.23 MDA) must
be exercised on reasonable suspicion, articulable.

Be ready to articulate: smell of cannabis,
observed transaction, intelligence, behaviour,
location. 'Just a hunch' = unlawful.

DPP v O'Donnell [1995] 3
IR 551

Strip search lawful only where (a) statutory
authority + (b) reasonable necessity + (c)
appropriate setting.

Strip search in cells, same gender, two
members, recorded, MIC informed. Never
roadside unless intimate search powers met.

McGrath v Commissioner
[1991] 1 IR 69

Search of person — must be proportionate.
Intrusive search needs higher justification.

Pat-down is one thing; full strip search is
another. Document the necessity.

DPP v Mallon
IECCA 29

[2011]

Photographing/recording inside dwelling during
search must be within warrant scope.

Don't film what isn't relevant. Treat dwellings
with respect — bodyworn or station camera,
focus on the evidence.

DPP v O'Mahony [2019]
IECA 111

Where warrant covers 'evidence relating to
offence', this is interpreted reasonably —
phones/computers covered for indictable offences.

Phones, devices, USBs — seize for forensic
exam. Bag, label, chain of custody, no poweron.

DPP v Murphy [2005] 4 IR
504

Lawful presence on premises for one purpose does
not authorise exploratory search.

Called to a domestic? You don't have search
powers unless you arrest + s.6 CJA, or smell/see
something giving fresh grounds.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

S.6 CJA 2006 (statutory)

Garda may search a person/vehicle in a public
place where reasonable suspicion of an offence.

Articulate the suspicion. Common in inner-city:
observed hand-to-hand, smell of cannabis,
known target, area history.

S.23 Misuse of Drugs Act
1977

Power to search person, vehicle, vessel where
reasonable suspicion of controlled drug.

The workhorse on the street. Document
grounds:
smell,
behaviour,
observed
transaction, intelligence, prior interaction.

S.30 OASA 1939

Stop, search, detain for suspected scheduled
offence (firearms, explosives, organised crime).

Inner-city firearms intelligence: this is your
power. Don't deploy it casually.

S.8 CJA 1984

Fingerprints/photographs/samples from detained
persons — statutory authority required.

Specific procedures: notice, recording, refusal
sanctions. Brief in advance — don't ad-lib.

3. Evidence, Admissibility & Confessions
Every interview, every exhibit, every CCTV harvest sits on these foundations. Voluntariness, chain of custody, Judges' Rules,
identification doctrine — collectively determine whether the file survives.
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

DPP v Lynch [1982] IR 64

Confession must be voluntary.
inducement, threats, promises
inadmissible.

Oppression,
render it

No 'help yourself by talking' language. No deals
offered. No hint of leniency. Stick to the script.

DPP v McCann [1998] 4 IR
397

Oppression is conduct that saps the will — long
sessions, hostile tone, sleep deprivation can all
amount to oppression.

Manage interview length, breaks, refreshment,
sleep. Don't bear down.

DPP v Pringle (1981) 2
Frewen 57

Judges' Rules — caution required before
questioning a suspect. Re-caution after breaks.

Caution on arrest. Caution before each
interview session. Caution after a meaningful
break.

DPP v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268

Trial judge has discretion to exclude even
voluntary confession where unfair.

Procedural fairness throughout. Custody Regs
are minimum, not aspiration.

DPP v Healy [1990] 2 IR 73

Constitutional right of reasonable access to
solicitor. Denial = exclusion of evidence obtained
during denial.

Never delay solicitor for tactical reasons. If
genuinely delayed (e.g. solicitor en route),
document and minimise.

DPP v Diver [2005] 3 IR 270

Adverse inference provisions (ss.18, 19, 19A CJA
1984) require strict compliance: specific question,
ordinary language, consequences explained.

Read the inference warning verbatim. Have
solicitor consultation first. Ask specific
questions about specific items/places/marks.

DPP v Mallon
IECCA 29

[2011]

Failure to electronically record interview where
mandated renders statement unreliable; may be
inadmissible.

Check equipment before interview starts. If
failure: stop, log it, decide whether to proceed.
Don't proceed off-camera 'just because'.

DPP v Connolly [2003] 2 IR
1

Right to silence — adverse inference only where
statutory conditions met and offence is in
scheduled category.

Inference offences are specific. Don't draw
inferences for routine offences.

DPP v Finnerty [1999] 4 IR
364

Right to silence at trial — failure to testify cannot
be commented on by prosecution at common law.

Don't comment on silence in court unless
statutory inference regime applies and
conditions met.

DPP v Bowes [2004] 4 IR
223

Chain of custody (continuity) essential for forensic
items. Every handler must be traceable.

Seal exhibits with tamper-evident bag. Sign and
timestamp every transfer. PEMS entries must
match physical labels.

DPP v Gilligan [2006] 1 IR
107

Hearsay rule — out-of-court statements offered for
truth are inadmissible unless exception applies.

Statements must be from witness with direct
knowledge. 'A man in the pub told me' has no
value.

DPP v Cronin (No.2) [2006]
4 IR 329

Points not raised at trial cannot generally be raised
on appeal — issues must be ventilated.

Brief prosecutor on all potential issues. Don't
assume defence will raise them — they may not.

DPP v Doolan [2018] IECA
320

Forensic evidence — expert must explain
methodology and limitations. DNA mixtures
particularly contentious.

FSI report = essential. Be ready to explain
sampling continuity in court.

DPP v O'Sullivan [2017]
IECA 271

CCTV evidence must be properly authenticated —
chain from camera to court.

Statement from system owner re: time/date
accuracy, copying method, custody. CCTV
proforma — fill it properly.

DPP v Curtin [2006] IECCA
33

Digital evidence requires technical authentication;
ESI must show integrity (hash values).

Computer/phone exams — Garda Cyber Crime
Bureau or local digital examiner. Hash before
exam.

DPP v Doyle [2015] IECA 5

Identification evidence — Turnbull warning
required (long observation, lighting, distance,
recognition, prior knowledge).

ID parade where possible. If informal ID,
document conditions: ADVOKATE — Amount
of time, Distance, Visibility, Obstruction,
Known/seen before, Any reason to remember,
Time elapsed, Errors.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

The People (DPP) v
Maguire [2018] IECA 310

Voice identification
corroboration needed.

caution;

Don't rely solely on voice ID without supporting
evidence (location, content of speech, prior
contact).

DPP v Quinn [1965] IR 366

Dock
identification
(in-court)
generally
undesirable — formal ID parade preferred.

Set up ID parade promptly while memory fresh.
Use Garda ID Bureau or video parade (VIPER).

The People (AG) v Casey
(No.2) [1963] IR 33

Visual identification by single witness
corroboration warning required.

—

Push for corroboration: CCTV, forensics,
admissions, recent possession. ID alone is risky.

DPP v Kelly [2007] IECCA
21

Bad character / previous convictions — generally
inadmissible unless gateway opens (e.g. attack on
witness character).

Don't volunteer accused's history in interview.
Wait for the gateway.

S.16 Criminal Justice Act
2006

Witness statement may be admitted at trial despite
witness refusing/recanting if certain conditions
met.

Domestic
violence,
gangland:
witness
withdrawal common. Take statement properly
first time — voluntary, recorded, signed, dated.

—

extreme
