4. Use of Force
S.18 NFOAPA codifies the framework. Subjective belief plus objective reasonableness. Every use of force is reviewed by GSOC,
the Coroner where deaths occur, and ultimately the courts.
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

DPP v Dwyer [1972] IR 416

Self-defence — force must be reasonable in the
circumstances as the Garda honestly believed them
to be.

Subjective belief + objective reasonableness.
Articulate what you perceived, why, and what
options you considered.

S.18 Non-Fatal Offences
Against the Person Act 1997

Statutory codification of lawful use of force: selfdefence, defence of another, protection of property,
prevention of crime, lawful arrest.

Cite s.18 NFOAPA in every use of force report.
Detail: (1) belief, (2) circumstances as you
believed them, (3) reasonableness of response.

S.20 NFOAPA 1997

Honest belief in circumstances justifying force is
sufficient even if mistaken, provided honest.

Mistake of fact does not vitiate self-defence
claim if honestly held — but reasonableness still
matters for force used.

DPP v Nally [2006] IECCA
128

No requirement to retreat from a lawful place
before using force in self-defence.

Inner-city: you don't have to back off when
lawfully on duty. But disengagement is often the
safer policing option.

DPP v Barnes
IECCA 165

[2006]

Defence of property — lethal force rarely justified
for property alone; must threaten person.

Burglary in progress with householder present =
potential threat to person. Burglary of empty
premises = property only.

McCann v UK (1996) 21
EHRR 97

ECHR Art 2 — lethal force only where absolutely
necessary. Planning of operation scrutinised.

Pre-planned operations (raids, ARU): risk
assessment, briefing, less-lethal options
documented.

Ryan v DPP [1989] IR 399

Reasonable force in effecting lawful arrest;
excessive force renders arrest unlawful and Garda
civilly/criminally liable.

Force continuum: communication → control →
contact → CS/baton → firearm. Justify each
escalation in your statement.

DPP v Davis [1993] 2 IR 1

Where force used is grossly disproportionate,
defence of self-defence fails entirely.

Knee on neck for compliant subject = grossly
disproportionate. Compliance ends force.

The People (DPP) v Sheehan
[2018] IECA 30

Restraint techniques — risk of positional asphyxia
must be considered.

Get the subject onto their side or sit them up as
soon as restrained. Continuous monitoring. Call
ambulance if any breathing concern.

DPP v Bowe & Casey
[2017] IECA 250

Multiple officer restraint must be coordinated —
pile-ons risk death.

Designated roles in restraint: head/airway,
limbs, torso. One officer calls the holds.

DPP v Doran [2002] IECCA

Use of pepper spray/CS — must be proportionate
to threat and risk assessed.

Distance, ventilation, contamination of others.
Record the spray, the warning, the result.

DPP v Reid [2009] IECCA
18

Use of firearm by Garda — strict scrutiny; force
continuum and absolute necessity.

Firearms = last resort. ARU/RSU on serious
threat only. Conventional Garda not armed
routinely.

Hill v Chief Constable of
West Yorkshire [1989] AC
53 (persuasive)

Garda generally owes no duty of care in negligence
for operational decisions in pursuit/investigation.

Operational immunity exists but does not
protect bad faith or gross negligence.

O'Keeffe v Hickey [2009] 2
IR 302

Vicarious liability of State for actions of Garda in
course of employment.

Acting in scope = State pays. Frolic of your own
= personal liability.

Garda Síochána Code of
Ethics 2017

Professional standards on use of force, dignity,
accountability.

Read it. Quote it in reports. It frames every
disciplinary review.

5. Road Traffic
Strict statutory compliance is everything. The intoxilyser/MIT regime is unforgiving — one procedural slip and the case is gone.
Avadenei and Finn are essential reading.
Case / Authority

Principle / Ratio

Operational Application

DPP v Finn [2003] 1 IR 372

S.13 RTA 1994 — strict compliance with
intoxilyser procedure required.

20-minute observation period: continuous, in

19. Inner-City Dublin Operational Playbook
Common Mountjoy/DMR North Central scenarios with the legal frame mapped to them. These are the situations that recur
— preparation here saves time in custody, statement-writing, and court.

Street-level drug deal observed (Hand-to-hand)
•

Power to stop/search: s.23 MDA 1977 (drugs) — articulate grounds (observed transaction, smell, intelligence,
behaviour, location).

•

Arrest: s.3 MDA possession if found — or s.15 if quantity/packaging suggests supply.

•
•

Detention: s.4 CJA 1984 once at station — even if low-level. MIC, custody record, rights notification.
Search of person: incident to arrest under common law + s.6 CJA 2006. Pat-down first, fuller search in cells with
two members same gender.

•

Phone seizure: yes — likely to contain evidence (s.6 CJA 2006 powers extend to articles 'evidence of offence').
Don't power on. Bag, label, Garda Cyber Crime Bureau or local digital examiner.

•

Cash: photograph, count with second member, lodge per Force Order. Consider CAB referral if substantial.

•

Charge: charge sheet specifying section, drug, quantity, value (estimate pending FSI). For s.15A: must show
value ≥ €13,000.

•

Case law to cite: Foley (knowledge), Ducque (inference of supply), Reilly (joint possession), Power (s.15A
threshold).

Public order incident — late-night city centre
•

Power to direct (move on): s.8 CJ(PO)A 1994. Verbal direction, clear, give opportunity. Document direction in
notebook + bodyworn camera.

•

Power to arrest: s.4 (intoxication + danger), s.5 (disorderly), s.6 (threatening/abusive). Choose section matching
specific conduct.

•

Group disturbance: consider s.15 (violent disorder — 3+ persons using unlawful violence) or s.16 (affray — 3+
persons using/threatening violence such that reasonable person would fear for safety).

•
•

Assault on Garda: s.19 CJ(PO)A 1994 (lower threshold than s.3 NFOAPA, higher penalty than s.2).
Force used: document under s.18 NFOAPA framework. State what you saw, what you believed, what you did,
why.

•

Witnesses: many in city centre — names + addresses, brief statement at scene where possible. CCTV from
premises (Temple Bar, O'Connell St, Capel St) — preserve immediately, 24–72 hr window.

•

Case law: Heeney (s.6 threshold), Lyons (s.8 direction), Cagney (recklessness), Tanner (harm), Mahon/Brennan
(violent disorder/affray).

Domestic incident — Mountjoy area call
•

First action: separate parties, ensure safety, medical if needed (ambulance).

•

Check PULSE en route: prior incidents, current orders (safety/protection/barring), risk markers, weapon markers.

•

Children present? — TUSLA notification mandatory. Document presence, behaviour.

•

Risk assessment: complete DVRAA/DASH at scene. High risk = MARAC, safety plan.

•

Arrest power: s.2 NFOAPA (simple assault), s.3 NFOAPA (assault causing harm), s.4 NFOAPA (serious harm),
s.5 (threats to kill), s.39 DVA 2018 (coercive control if pattern), s.33 DVA breach of order.

•

Strangulation: always ask 'was there pressure on your neck?' Document red marks, petechiae, voice changes.
Strangulation = strong homicide predictor.
Statement: take from complainant ASAP — first account is best account. Don't delay. Record demeanour, injuries
(photograph).

•

•

Statement protection: explain s.16 CJA 2006 — first statement may be used even if later withdrawn.

•

Bail objection: prepare for opposition with specific evidence (prior incidents, escalation, threats, intimidation
potential).

•

Case law: Eadon (coercive control pattern), MK (no delay inference), O'Loughlin (strangulation), O'Reilly
(spouse competence).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Burglary — residential
•

Power to enter (if in progress): hot pursuit / s.6 CJA 1997 / s.10 CJA 1984 / specific statutory authority. Otherwise:
warrant.

•

Scene preservation: tape off, log entries/exits, photograph in situ before disturbance. Forensic call-out (Garda
Technical Bureau for serious).

•

Charges: s.12 CJ(T&FO)A 2001 (burglary); s.13 (aggravated burglary — weapon); s.14 (robbery if force/threat
used during).

•

Recent possession: stolen goods recovered = strong inference. Interview immediately on provenance.

•
•

Tools recovered: s.15 CJ(T&FO)A possession of articles + s.11 trespass with intent (alternative charge).
CCTV harvest: route from premises to where suspect went. Door-to-door for additional cameras. 7-day priority
window.

•

Case law: Bowe (entry to restricted area), Brennan (recent possession), Berry (aggravated), Ryan (article
possession).

Assault — city centre weekend
•

Medical first. Photograph injuries within 24 hr (bruising appears later). Medical statement from treating
doctor/ED.

•

Witness canvas: bouncers, taxi drivers, bystanders, paramedics. CCTV from venue + street.

•

Charge: s.2 NFOAPA (no harm/minor); s.3 (harm — bruising, cuts treated); s.4 (serious harm — fractures, stab,
head injury). Match section to medical evidence.

•

Joint enterprise: group assault — each participant's role + foresight. Question each separately on what they
did/saw/foresaw.

•

Self-defence interview: take account fully — provocation, fear, retreat options. Don't dismiss; assess.

•

Recklessness mens rea: question on awareness of risk. Cagney standard — subjective foresight.

•

Case law: Tanner (harm), Cagney (recklessness), Maples (joint enterprise), Edwards (spitting), Sheehan (restraint
risk).

Suspected gangland incident
•

Initial response: scene preservation paramount. Cordons wide. Witness identification before scatter.

•
•

Detective notification: immediate. Inspector-level call-out for serious.
CCTV — every camera within 1 km. Time critical — overwrite cycles.

•

Ballistic evidence (firearm): mark in situ, FSI ballistics, chain of custody pristine.

•

Witness protection considerations from the outset — anonymous statements where lawful, video link,
intermediary.

•

Intelligence: GNDOCB, local crime office, surveillance — share information through proper channels.

•

Charging strategy: substantive offence + s.71/72 CJA 2006 (organised crime) + firearms s.15/27 + witness
intimidation (s.41 CJA 1999) if applicable.

•

SCC certification: where intimidation risk material, brief DPP on basis for non-jury trial.

•

Case law: Birney (organised crime offence), Ward/SCC (PII), Gilligan (accomplice corroboration), Hourigan
(surveillance).

Mental health crisis
•

S.12 Mental Health Act 2001: Garda may take person believed to be suffering mental disorder + immediate
danger to themselves/others to approved centre.

•
•

Document: behaviour observed, immediate danger basis, opinion formed.
Use of force: minimum necessary. De-escalation first. Restraint techniques avoiding asphyxia risk (Sheehan).

•

Ambulance + AMHP (Approved Mental Health Practitioner) where possible — Garda role is bridging, not
assessment.

•

If criminal offence committed: balance criminal pathway vs. mental health pathway. Often diversion/treatment is
correct.

•

Custody: if detained for offence, MIC informed of mental health, medical attention, suicide risk markers.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Sudden death / coroner's case
•

Scene preservation: presumption of suspicious until ruled out. Don't disturb.

•

Continuity: log all attendees. Photograph in situ.

•

Witness statements: who found, when, what observed, last seen alive.

•

Coroner notification: per Coroners Act 1962 + 2019 amendments. Doctor to certify or refer to Coroner.

•

PM: attend where possible. Liaise with State Pathologist's Office for suspicious.

•
•

Family liaison: trained FLO where significant. Document all communications.
Don't speculate to family on cause. Don't release information beyond what is necessary.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

20. Detective Toolkit & Investigative Doctrine
Specialist powers, evidence-gathering doctrine, and investigative frameworks used in CID, GNDOCB, GNBCI, and DDU
work. Relevant to probationers being briefed by detectives and to those aspiring to detective work.

Surveillance
•

Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009: authorisation by Superintendent + District Court judge for serious
offences.

•

Categories: tracking devices, recording devices, surveillance devices.

•

Internal: Garda Surveillance Unit liaison. Documentation: authorisation, operational orders, observation logs.

•

Product admissibility: continuity, integrity, judicial authorisation. Hourigan and subsequent cases.

•

Without authorisation: limited routine observation lawful; intrusive surveillance requires authority.

Covert Human Intelligence Sources (CHIS / Informants)
•

Garda Síochána Policy on Use of CHIS — strict registration + handling + control regime.

•

Two-handler rule: never single-handler relationship.

•

Tasking and authorisation chain — recorded.

•

Disclosure: identity protected via PII (Ward case). Don't disclose without DPP consultation.

•

Risk assessment ongoing. Welfare of source considered.

•

Product use: corroborate before action. Don't act on uncorroborated single-source.

Telecoms & Digital Evidence
•

Communications (Retention of Data) Act 2011 — post-Dwyer regime restricted. Targeted requests only.

•

Phone forensics: Cellebrite/UFED extractions by trained examiner. Hash before exam.

•

Social media: open source (OSINT) lawful; private accounts require warrant/MLA.

•
•

Cell site analysis: provider records — geolocation by tower handshakes. Expert testimony essential.
Encryption: legal compulsion to provide passwords limited (s.7 Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act 2014
— DNA samples; digital different).

•

Digital chain of custody: imaging, hashing, write-blockers, examiner statement. Same standards as physical.

Financial Investigation
•

Money laundering offences (Criminal Justice (Money Laundering & Terrorist Financing) Act 2010): wide ambit,
includes knowledge/reasonable grounds.

•

CAB powers: civil forfeiture on balance of probabilities; lower standard.

•

Bank disclosure: production orders, account monitoring orders.

•

STR (Suspicious Transaction Reports) — FIU. Triggers for investigation.

•

Lifestyle evidence: unexplained wealth, expenditure exceeding declared income.

•

Asset tracing: vehicles, property, accounts — register checks (RBO, Land Registry, CRO, RSA).

Forensic Strategy
•

FSI (Forensic Science Ireland): DNA, fibres, footwear, glass, paint, gunshot residue, fire investigation.

•
•

Garda Technical Bureau: fingerprints, photography, mapping, ballistics, document examination, scenes of crime.
Sampling under Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence and DNA Database System) Act 2014 — strict procedure.

•

DNA Database: hits trigger investigation but not standalone proof.

•

Fingerprint evidence: AFIS comparison; expert opinion.

•

Footwear: pattern + wear/damage marks; database comparison.

•

Sample contamination: avoid at all costs. PPE, single-use, no return to scene.

Identification — Detective Doctrine

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

•

Casey warning: single-witness ID risky — corroborate.

•

Identification parade: VIPER (Video Identification Parade Electronic Recording) preferred. Garda ID Bureau.

•

Quilliam (England, persuasive) + Doyle: conditions for safe ID — ADVOKATE.

•
•

Dock identification (Quinn): disfavoured.
Voice ID (Maguire): extreme caution; corroborate.

•

CCTV recognition: where Garda recognises suspect from prior knowledge — admissible with explanation.

Witness Management
•

PEACE model interviewing for witnesses (cognitive interview).

•

Vulnerable witness — special measures (s.13–19 CEA 1992).

•

Witness intimidation: charges under s.41 CJA 1999 + bail objections + protection measures.

•

Anonymity: limited in Irish law — apply through DPP / Court.

•

Witness Care: family liaison, court familiarisation, transport assistance.

•

Retracting witness: s.16 CJA 2006 statement admissibility option.

Joint Investigations
•

Europol / Eurojust: serious cross-border.

•

European Arrest Warrant (EAW): extradition within EU.

•
•

MLA (Mutual Legal Assistance) Act 2008: outside EU.
Joint Investigation Teams: formal multi-jurisdictional team.

•

Lead times long (months) — plan early.

Major Incident / Crime Investigation Doctrine
•

Senior Investigating Officer (SIO) — typically Inspector or Superintendent.

•

Major Incident Room (MIR): structured. Investigation log, decision log, action allocations.

•

PIP (Professionalising Investigation Programme) — UK model used as reference.

•

Decision log: every significant decision recorded with rationale (HOLMES-equivalent).

•

Action management: tasking, deadline, accountability.

•

Family liaison officer (FLO) — trained role.

•
•

Media strategy: through Press Office. Don't ad-lib.
Reviews: 7-day, 28-day, peer review. Identify gaps early.

S.4 CJA 1984 Clock — Quick Reference
•

Initial detention: up to 6 hours (MIC authorisation)

•

First extension: up to 6 more hours (Superintendent)

•

Second extension: up to 12 more hours (Superintendent / Chief Superintendent depending)

•

Total maximum (s.4): 24 hours

•

S.30 OASA: 24 hrs + 24 hrs (Chief Super) + 24 hrs (Court) = 72 hrs

•

S.50 CJA 2007 (organised crime/serious): 7 days with court extensions

•

Excludable time: medical, solicitor consult, sleep period (midnight–8am normally), arrival/departure

The Custody Regs Essentials
•

Member-in-charge informed at point of arrival

•

•

Custody record entries: contemporaneous, timestamped, signed

•

•

Charge Sheet Drafting — Common Errors to Avoid
•

Wrong section / wrong Act — read the statute again before drafting

•

Date/time imprecise — narrow to known window

•

Location vague — full address or precise description

•

Mens rea element missing in particulars where required (intent, knowledge, recklessness)

•

Drug — wrong schedule classification or wrong street value

•

Aggregated charges — separate counts where conduct discrete

•

Wrong defendant details — full name, DOB, address from PULSE-verified source

Statement Drafting — The Probationer Checklist
•

First person, past tense, chronological

•

Time/date/location of every observation

•

Distinguish what you saw, heard, did, were told

•

Hearsay carefully — note source, mark for relevance not truth where appropriate

•

Verbatim quotes for significant statements (caution, suspect words, witness words)

•

Continuity of any exhibit you handled — receipt, custody, transfer

•

Sign + date + Garda number

•

Bodyworn camera footage cross-referenced

Bodyworn Camera — Best Practice
•

Activate before any policing interaction

•

Announce 'I am recording'

•

Don't deactivate during use of force / arrest / search

•

Audit footage same shift — note discrepancies between your recollection and footage

•

Footage is evidence — handle with continuity standards

Disclosure Schedule — Maintaining the File
•

MG6C: used material

•

MG6D: unused material

•

Every item: number, description, location, who took/held

•

Sensitive material: separate schedule, not in main file, brief DPP

•

•

Anything potentially exculpatory: disclose

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Final Notes
This reference compresses years of doctrine into a working tool. It is not exhaustive — particular sections (homicide,
sexual offences, financial investigation) merit dedicated study. Treat the cited authorities as starting points; pull the
judgments where the issue is live in a case you are running.
Key habits that protect any Garda:
•

Articulate the power before you act — name the section, the Act, the grounds.

•

Document contemporaneously — same shift, in your own hand, before memory fades.

•
•

Treat every action as reviewable — because it is (GSOC, Coroner, courts, discipline, civil).
Caution properly, every time — the cost of over-cautioning is nothing; the cost of under-cautioning is the case.

•

Preserve everything — CCTV, exhibits, statements, scenes. Defence can request it; you must have it.

•

Read the statute again — not the textbook summary. The wording is everything.

• Ask the detective when unsure — case strategy is their territory, but they need your scene knowledge.
Stay safe. Stay lawful. Stay accountable.

WORKING REFERENCE — NOT LEGAL ADVICE
Case law evolves. Always verify exact neutral citations on Bailii / courts.ie and confirm the live statute before
relying in evidence or court. This reference distils principles and operational application for training and
preparation — it does not replace formal legal advice or the official legal databases.

How to Use This Reference
This reference covers Irish criminal procedure and case law for the mid-level operational Garda. It is organised
into six volumes — Powers, Evidence and Interview, Offences, Constitutional and Procedure, Witness
Handling, and Common Mistakes. The material is calibrated for the operational Garda who knows the basics
and needs depth for cross-examination preparation, file building, supervisor briefing, and spotting problems
before they become defects.
Each case entry contains:
• FACTS — what happened in the case
• PRINCIPLE — what the court held and why
• RATIO — the binding principle distilled
• TEST — the question to ask yourself when applying it
• HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT — what defence will ask, what trial judge will examine
• SPECIMEN TESTIMONY — model wording you can adapt for evidence
• PRACTICAL APPLICATION — what you do differently on the ground
• WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING — supporting record-keeping requirements
• COMMON PITFALLS — what actually goes wrong
• CROSS-REFERENCES — related cases and statutory provisions
Statutory entries follow a similar structure with FRAMEWORK / ELEMENTS / POWERS / TEST sections.
Mistakes entries (Volume 6) follow ISSUE / WHY / HOW / PREVENT structure.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Use the reference forwards (case-law → operational application) when learning or preparing for court, and
backwards (need a practical answer → find the case-law foundation) when handling a specific situation. Crossreferences between sections are deliberate — a single operational situation often engages multiple legal
frameworks.
VOLUME 1
POWERS
Arrest, Search, Detention — The Coercive Foundation

1. Arrest, Caution & Suspect Rights
Arrest is the gateway. Every coercive act that follows flows from a lawful arrest. The mid-level Garda must be
fluent not just in headline cases but in operational mechanics of how arrest powers are challenged in court and
defended in cross-examination.
Arrest is the gateway. Every coercive act that follows — detention, search of person, interview, fingerprints,
samples, charge — flows from a lawful arrest. Get the arrest wrong and the entire downstream case is exposed.
The mid-level Garda needs to be fluent not just in the headline cases but in the operational mechanics of how
arrest powers are challenged in court and how to defend each step in cross-examination.

1.1 The Foundation Cases — Reasonable Suspicion & Lawful Arrest
DPP v Walsh [1980] IR 294 (SC)
FACTS: The Gardaí arrested Walsh on the strength of a colleague's statement that he had reason to
suspect involvement. The arresting member had not independently formed suspicion at the moment
of arrest — he was acting on the colleague's word. Walsh challenged the lawfulness of his arrest on
this basis.
PRINCIPLE: The Supreme Court held that an arrest is a personal act by the arresting member. The
member who lays hands on the suspect must themselves hold reasonable suspicion at the moment of
arrest. It is not enough that another Garda has the grounds; the arresting member's own state of mind
matters.
RATIO: Reasonable suspicion is personal and contemporaneous. The arresting member cannot
'borrow' suspicion from a colleague.
TEST: At the moment of laying hands on the suspect, did the arresting member personally hold
reasonable suspicion based on facts they were aware of? Suspicion formed later, or held only by
another member, does not retrospectively cure a defective arrest.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence counsel will ask: 'Garda, what did you yourself know
at the moment of arrest? Tell me the specific facts in your own mind.' If your answer is 'Sergeant
told me to arrest him', the arrest is defective. You must be able to articulate facts you personally
were aware of — what you saw, what you heard, what you were briefed on (and accepted on
reasonable grounds), what you observed in the suspect's conduct.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] on [date], I observed [the specific conduct or facts]. I had
also been briefed by Garda [name] that [content of briefing] and I had no reason to doubt that
information. On the basis of what I personally observed and the briefing which I accepted as reliable,
I formed a reasonable suspicion that the suspect had committed [offence] contrary to [section]. At
[time] I approached the suspect, identified myself, stated the reason for the arrest in plain terms and
administered the caution."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Before you arrest, articulate it in your own head in one sentence:
'I am arresting X because I personally know [facts] which give me reasonable grounds to suspect
[offence].' If you can't say that sentence honestly, don't arrest — get the grounds first. If you're acting
on a colleague's information, that's fine, but you must be told the substance, not just the order. A
briefing that includes facts and is accepted on reasonable grounds becomes your own.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Your notebook entry for the arrest must contain the
grounds in your own words. Not 'arrested on instructions of Sgt' — but the facts. Briefing officer

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

should also note that the briefing was given and what it contained. In court, both notebooks should
match.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Following orders blindly — 'Sergeant pointed and said arrest him' is
not enough. (2) Backfilling grounds after arrest in your statement — defence will spot the contrast
with your notebook. (3) Failing to mention the briefing source in evidence and then having it emerge
in cross-examination — looks like concealment. (4) Multi-member arrests where each thinks the
other had the grounds.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Christie v Leachinsky (reason at time of arrest); DPP v Pringle (caution);
Dunne v Clinton (lawful authority anchors all coercion); McMahon v Leahy (post-arrest
articulation).
Christie v Leachinsky [1947] AC 573 (HL) — applied in Irish law
FACTS: Leachinsky was arrested under a Liverpool by-law which conferred no power of arrest.
The arresting officers told him only that he was being arrested for 'unlawful possession'. He sued for
false imprisonment. The House of Lords examined whether an arrest is lawful where the true legal
basis is not communicated.
PRINCIPLE: An arrested person is entitled to be told the true reason for the arrest in language they
can understand, unless circumstances make this impossible (e.g. they are violently resisting, fleeing,
or have made themselves incapable of understanding). Technical legal language is not required —
plain language is.
RATIO: Communication of reason at time of arrest is a constituent of lawful arrest, not a procedural
extra. Without it, the detention is unlawful from the outset.
TEST: Was the reason communicated at, or as soon as practicable after, the moment of arrest, in
language reasonably calculated to be understood by this particular suspect?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Counsel will often cross-examine on the precise words used at
arrest. 'What did you say to him at the doorstep?' Vague answers ('I told him he was under arrest')
are weak. Specific recollection, ideally captured in the notebook contemporaneously, is strong.
Where the suspect is intoxicated, has limited English, or is otherwise compromised, you should
record the steps taken to ensure understanding.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I approached the suspect at [location] at [time]. I said: 'I am Garda
[name] from [station]. I am arresting you under section [x] of the [Act] on suspicion of [offence]
which occurred at [place/time]. You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but
anything you say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.' I noted that the
suspect appeared to understand — he nodded, and said [response]. I made a contemporaneous note
in my official notebook."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Three things at arrest in this order: identify yourself + state the
offence + caution. 'I am Garda Mc Greevy, Mountjoy. I am arresting you for [offence]. You are not
obliged to say anything…' Practise this sentence cold. Adapt language for the suspect — plain
English for someone with limited education, interpreter for someone with limited English (note
arrival time of interpreter; until then, simplest possible explanation, refined when interpreter
present).
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Witness Garda where possible — independent
corroboration of the words used. Both notebooks contemporaneous. If suspect's response is
significant ('You've got the wrong man' / 'I know what this is about'), note it verbatim. Don't
paraphrase.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Mumbling the caution. (2) Stating the offence in legal jargon
('possession contrary to s.3 MDA 1977' rather than 'possession of cannabis'). (3) Not adapting to
obvious comprehension difficulties. (4) Skipping the caution because you 'said it earlier'. (5) Saying
'I think you know what this is about' rather than stating the offence — court reads this as evasive.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Walsh (personal suspicion); DPP v Pringle (caution wording);
DPP v Conroy (caution before incriminating question); Dunne v Clinton (constitutional foundation).
DPP v Pringle (1981) 2 Frewen 57 (CCA)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FACTS: Pringle was arrested and questioned in circumstances where the timing and adequacy of
the caution were challenged. The CCA considered the foundational rule that a caution must precede
any incriminating questioning of a suspect.
PRINCIPLE: The caution must be administered (a) at the time of arrest, (b) on entry to the interview
room before questioning starts, (c) after every break in interview, and (d) before any question
reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response. The words are: 'You are not obliged to say
anything unless you wish to do so, but anything you say will be taken down in writing and may be
given in evidence.'
RATIO: The caution operationalises the constitutional right against self-incrimination. Failure to
administer it at the required points renders the resulting statement vulnerable to exclusion.
TEST: At every moment when an incriminating answer might be elicited, was a fresh caution in
force? (A caution doesn't last forever — it 'resets' after breaks, transfers, time elapses.)
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will scrutinise the interview record for caution
intervals. Every break — toilet, meal, solicitor consult, transfer — and the caution must be readministered when interview resumes. Every notebook entry of a statement made should record that
the caution was given immediately before.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] I administered the caution in the standard form: 'You are
not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but anything you say will be taken down in
writing and may be given in evidence.' I asked the suspect 'Do you understand?' He replied
[verbatim]. I commenced the substantive interview at [time]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Caution wording memorised exact. Don't improvise. Don't add
anything. If asked 'what does that mean?', explain simply: 'You don't have to answer my questions.
But if you do answer, I'll write down what you say, and that can be used in court.' Don't say 'it'll help
if you cooperate' or anything that even hints at inducement (Tyndall).
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Recording (electronic, where in designated station)
captures the caution. In non-recorded settings, witness Garda corroborates. Notebook entry by both
members. Custody record by Member-in-Charge should also reflect cautions administered.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Cautioning at arrest then never again — defective on entry to
interview. (2) Paraphrasing the caution. (3) Adding extra-statutory commentary ('and just so you
know, if you don't cooperate we can…'). (4) Failing to caution before 'casual' chats that elicit
admissions (the casual chat is the interview if it produces evidence). (5) Cautioning after a break has
begun rather than before resumption.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Christie v Leachinsky (arrest communication); DPP v Conroy (caution
before specific incriminating question); Heaney v Ireland (right to silence); Doyle v DPP [2017]
(solicitor before incriminating question).
DPP v Conroy (1986) 3 Frewen 92 (CCA)
FACTS: Conroy made statements to Gardaí after being asked specific questions about an offence.
The adequacy and timing of the caution preceding the incriminating questions was challenged.
PRINCIPLE: The caution must precede the specific question reasonably likely to elicit an
incriminating answer. A general caution at arrest does not exhaust the obligation; a fresh caution is
required when the questioning turns to matters where an answer would tend to incriminate.
RATIO: Each shift in interview direction toward incriminating territory triggers fresh caution
obligation. The protection runs through the entire process, not just the door.
TEST: Before this specific question that could incriminate, was a caution in force? (Either freshly
administered, or recently administered with no intervening break or context shift.)
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial judge will examine the interview transcript / record for
caution placement against incriminating questions. Where the caution is remote in time from the
question that produced the evidence, the prosecution must justify that gap.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "Before asking the suspect specifically about [matter], I readministered the caution at [time]: '…'. The suspect indicated understanding. I then asked: '[verbatim

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

question]'. The suspect replied: '[verbatim answer]'."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: When in doubt, re-caution. The cost is 10 seconds; the benefit is
admissibility. Specifically: before deploying any inference warning (ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984);
before putting a key piece of evidence to the suspect; before any change in interviewer; after any
solicitor consultation.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Recorded interview makes this automatic if you discipline
yourself to caution on the record. In handwritten interviews, each fresh caution recorded with time.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating the arrest caution as the only caution needed. (2) Drifting
from background questions to substantive questions without fresh caution. (3) Failing to caution
after solicitor consults (suspect may now be ready to engage substantively).
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Pringle (basic caution); DPP v Connolly (inference warnings);
Heaney v Ireland.
Dunne v Clinton [1930] IR 366
FACTS: An early constitutional case on the nature of arrest. The Supreme Court considered the
constitutional foundation for any deprivation of liberty by Gardaí.
PRINCIPLE: Every arrest must be grounded in either a common law power (e.g. breach of the
peace) or statutory authority. There is no general power to detain for questioning, for investigation,
or 'to help with enquiries'. The Garda must be able to point to the legal authority for the act of arrest.
RATIO: Article 40.4.1 ('no citizen shall be deprived of liberty save in accordance with law') requires
specific legal authority for every detention. 'Helping with enquiries' is not detention; if a person is
not free to leave, that is arrest, and arrest needs a power.
TEST: Can I name the specific section or common-law rule under which I am exercising the power
to arrest right now? If not, the arrest is unlawful.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Cross-examination starts with 'Under what power did you arrest
my client?' If you can't name it, the case is in trouble. Even if you got the right power, defence may
probe whether you knew it at the time (notebook will show this).
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I arrested the suspect under section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act
1984 for an arrestable offence, namely [offence] contrary to [section/Act]. I had reasonable grounds
to suspect his involvement based on [grounds]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Carry the major arrest powers in your head: s.4 CJA 1984 (general
arrestable offences); s.30 OASA 1939 (scheduled offences); s.49 RTA 1961 (drunk driving); s.30
CJ(PO)A 1994 (public order); s.6 CJA 1984 (search incident to arrest, not an arrest power itself);
arrest without warrant for breach of the peace (common law). For drugs: arrest under s.4 CJA 1984
for the underlying MDA offence.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Notebook should record the specific power. Custody
record will also record it on intake. Both should match.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'Arrested for being a prick' / 'arrested under section any-section' —
comedy in the canteen, disaster in court. (2) Wrong section cited at arrest (e.g. s.30 OASA where
the offence is not scheduled). (3) Treating 'voluntary attendance' as cover for what is in fact
detention.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.4.1 Bunreacht; DPP v Walsh; State (Trimbole) v Governor
(lawful authority required throughout).

1.2 Statutory Arrest Powers — Section by Section
STATUTE — Section 4, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Detention for arrestable offences
FRAMEWORK: Where a person is arrested without warrant for an arrestable offence (an offence
carrying 5 years imprisonment on first conviction), they may be detained for the proper investigation
of that offence. The Member-in-Charge at the station authorises initial detention if satisfied detention
is necessary for proper investigation. Maximum 24 hours absent re-arrest.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

ELEMENTS: (a) Arrest without warrant; (b) for an arrestable offence; (c) reasonable suspicion of
involvement; (d) Member-in-Charge satisfied detention is necessary for proper investigation.
POWERS / EFFECT: Detention authorised initially for up to 6 hours by Member-in-Charge.
Extension to 12 hours by Superintendent's written direction. Extension to 24 hours by Chief
Superintendent's written direction. Total cap 24 hours absent fresh grounds for re-arrest under s.10.
TEST: Each extension requires the named rank to be satisfied that further detention is necessary for
proper investigation. Mere convenience or rolling-clock detention is not lawful.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will scrutinise: (a) was arrest properly grounded
(Walsh)? (b) was detention timely authorised by Member-in-Charge? (c) was each extension
separately considered, in writing, by the correct rank? (d) was the suspect kept appraised of detention
status? Any gap in the chain — for instance, an extension authorised retrospectively — risks
exclusion of evidence obtained in the gap.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] I conveyed the suspect to [station]. I presented him to
Member-in-Charge Sgt [name]. The grounds for arrest were explained and the offence stated. At
[time], Sgt [name] authorised detention under s.4 CJA 1984, recording in the custody record his
satisfaction that detention was necessary for the proper investigation of the offence."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Each rank, each extension, written direction, signed and timed.
Treat the clock as starting at custody record opening (not at moment of laying hands on suspect, but
defence may argue both). Plan interviews within the clock — first interview within first 4 hours
typically, allowing solicitor consultation, rest, meals.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record is the foundational document. Memberin-Charge entries must be contemporaneous. Solicitor visits, meals, sleep, exercise, medical
attention — all logged. Independent witness in custody record is the case.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Detention authorised by Member-in-Charge of insufficient rank or
status. (2) Extensions late — the Super signs at hour 6:30 when the 6-hour extension should have
been at hour 6:00. (3) Suspect not informed of detention status changes. (4) Interview commencing
before solicitor consulted where suspect requested.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien (24hr cap); DPP v Finn (extension grounds); DPP v
Boylan (clock suspensions); DPP v Buck (Custody Regs); Walsh; Christie v Leachinsky.
STATUTE — Section 30, Offences Against the State Act 1939 Detention for scheduled offences
FRAMEWORK: Power to arrest without warrant where reasonable suspicion of involvement in a
scheduled offence under the Act, or any offence under the Act, or treason. Detention up to 72 hours
total.
ELEMENTS: (a) Reasonable suspicion; (b) of scheduled offence (Schedule to OASA or by
Ministerial order — typically firearms, explosives, organised crime offences); (c) arrest without
warrant.
POWERS / EFFECT: Initial 24 hours by Member-in-Charge authorisation. Further 24 hours by
Chief Superintendent direction. Further 24 hours by District Judge order (made on Garda
application, suspect entitled to representation). Maximum 72 hours.
TEST: Each phase requires the named decision-maker to be satisfied that further detention is
necessary for proper investigation. The DJ phase is judicial — suspect represented, application
contested.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will examine: was the offence properly characterised
as 'scheduled'? Was the s.30 application well-founded? At the DJ stage, the prosecution must justify
continued detention to a court. Where the underlying suspicion thins out, continued detention is
challenged.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I arrested the suspect under section 30 of the Offences Against the
State Act 1939 on suspicion of [scheduled offence — e.g. possession of a firearm contrary to section
27A Firearms Act 1964, which is a scheduled offence]. My grounds were [specific grounds]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: S.30 is for the seriously suspected offence — firearms, explosives,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

OC-related. Not a tool for general crime. Get the scheduled offence right at the moment of arrest;
switching basis later raises challenge. The DJ application is a formal court process — file prepared,
evidence summarised, often objections from defence.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Each authorisation in writing. Member-in-Charge custody
record. Chief Super direction with grounds. DJ order signed and timed. Each Garda involved with
contemporaneous notes.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) S.30 used where offence is not scheduled. (2) Suspicion not articulable
when challenged. (3) Inadequate file for DJ application — last-minute scramble. (4) Treating s.30
detention as routine — it is exceptional and high-scrutiny.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Heaney v Ireland (s.52 — historic but instructive on rights during s.30);
Damache v DPP (independent issuer for warrants — separate but illustrates court scrutiny); DPP v
Quilligan (s.30 detention purpose).
STATUTE — Section 30 (as amended), Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Arrest for public order
offences
FRAMEWORK: Power to arrest without warrant for public order offences under the Act where
reasonable suspicion exists.
ELEMENTS: (a) Reasonable suspicion; (b) of public order offence under the Act (s.4 intoxication
in public place; s.5 disorderly conduct; s.6 threatening/abusive/insulting behaviour; s.8 failure to
comply with direction; s.9 obstruction of peace officer; s.13 trespass; s.19 assault on peace officer).
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest without warrant; transport to station for charge or release; underlying
offences are typically summary, charged in District Court.
TEST: Reasonable suspicion of one of the listed offences. The conduct must be specific and
articulable.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: District Court trials of public order offences turn on the specific
conduct. Defence challenges focus on: (a) was the conduct as described? (b) was it in a public place?
(c) for s.6, was a breach of peace likely or threatened? Garda evidence must be specific,
contemporaneous, witness-corroborated.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] at [public place], I observed the accused [specific verbatim
conduct — words used, gestures, audience reaction]. The conduct in my opinion amounted to
[offence] contrary to s.[x] of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994. I cautioned and arrested
under s.30 of the Act. I conveyed him to [station]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Public order: capture the conduct precisely. Generic descriptions
like 'he was being a nuisance' or 'causing a disturbance' lose cases. Specific verbatim wording with
context wins them — the exact words used, the gestures, the audience reaction (members of the
public stopping, looking, moving away), time of day, type of premises. The more specific the
notebook entry made at the time, the stronger the case at trial.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Witness Garda corroboration is gold. Bystanders' details
where they can be taken without losing the suspect. CCTV from nearby pubs/clubs/shops — request
immediately.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Vague characterisations. (2) S.6 charged without articulating breach
of peace likelihood. (3) Intoxication s.4 charged where conduct is really s.6 (s.6 is the better charge
where disorder element present). (4) Failing to caution at arrest in the heat of the moment.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Heeney (recklessness sufficient for s.6); DPP v Cagney
(intoxication charge limits); DPP v Forsey (public place definition).
STATUTE — Section 49(8), Road Traffic Act 1961 (as amended) Arrest for drunk driving
FRAMEWORK: Power to arrest without warrant where opinion formed that a person is committing
or has committed an offence under s.49 (driving / attempting to drive / being in charge while
intoxicated to excess).
ELEMENTS: (a) Garda forms opinion (b) that person is committing / has committed s.49 offence

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

(c) on reasonable grounds (e.g. smell of intoxicant, slurred speech, unsteady on feet, evident
impairment, positive roadside breath test, accident circumstances).
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest without warrant. Power to require breath sample (s.12 RTA 2010) at
station via evidential breath machine, or blood / urine via designated doctor. Statutory specimen
procedure must be strictly followed.
TEST: Garda opinion that offence is being / has been committed, formed on reasonable grounds.
Opinion need not be certainty.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: RTA prosecutions are procedure-critical. Defence challenges
typically attack: (a) grounds for opinion (was it more than 'he was driving'?), (b) statutory specimen
procedure (20-minute observation, demand wording, machine certification, doctor authorisation),
(c) form completion (s.17 RTA 2010 certified statement), (d) calibration, (e) chain to lab for
blood/urine.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] at [location], I stopped vehicle reg [x] which I observed
[specific driving — weaving, slow response to traffic signals, etc]. On approach, I detected a strong
smell of intoxicating liquor from the driver. I noted his eyes were glassy, his speech slurred. I formed
the opinion he was driving while intoxicated. I administered a roadside breath test on calibrated
device [serial number]. Result: fail. At [time], I arrested under s.49(8) RTA 1961 and conveyed him
to [station] for evidential breath analysis under s.12 RTA 2010."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the opinion: driving observed + physical signs (smell, eyes,
speech, balance) + roadside test (if available). Each adds weight. At station: 20-minute observation
(nothing in mouth — no smoking, eating, vomiting), demand in prescribed wording, breath via
certified machine OR blood/urine via designated doctor. The form is the case — fill every box, sign,
time.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Roadside witness Garda where available. Designated
doctor for blood/urine — their note is crucial. Member-in-Charge custody record. The s.17 certified
statement form is the prosecution document — keep working copies.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 20-minute observation period not properly observed (suspect smokes,
vomits, has water — restart the clock). (2) Demand wording paraphrased rather than read verbatim.
(3) Form not signed at the right moment. (4) Calibration records not available. (5) Chain of custody
for blood / urine to MBRS lab broken. (6) Failure to caution under s.49(8) before any incriminating
question.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v McNiece (specimen procedure); DPP v Moorehouse (Court
testimony on driving); DPP v Finnegan (opinion grounds); DPP v Avadenei (interpreter rights); DPP
v Cabot (form filling).
STATUTE — Section 6, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Search incident to arrest (not an arrest power)
FRAMEWORK: Where a person is in custody, a Garda may search them and seize anything found
which may be evidence of the offence or any other offence, or which the Garda reasonably believes
the person intends to use to escape or to cause harm.
ELEMENTS: (a) Person in custody following arrest; (b) search by Garda; (c) seizure of evidence /
dangerous items / escape items.
POWERS / EFFECT: Search of person and items in their immediate possession (clothing, bag
carried). Seizure with continuity statement. Does NOT extend to vehicle, dwelling, or remote
premises — those require separate powers (warrant, plain view, statutory search powers).
TEST: Is the search incident to a lawful arrest? Is the item evidence, dangerous, or an escape risk?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence may challenge: (a) was the arrest lawful (if no, the
search is unlawful too)? (b) was the search proportionate (full strip search requires higher authority)?
(c) was continuity preserved from seizure to court?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], following arrest of the suspect under [power] for [offence],
I conducted a search of his person under s.6 CJA 1984. I located in his right jacket pocket [item]. I
seized the item, sealed it in tamper-evident exhibit bag [number], and signed across the seal at
[time]."

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Outer clothing, pockets, bag at outset. Same-sex searcher for
anything more than outer search. Strip search only on Member-in-Charge authorisation with specific
grounds. Intimate searches require Superintendent authorisation and medical attendance. Photograph
items in situ where significant before seizure.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Witness Garda present for search. Both initial. Property
log entries match. Continuity statements from each handler thereafter.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Searching without recording. (2) Strip search without proper
authorisation. (3) Cross-gender search beyond outer clothing. (4) Failing to bag and seal at scene —
leaving items loose risks contamination. (5) Returning items at end of custody without proper
accountability.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Fagan (scope of search incident to arrest); DPP v Forde (phones
— power to seize, not to browse); Article 40.3 (personal rights — privacy implications of intrusive
search).
STATUTE — Section 10, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Re-arrest where new evidence emerges
FRAMEWORK: Where a person was previously detained under s.4 and released, they may be rearrested for the same offence if a Garda has reasonable grounds for suspecting they have committed
the offence and there is new information available since release.
ELEMENTS: (a) Prior s.4 detention completed; (b) release; (c) new information emerged; (d) fresh
reasonable grounds; (e) Superintendent's authorisation for re-arrest.
POWERS / EFFECT: Re-arrest and fresh s.4 detention clock (24 hours from new arrest).
TEST: Genuinely new information — not a re-shuffle of facts already known. Forensic results,
witness statements, fresh CCTV recovery, surveillance products, all qualify.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will challenge whether the 'new information' is truly
new. If it was reasonably available during the first detention, the re-arrest is defective. Investigation
log / decision log must show the timeline of new material.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date 1], the suspect was detained under s.4 CJA 1984 and
released without charge. On [date 2], the following new information became available: [specifics —
DNA result returned from FSI, witness statement obtained, etc]. On the basis of this new
information, with Superintendent [name]'s authorisation, the suspect was re-arrested at [time] under
s.10 CJA 1999."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Document the chronology meticulously. Decision log entries
showing when new material was received. The Superintendent's authorisation should reference the
specific new material. Investigation officers should not rush re-arrest — better to have the new
material crystallised first.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Forensic chain of custody from sample collection to lab
to result, with date-stamps. Witness statement bearing date taken. Surveillance product with
deployment / retrieval dates.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating information as 'new' that was actually in the file all along but
not noticed. (2) Re-arrest as a tactic to reset the clock without genuine new material. (3) Inadequate
Superintendent briefing.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien (s.4 cap and re-arrest); s.4 CJA 1984.

1.3 Recurring Mid-Level Issues — How Arrest Goes Wrong
DPP v O'Brien [2005] 2 IR 206 (SC)
FACTS: Detention extended beyond 24 hours under s.4 CJA 1984 in circumstances where the basis
for extension was challenged. The Supreme Court examined the strict cap on s.4 detention.
PRINCIPLE: S.4 detention is strictly capped at 24 hours. Beyond this, the detention is unlawful
unless a separate power has been invoked (re-arrest under s.10 for new information; charge and
remand; release).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

RATIO: Statutory time limits on detention are absolute. Even moments past the cap render the
detention unlawful and evidence obtained in that period at risk.
TEST: At every moment of detention: am I within the statutory cap for the power invoked?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial will examine the custody record minute-by-minute. The
Member-in-Charge and investigating member must be able to defend each segment of the clock.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The suspect was arrested at [time on day 1]. He was charged at [time
on day 2], which was [hours/minutes] within the 24-hour cap. Throughout the detention, the clock
was suspended for the following periods: solicitor consultation [time range], medical attention [time
range], rest period [time range] — totalling [x] hours of suspension. Net detention was within the
cap."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Plan backwards from the cap. If arrest at 14:00 Friday, charge or
release by 14:00 Saturday net of suspensions. Build buffer — don't run to the wire. Where complex
investigation requires more time, consider s.10 re-arrest with new material rather than pushing the
s.4 clock.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Member-in-Charge custody record entries every change
in status. Solicitor visit logs. Doctor's note. Rest periods recorded with bedding-down and waking
times.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Losing track of cumulative time across suspensions. (2) Late
extensions retrospectively backdated. (3) Failure to record clock suspensions properly. (4) Charging
marginally after the cap.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Finn (extension grounds); DPP v Boylan (suspension rules); s.4
CJA 1984; s.10 CJA 1999.
DPP v Finn [2003] 1 IR 372 (SC)
FACTS: Detention extension under s.4 CJA 1984 challenged on grounds that the Superintendent
did not personally apply mind to the necessity for extension. The Supreme Court considered the
standards for extension decisions.
PRINCIPLE: Extensions of detention must be based on the named rank's personal application of
mind to specific grounds. Rubber-stamping is not lawful. The rank must consider whether detention
remains necessary for proper investigation and articulate the investigative steps still needed.
RATIO: Each extension is a fresh decision, not a continuation of the original. The decision must be
reasoned and defensible.
TEST: Did the named rank personally consider the case and articulate grounds for extension? Are
those grounds defensible — concrete investigative steps requiring continued detention?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Counsel will cross-examine the Superintendent / Chief
Superintendent on their decision. 'What investigative steps did you consider remained to be done?'
'Why couldn't they be done without detention?' The decision-maker should be able to answer
specifically.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], I attended Superintendent [name] in his office. I briefed
him on the investigation status as follows: [interviews to date, evidence under analysis, witness
statements awaited, forensic processing, etc]. I outlined the further investigation required: [planned
interviews, awaited results, etc]. He considered the matter and, satisfied that further detention was
necessary for proper investigation, signed a direction extending detention for [period]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Brief the rank with specifics. Don't ask for 'another 6 hours
because we're still going'. Tell them: 'We need to put the CCTV to him, the FSI test result is due in
4 hours, the second victim statement just came in.' Make the necessity concrete. The rank's direction
should reflect this — even brief reasons in writing protect the case.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record entry by Member-in-Charge of extension
granted, with time. Superintendent's written direction with grounds. Detective Sergeant /
investigating member's briefing note.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Briefing a rank by phone without records. (2) Direction signed without

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

genuine consideration. (3) Direction worded so generically it could apply to any case. (4) Rank
cannot recall the grounds at trial.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien (24-hour cap); DPP v Boylan (suspensions); s.4 CJA
1984.
DPP v Boylan [1991] 1 IR 477 (SC)
FACTS: The Supreme Court considered the rules governing suspension of the s.4 detention clock
— for solicitor consultations, medical attention, rest periods, food.
PRINCIPLE: The detention clock suspends for: (a) solicitor consultation, (b) medical attention, (c)
rest period of at least 8 hours overnight, (d) other specified categories. Each suspension must be
recorded with start and end times. The cumulative suspension extends the wall-clock detention but
the 24-hour 'detention time' cap is preserved.
RATIO: Suspensions are protections for the suspect (sleep, legal advice, health), not productivity
tools for investigators. They suspend the clock but do not extend overall detention purpose.
TEST: Each suspension recorded? Suspect informed? Genuine activity (solicitor actually
consulting, suspect actually resting)?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will examine each suspension. Did the suspect actually
consult solicitor for 90 minutes or were they back in the cell? Did the suspect actually sleep for 8
hours or were they roused after 4? Records must be honest and detailed.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], the suspect's solicitor [name] arrived. Detention clock
suspended at [time]. Solicitor consulted with suspect privately in interview room 1 until [time].
Detention clock resumed at [time]. Total suspension: [duration]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Member-in-Charge logs every suspension contemporaneously.
The 8-hour rest period — typically overnight — must be genuine: lights dimmed, bedding provided,
no disturbance unless welfare. Solicitor visits separately timed. Medical attention — doctor's note
logged.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record is primary. Solicitor sign-in / sign-out.
Doctor's note. Cell check log every 30 minutes during rest period.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Pretending suspect rested when they were interviewed. (2) Recording
8 hours rest when actually 5. (3) Failing to log brief solicitor consultations.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien; DPP v Finn; Custody Regulations 1987.
Re Ó Laighléis [1960] IR 93
FACTS: Habeas corpus application challenging lawfulness of detention under emergency
legislation. The Supreme Court considered the relationship between detention powers and the
Constitution.
PRINCIPLE: Every detention is subject to constitutional review. The High Court may inquire into
the lawfulness of any detention and order release where the detention is found unlawful. Habeas
corpus is the foundational remedy under Article 40.4.2.
RATIO: Detention powers are not self-validating. Any person detained has the constitutional right
to immediate judicial inquiry into the lawfulness of that detention.
TEST: Could a habeas corpus inquiry find the detention unlawful — defective power, no grounds,
expired clock, no authorisation?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Habeas corpus applications are rare but devastating when they
succeed. They typically arise where solicitors believe the s.4 clock has expired, or the power invoked
doesn't apply, or constitutional rights have been breached. Garda evidence must justify the detention
to the High Court within hours.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: (In habeas corpus context) "The suspect was arrested at [time] under
[power] on grounds of [grounds]. The Member-in-Charge authorised detention at [time]. Each
extension was authorised by [rank] at [time] for [reason]. Solicitor was contacted at [time] and
consulted at [time]. The current detention is authorised under [direction] until [time]."

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If habeas corpus is threatened, the file must be presentable in
minutes. Custody record, authorisations, briefings — all to hand. Where the foundation is weak,
charge or release before the application is heard.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Every authorising rank traceable, every direction in
writing, custody record contemporaneous.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating habeas corpus threats as bluster. (2) Loose detention practices
in a long-running operation. (3) Failure to anticipate the procedural posture.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.4.2 Bunreacht; State (Trimbole) v Governor of Mountjoy.
State (Trimbole) v Governor of Mountjoy [1985] IR 550 (SC)
FACTS: Trimbole was detained pending extradition in circumstances where the underlying arrest
was found to involve abuse of process. The Supreme Court considered whether the detention could
continue.
PRINCIPLE: Where the initial arrest is found to involve abuse of process — particularly conscious
and deliberate breach of constitutional rights — subsequent detention may be unlawful even if the
formal procedures are followed thereafter.
RATIO: Procedural compliance after the fact does not cure foundational unlawfulness in the initial
arrest. The whole chain is rotten if the first link is.
TEST: Was the initial arrest lawful in substance, not just form?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where defence can show that an apparently formal arrest in fact
masked another purpose — e.g. holding for questioning on unrelated matter, or arrest by pretext —
the court may find abuse and exclude subsequent evidence.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: Trial may require Gardaí to explain not just the formal grounds but
the genuine investigative purpose. Honest articulation protects the case; evasion damages it.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Arrest for what you actually suspect, not as cover for something
else. If you arrest for s.49 RTA to get a chance to question on drugs, the drug evidence is at risk.
Charge or be honest about what you're doing.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Pretextual arrests. (2) Using minor charges to detain for major
investigation without solid s.4 / s.30 grounds. (3) Failure to investigate the offence of arrest while
focusing on something else.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v JC (constitutional breach test); Article 40.4.1; State (Quinn) v
Ryan.
State (Quinn) v Ryan [1965] IR 70
FACTS: Procedural abuse in handling of an extradition / arrest situation. The Supreme Court took
a strong stance on Garda compliance with constitutional and procedural obligations.
PRINCIPLE: Conscious and deliberate breach of constitutional rights by Gardaí is treated as
serious abuse of process. Such breaches may result in release of the suspect and exclusion of
evidence.
RATIO: The State expects high standards of Garda compliance. Bad faith or deliberate breach is
not viewed as mere procedural slip.
TEST: Was the breach deliberate, conscious, or in bad faith?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Honesty about mistakes protects the case. Concealment makes
a slip into bad faith.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If something went wrong, note it honestly. 'At [time] I realised I
had not administered the caution at the doorway. I administered it immediately upon recognition.'
This is recoverable; concealment is not.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Covering up mistakes — defence will find them. (2) Tidying
notebooks retrospectively. (3) Inconsistent statements between members.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v JC; State (Trimbole); Article 40.3.
DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 (SC, 7 judges)
FACTS: Evidence obtained pursuant to a search warrant later found defective — the warrant issuer's
authority was unconstitutional, but the Gardaí relied on it in good faith without knowledge of the
defect. The Supreme Court reconsidered the exclusionary rule established in Kenny.
PRINCIPLE: Evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights is presumptively inadmissible,
but the prosecution may seek to overcome the presumption by establishing that the breach was
inadvertent — the Garda acted in good faith and the breach was not the product of conscious and
deliberate disregard for the suspect's rights. Deliberate or reckless breaches still result in exclusion.
RATIO: Inadvertent breach + good faith = potential admission. Deliberate / reckless breach =
exclusion. The onus of establishing inadvertence is on the prosecution.
TEST: Was the breach (a) inadvertent (not realised at the time), (b) in good faith (no recklessness,
no conscious disregard), (c) of a kind that does not undermine the fairness of the proceedings overall?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where evidence is challenged on JC grounds, the trial will
explore the state of knowledge of the Gardaí at the relevant time. Did they know the warrant was
defective? Did they check? Were they on notice? Court considers training, briefings, supervisor
consultation, time pressure, and whether the breach is a 'reasonable person could have made it' kind
of mistake.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I executed the warrant at [time]. I had checked the warrant against
the standard checklist before execution. I noted that the issuing officer's rank and signature appeared
regular. I had no reason at the time to believe the warrant was defective in any respect. I was unaware
of [the defect] until [time], after the evidence had been seized."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: JC is a safety net, not a strategy. Always proceed as if the strict
rule applies — check warrants, name powers, document grounds. Where you discover after the fact
that something was defective, disclose it honestly and let the JC analysis proceed. Cover-up converts
inadvertence into bad faith and you lose the protection.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Notebook entries showing checks made before execution.
Briefing notes. Supervisor consultations. Any concerns raised and how addressed.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Relying on JC as cover for sloppy practice. (2) Concealing discovered
defects. (3) Failure to articulate the steps taken to check lawfulness at the time.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Kenny (overruled, but background); Damache v DPP (defective
warrant context); People (DPP) v Quirke (good faith test refinement).

1.4 Caution Mechanics — Beyond the Basic Wording
DPP v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 (SC)
FACTS: Breaches of the Custody Regulations 1987 during a s.4 detention; statement challenged.
The Supreme Court considered the consequences of regulatory breach.
PRINCIPLE: Custody Regulations breaches do not automatically render statements inadmissible.
Court has discretion considering: (a) nature and gravity of breach, (b) whether deliberate /
inadvertent, (c) effect on the suspect, (d) effect on reliability of evidence, (e) whether the suspect's
rights were genuinely compromised. Regulatory breach is a factor, not a determinant.
RATIO: Procedural compliance under the Regulations is important but the system tolerates minor
inadvertent breach where the substance of fairness is preserved.
TEST: Was the breach deliberate or inadvertent? Did it affect reliability or fairness? Did it cause
real disadvantage to the suspect?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where defence raises breach of Custody Regs, the prosecution
must take it head-on. Member-in-Charge explains the breach, its discovery, its correction. The
custody record discipline is the foundation.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I acknowledge that at [time] the suspect's meal was not provided

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

within the 3-hour interval prescribed by the Regulations. This was due to [reason]. As soon as the
omission was noted at [time], the meal was provided. The suspect indicated he was not in distress
and did not raise complaint. No interview was conducted during the delay period."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Custody Regs are operational law. Print and laminate the key
timings: meal intervals (3 hours), sleep (8 hours uninterrupted in 24), exercise where practicable,
medical attention on request or as needed, solicitor access on request, family notification on request,
interpretation if needed. Log everything contemporaneously.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record is primary. Member-in-Charge entries are
the line of defence.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Skipping a meal in the heat of a fast-moving investigation. (2) Not
letting suspect sleep when they need to. (3) Failing to log breaches and corrections honestly. (4)
Treating Custody Regs as bureaucratic rather than substantive.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Custody Regulations 1987; DPP v Bowes & McGrath; DPP v
Onumwere.
DPP v Bowes & McGrath [2004] 4 IR 223 (CCA)
FACTS: Multiple Custody Regulations breaches in a long detention. Court considered the
cumulative effect and the relevance of bona fides.
PRINCIPLE: The Court of Criminal Appeal distinguished bona fide procedural slips from
deliberate or reckless breaches. Bad faith breaches treated significantly more severely. Inadvertent
breach noticed and addressed less serious. The custody record must reflect the truth — concealed
breach is treated as deliberate.
RATIO: Honesty in the custody record is a key factor in defending breaches. Concealment converts
a slip into a serious breach.
TEST: Was the breach noted, addressed, and openly recorded?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where breaches are honestly disclosed in the custody record,
the court is far more likely to forgive. Where defence finds the breach despite efforts to conceal, the
case is damaged disproportionately.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] I recognised that I had not actioned the suspect's solicitor
request within the period typically observed. I immediately contacted [solicitor] and recorded the
discrepancy in the custody record. No interview was conducted in the intervening period. The
suspect was informed of the delay and consulted the solicitor at [time]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Custody record is your friend, not your enemy. Tidy honest entries
always; backfilled tidy entries are easily exposed. Even-handed treatment of breaches: 'At 14:32 I
noticed X had not been done. At 14:34 it was actioned.' Done. Move on.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Independent custody record entries. Time stamps
consistent with other records (PULSE, station log).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Tidying the custody record at end of shift. (2) Failing to note brief
delays. (3) Inconsistencies between custody record and interviewing member's notes.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Buck; Custody Regulations 1987.
DPP v Onumwere [2007] 3 IR 396 (CCA)
FACTS: Suspect with limited English questioned without interpreter; statement challenged on
grounds of inadequate understanding.
PRINCIPLE: Where a suspect's command of English is insufficient to understand the caution, the
rights, or the questioning, an interpreter must be provided. Statements taken without adequate
language support are at risk of exclusion.
RATIO: Voluntariness and the right against self-incrimination require genuine understanding.
Language barriers compromise both.
TEST: Did the suspect actually understand the caution, the rights, and the questions? Was an

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

interpreter required and provided?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where the suspect's language is non-English-first, court will
examine: (a) how was language assessed at intake? (b) was an interpreter offered? (c) what level of
fluency did the interviewing member assess? (d) is there documentation of the assessment?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On intake, I assessed the suspect's command of English by asking
general questions in English. He responded fluently in [matters]. He stated his preferred language
was [language] and accepted my offer of an interpreter. Interpreter [name] arrived at [time] and was
present for all subsequent interview and procedural steps."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city Dublin: diverse population means language issues
frequent. Asking 'do you understand English?' is not enough — the answer is usually 'yes' even when
not. Assess by reciprocal conversation: ask for personal details in English; if hesitation or errors,
offer interpreter. PULSE flag for interpreter required. Garda Síochána interpreter service has lists.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Interpreter's details logged. Time of arrival. Interpreter
signs statement they translated. Translation accuracy attested.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Cursory language assessment. (2) Using bilingual family members as
interpreters (compromises objectivity). (3) Using other suspects or co-arrested. (4) Interpreter not
present for full interview. (5) Caution administered in English with no interpretation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 6 ECHR (interpreter rights); EU Directive 2010/64; DPP v
Avadenei (RTA interpretation).
DPP v Avadenei [2017] IESC 77 (SC)
FACTS: Suspect in RTA matter not provided with interpretation of the specimen procedure.
Statement of specimen result challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Interpretation rights extend to the specimen procedure in RTA matters where the
suspect's English is insufficient. Failure to provide may compromise the prosecution.
RATIO: Procedural fairness in technical procedures (RTA specimen, fingerprinting, sampling)
requires understanding equivalent to other custody matters.
TEST: Did the suspect understand the procedure, the demand, the consequences of refusal?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: RTA cases often turn on the specimen procedure. Where
language is in issue, every element of the demand and procedure must be shown to have been
understood — typically via interpreter.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: RTA specimen: standard demand wording, with interpreter where
needed. Designated doctor for blood / urine has interpreter access. 20-minute observation maintained
throughout interpretation.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Assuming RTA procedure is exempt from interpretation rights. (2)
Demand wording translated approximately rather than verbatim.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Onumwere; s.12 RTA 2010; EU Directive 2010/64.
Gormley & White v DPP [2014] IESC 17 (SC)
FACTS: Suspects interviewed before consulting solicitor; admissions made. The Supreme Court
considered whether the right to legal advice requires solicitor consultation before interview.
PRINCIPLE: There is a constitutional right of reasonable access to legal advice for a person in
custody. Where the suspect has requested a solicitor, no questioning may occur until the solicitor
has been consulted (or until reasonable efforts to make the solicitor available have been exhausted
and the suspect waives the right).
RATIO: Solicitor access is a constituent of fair process — investigators must wait, not press ahead.
TEST: Did the suspect request a solicitor? If so, has reasonable access been provided before
substantive questioning?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where solicitor consultation was not provided before interview,
defence will challenge admissibility of resulting admissions. Records must show: time of request,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

efforts to contact solicitor, time of consultation, time of subsequent interview.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], the suspect was informed of his right to consult a solicitor.
He requested [solicitor name]. At [time], I contacted the solicitor's office and arranged attendance.
At [time], the solicitor attended and consulted the suspect privately. At [time], following the
consultation, I commenced the interview after re-administering the caution."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Solicitor request triggers a hard stop on substantive questioning.
Continue with administrative matters (custody record, photographs, fingerprints) but no interview.
Member-in-Charge supports the solicitor contact process. Where solicitor unavailable, document
efforts — calls made, alternative solicitors offered.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record entries with times. Solicitor sign-in.
Member-in-Charge contemporaneous notes.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Interviewing 'briefly' before solicitor arrives. (2) Treating 'general
questions' as not amounting to interview when they elicit incriminating responses. (3) Pressuring
suspect to waive solicitor.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Doyle [2017] (solicitor presence vs consultation); Murray v UK;
Article 38.1; Article 40.3.
DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 (SC, 7 judges)
FACTS: Suspect interviewed without solicitor present (although solicitor had been consulted
before). Defence argued for right to have solicitor present throughout interview.
PRINCIPLE: The Supreme Court clarified that the constitutional right is to reasonable access to
legal advice (consultation) — not an absolute right to have a solicitor present throughout interview.
Practice has converged on facilitating presence, but its absence is not automatic breach.
RATIO: Reasonable access = consultation. Presence during interview = practice (good practice) but
not constitutional necessity.
TEST: Was reasonable access (consultation) afforded? If presence was refused, was the refusal
documented and reasoned?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Current practice facilitates solicitor presence in nearly all cases.
If you refused presence, you must justify it. Brief reasons in custody record protect the case.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The suspect was offered solicitor consultation and consulted [name]
at [time]. The solicitor sought to attend during interview. I [permitted / declined for the following
reasons: ...]. The suspect was reminded that solicitor consultation could be sought at any time."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: In practice: facilitate presence unless there is a specific
operational reason. Brief the solicitor on procedure (your role, theirs, their right to intervene on legal
matters but not on factual responses). Where mid-interview consultation needed — pause recording,
allow consultation, resume on record.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Recording captures the solicitor presence / absence
transparency. Custody record entries on consultation timing.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Refusing presence without articulable reason. (2) Allowing solicitor
to take over the interview. (3) Failing to allow mid-interview consultation when sought.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Gormley & White v DPP; Murray v UK; ECHR Article 6.
Murray v UK (1996) 22 EHRR 29 (ECtHR)
