FACTS: Inferences drawn at trial from suspect's silence under the Northern Ireland equivalent
provisions. The ECtHR considered compatibility with the right to a fair trial under Article 6 ECHR.
PRINCIPLE: Inferences from silence are compatible with Article 6 ECHR provided: (a) the suspect
had access to a solicitor before being subjected to the inference regime, (b) the court directs itself or
the jury appropriately on the dangers of relying solely on silence, (c) other evidence supports the
inference, (d) the trial as a whole is fair.
RATIO: Inference regimes are permissible with safeguards. Solicitor access is the gateway

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

safeguard.
TEST: Were all the safeguards in place at the time of the inference warning?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where inference warnings have been deployed, defence will
examine: (a) was solicitor consulted before the warning? (b) was the warning correctly delivered?
(c) does other evidence support what an inference would suggest? (d) was the trial direction
adequate?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inference warnings (ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984) without solicitor
consultation are wasted — the inference is unavailable. Always: solicitor first, then warning, then
specific question. Document the sequence meticulously.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Deploying inference warning before solicitor consulted. (2) Failing to
document the specific question / response.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Heaney v Ireland; DPP v Connolly (inference warnings); Gormley &
White; ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984.
DPP v Connolly [2003] 2 IR 1 (CCA)
FACTS: Inferences drawn at trial from the suspect's refusal to answer specific questions during
interview. The procedure for the inference warning was scrutinised.
PRINCIPLE: Strict compliance with the statutory warning procedure under ss.18/19/19A CJA
1984 is required. The conditions are: (a) solicitor consulted (or genuine waiver), (b) warning given
in prescribed form (different wording for each section), (c) suspect afforded reasonable opportunity
to understand, (d) specific question put on the specific matter (item / mark / place / fact). Any failure
invalidates the inference.
RATIO: Inference provisions impinge on the right to silence; strict compliance is the constitutional
safeguard.
TEST: Were ALL conditions satisfied for the specific inference sought? Each is necessary; one
failure removes the inference.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial judge will examine the interview record for compliance
with each step. Where any element is missing, the inference is unavailable and the underlying silence
becomes neutral.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], following solicitor consultation completed at [time], I
administered the warning under section [18/19/19A] of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 in the
prescribed terms: '[verbatim from the station form]'. I asked the suspect 'Do you understand?' He
replied [verbatim]. I offered further solicitor consultation. He [accepted / declined]. I then asked:
'[specific question]'. The suspect responded: '[verbatim — or 'remained silent']'."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Get the station's verbatim warning form. Read from it. Do not
paraphrase. The form contains the magic words; deviation loses the inference. Sequence: solicitor
→ warning → specific question → response noted verbatim → re-caution → continue.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Recording captures the warning. Notebook of
interviewing member contains the exact words used.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Paraphrasing the warning. (2) Wrong section warning (s.18 for a
presence question; s.19 for a physical evidence question). (3) Not pausing for understanding. (4)
Vague rather than specific question.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Murray v UK; ss.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984; Heaney v Ireland; DPP v Bowes
& McGrath.
Heaney v Ireland [1996] 1 IR 580 (SC); (1995) ECtHR
FACTS: Constitutional challenge to s.52 OASA 1939 (compelled-answer provision during s.30
detention). The Supreme Court upheld the section as proportionate; the ECtHR subsequently found
a breach of Article 6 ECHR.
PRINCIPLE: Restrictions on the right to silence must be proportionate. The Heaney proportionality
test: (a) rational connection between restriction and objective, (b) restriction impairs the right as little

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

as possible, (c) effect on the right is proportionate to the objective. Where this is not met, the
restriction is invalid. The ECtHR concluded that s.52 OASA, as it then operated, failed Article 6.
RATIO: Compulsion to answer in detention must satisfy strict proportionality. The Irish judicial
position recognises this; ECtHR has set a high bar.
TEST: Is the restriction on silence proportionate — necessary, minimal, balanced?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: S.52 OASA prosecutions for refusal to answer in s.30 detention
are effectively not pursued post-Heaney ECtHR. The right to silence dominates unless a statutory
inference regime is properly invoked.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Don't expect to compel answers under s.52 OASA. The legitimate
pathways for adverse consequence from silence are the inference regimes (ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984)
with all safeguards in place.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Connolly; Murray v UK; ECHR Article 6; ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984.

1.5 Recurring Pitfalls — Snapshots from Real Cases
These short entries capture recurring mid-level mistakes that have surfaced in case law. Each can
sink a case — and each is preventable with discipline.
DPP v Cullen — Wrong section cited (illustrative composite)
FACTS: Arresting member cited s.30 OASA in the heat of the moment for an offence that was not
scheduled. The defective characterisation was challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Citing the wrong power at arrest can be fatal where the cited power does not in fact
authorise the arrest. Where a different power did authorise it, courts may sometimes excuse — but
the safest course is to cite correctly first time.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Know your powers. Don't say 'section 30' if you mean 'section 4'.
Standard arrestable offence — s.4 CJA 1984. Public order — s.30 CJ(PO)A 1994. Scheduled OASA
— s.30 OASA 1939. Drugs — typically s.4 CJA 1984 for underlying MDA offence. Drunk driving
— s.49(8) RTA 1961.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Reflexive 'section 30' for anything serious. (2) Failure to update
notebook with the correct citation when realised. (3) Inconsistency between arresting officer and
Member-in-Charge custody record.
Arrest by pretext — Recurring theme (line of authority from Trimbole onwards)
FACTS: Arrest for a minor matter where the actual investigative interest is elsewhere. Courts treat
this as abuse of process where it amounts to using one power to access another.
PRINCIPLE: Arrest must be for the offence cited, with genuine investigative purpose. Pretextual
arrest — minor charge as cover for major investigation — is at risk of being found an abuse.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If you suspect involvement in serious matter X, get the grounds
for X and arrest for X. Don't arrest for s.49 RTA in the hope of getting at drugs evidence. If the drugs
evidence emerges during a genuine RTA stop, that's plain view and proper. If the stop was a pretext,
plain view evidence is at risk.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Disclosing internally that the arrest was a 'way in'. (2)
Disproportionate investigative activity on the minor charge. (3) Failure to actually pursue the offence
of arrest.
Failure to communicate ground of arrest (Christie v Leachinsky line)
FACTS: Recurring scenario: suspect arrested, taken to station, only at custody is the offence clearly
articulated.
PRINCIPLE: Reason must be communicated at the moment of arrest, in plain language, unless
circumstances make this impossible. Late articulation is curable in some cases but is always a
defence point.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Three things at arrest: who you are, what the offence is, the

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

caution. 'I am Garda X, Mountjoy. I am arresting you for [plain English offence]. You are not obliged
to say anything…'
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'You know what this is about'. (2) Legal jargon. (3) Mumbled or
unclear. (4) Different version stated to suspect vs to Member-in-Charge.
Detention clock failures (O'Brien, Finn, Boylan line)
FACTS: Recurring scenarios: extensions late; suspensions not properly recorded; cap exceeded.
PRINCIPLE: Every minute counted, every extension on time, every suspension logged with start
and end.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Member-in-Charge watch is the case. Detention plan written from
arrest forward. Interviews scheduled within cap. Buffer maintained.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'I'll get the Super to sign it in a minute' — minute becomes 30 minutes,
extension is retrospective. (2) Suspect rests for 4 hours, recorded as 8. (3) Solicitor consult for 90
minutes, not logged as suspension.
End of Volume 1, Part 1: Arrest. Search and Detention follow in the next sections. Cross-references
will tie the whole reference together; for now, treat each case as a complete picture in itself, ready
to be deployed in interview prep, court testimony preparation, or supervisor briefing.

2. Search, Entry & Warrants
Article 40.5 protects the dwelling — the highest constitutional protection in policing. Every search needs a
named power. The mid-level Garda must know which power applies in which situation and defend the choice
on the stand.
Search is the next gateway. Search of person, vehicle, premises and dwelling all engage different constitutional
protections — most fundamentally Article 40.5 (inviolability of the dwelling). Every search needs a named
power: warrant, statutory power, consent, hot pursuit, or specific emergency authority. The mid-level Garda
must be fluent in which power applies in which situation, and how to defend the choice in court.

2.1 Article 40.5 and the Constitutional Foundation
DPP v Dunne [1994] 2 IR 537 (SC)
FACTS: Search warrant executed in circumstances where the warrant's validity on its face was
challenged. The Supreme Court considered the constitutional importance of the warrant procedure.
PRINCIPLE: A search warrant is a serious interference with constitutional rights. The warrant must
be valid on its face — properly issued by a person with authority, properly identifying the premises,
the offence, the date, and signed. Defects on the face of the warrant compromise its validity and
expose evidence to exclusion.
RATIO: The warrant document itself is constitutionally significant. It is not a formality but the legal
authority for the intrusion.
TEST: Is the warrant valid on its face? Does it correctly identify (a) the issuer (with authority), (b)
the premises (specifically), (c) the offence (specifically), (d) the date and validity period, (e) the
executing member or 'any member', (f) the signature?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will obtain a copy of the warrant and scrutinise it.
Missing detail, misspelled address, wrong offence cited, expired date — all are admissibility points.
Where the warrant has been amended or annotated, every change must be explicable.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "Before executing the warrant, I read it carefully. I confirmed: the
issuer was [name and rank/role], who had jurisdiction; the premises were correctly identified as
[address]; the offence specified was [offence]; the warrant was within its 7-day validity period; and
the warrant was signed. I retained the warrant during execution and presented it on demand."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Before crossing the threshold: read the warrant. Cross-check the
address with what you can see (signage, number). If there's a discrepancy (e.g. building has changed
name), pause and consider whether you have the right premises. Carry the warrant — the

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

householder is entitled to demand sight (s.4 of the relevant warrant Acts typically requires you to
show it). Photograph the warrant before / after execution for the file.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Original warrant on file. Photocopy or photograph in case
file. Member executing carries it. Member-in-Charge retains record of warrant.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Executing without reading. (2) Wrong address — typo in warrant or
wrong building. (3) Expired warrant — many statutory warrants 7 days. (4) Warrant for premises A
but executed at premises B because that's where the suspect went. (5) No copy in case file postexecution.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Damache v DPP (independent issuer); People (DPP) v Mallon (warrant
validity); Article 40.5; s.10 CJ(MP)A 1997 (warrant standard form provisions).
Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11 (SC)
FACTS: Search warrant under s.29 OASA 1939 issued by a Garda Superintendent who was a
member of the investigating force. The Supreme Court considered the constitutional requirement of
independence in the warrant issuer.
PRINCIPLE: Search warrants permitting entry to a dwelling must be issued by an authority
independent of the investigation. A senior officer of the investigating force lacks the requisite
independence. S.29 OASA, as it then stood, violated the separation between investigator and
authoriser. Legislative amendment followed, transferring the issuing authority to the District Judge.
RATIO: Independence of warrant issuer is a constitutional requirement for dwelling-search
warrants. The court takes Article 40.5 (inviolability of dwelling) seriously.
TEST: Was the warrant issuer independent of the investigation? For dwellings, this typically means
a District Judge.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where a warrant for a dwelling was issued by an internal
authority, defence will challenge under Damache. The evidence obtained is at high risk of exclusion
unless the JC saving applies (and the Damache point was widely known after 2012, so 'inadvertent'
will be hard to establish for subsequent operations).
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The warrant was applied for on [date] before [issuer — typically
District Judge name]. I gave evidence on oath of the grounds. The warrant was issued at [time] and
was executed within its 7-day validity period."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: For dwelling searches, default position is District Judge warrant.
Specific statutory exceptions exist (some Inspector-issued warrants in narrow circumstances) but for
OASA / serious offences / general searches, go to the DJ. Have your information / sworn evidence
ready: grounds, premises, what is sought, why this premises.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: DJ's records, sworn information document, executed
warrant — all on file.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Using a Superintendent issuer post-Damache. (2) Inadequate sworn
information. (3) Application made by a member not directly involved in investigation (better to send
the actual investigator).
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.5; DPP v Dunne (warrant face validity); People (DPP) v JC
(constitutional breach test); s.29 OASA 1939 (as amended post-Damache).
DPP v Mallon [2011] IECCA 29
FACTS: Search warrant defective in execution; question of whether technical defects rendered the
search unlawful.
PRINCIPLE: Not every technical defect in a warrant or its execution renders the search unlawful.
Court considers nature of defect, materiality, effect on suspect's rights. Substantial compliance with
statutory requirements may preserve admissibility; gross deviation will not.
RATIO: Substance over form for minor defects; strict compliance for substantive requirements.
TEST: Is the defect material to the validity of the search and the protection of constitutional rights,
or is it a minor technical issue?

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges all defects; trial judge distinguishes
substantive from technical. The Garda's response should be that the substantive protections were
observed.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Don't rely on Mallon — strive for full compliance. Use it as a
safety net only.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating Mallon as licence for sloppy practice. (2) Multiple minor
defects accumulating to substantive concern.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Dunne; DPP v JC; Damache.
Article 40.5 Bunreacht na hÉireann (constitutional provision)
FACTS: The constitutional protection of the dwelling: 'The dwelling of every citizen is inviolable
and shall not be forcibly entered save in accordance with law.'
PRINCIPLE: The dwelling enjoys the highest level of constitutional protection. Forcible entry
requires specific legal authority — warrant, statutory power expressly applicable to dwelling,
consent, hot pursuit, or genuine emergency (life-saving). Entry without authority is breach of Article
40.5.
RATIO: The dwelling is constitutional sanctuary; powers of entry are exceptions specifically
authorised by law.
TEST: Is there a specific legal authority for this entry? Name it before crossing the threshold.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where defence shows entry without lawful authority, all
evidence flowing from the entry is at constitutional risk. JC analysis (inadvertent / good faith) may
help — but Article 40.5 is a high-stakes protection.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I entered the dwelling at [address] at [time]. The legal authority for
entry was [specific authority — warrant under [Act]; consent given by [person]; hot pursuit of
suspect from [scene]; emergency to save life of [person]; statutory power under [section] which
expressly authorises dwelling entry]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Dwelling = where someone resides. Includes flats, houses, mobile
homes, room within a shared house. NOT — common areas of apartment block (hallway, foyer,
stairwell), gardens beyond the curtilage in some interpretations, business premises (unless a dwelling
is integral). When in doubt — get the warrant or get consent (in writing where possible).
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: All entries logged with authority cited.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating a flat block hallway as dwelling (it's not — but the individual
flat is). (2) Crossing from public area into curtilage without authority. (3) Following suspect into
dwelling without hot pursuit basis. (4) 'Welfare check' as cover for investigative entry.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Damache; DPP v Dunne; DPP v Lavery (consent searches); Criminal
Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011.

2.2 Statutory Search Powers
STATUTE — Section 29, Offences Against the State Act 1939 (as amended) Search warrant for OASA
offences
FRAMEWORK: Application to District Judge (post-Damache). On sworn information of a
member of the Garda Síochána, the DJ may issue a warrant authorising entry to specified premises
and search for specified articles in connection with an offence under the Act.
ELEMENTS: (a) Sworn information; (b) DJ satisfied of reasonable grounds; (c) specified premises;
(d) specified offence; (e) specified articles or evidence sought.
POWERS / EFFECT: Entry, search, seizure. May force entry if necessary. Authority extends to
dwelling. 7-day validity typical.
TEST: DJ satisfied of reasonable grounds on sworn information.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: DJ records of issue, sworn information on file, executed warrant
returned with endorsement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date], I applied to District Judge [name] for a warrant under s.29
OASA 1939 (as amended). I gave evidence on oath of the following grounds: [specifics]. The
warrant was issued at [time]. I executed it on [date] at [time]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Application prepared properly — written information with full
grounds, sworn before DJ. Don't rush — the application is the case foundation. Once issued, execute
within validity, properly recorded.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Information thin on grounds. (2) Premises vague. (3) Articles sought
generic ('any items of evidential value' — too broad).
CROSS-REFERENCES: Damache; DPP v Dunne; Article 40.5.
STATUTE — Section 26, Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 Search warrant for drugs
FRAMEWORK: Application to District Judge on sworn information of a member of An Garda
Síochána. DJ may issue warrant authorising specified member, accompanied by other members as
necessary, to enter and search premises and persons found therein, and to seize drugs and related
items.
ELEMENTS: (a) Sworn information; (b) DJ satisfied of reasonable grounds to suspect (i) drugs
offence on premises or (ii) drugs on premises; (c) specified premises.
POWERS / EFFECT: Entry (force if necessary); search of premises; search of persons on premises;
seizure of drugs, equipment, money, documents; arrest of persons reasonably suspected.
TEST: Reasonable grounds for suspicion of drugs offence at the premises.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Drugs warrants are the most common warrant type for midlevel Gardaí. Defence will scrutinise: grounds, premises specificity, scope of search, plain view
items seized, persons searched without specific grounds.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date] I applied to District Judge [name] for a search warrant
under s.26 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977. I gave evidence on oath of the following intelligence
and observations giving rise to reasonable grounds: [specifics — surveillance observations,
informant intelligence (without identifying source — 'confidential information received'), seizures
from associates, etc]. The warrant was issued for premises at [address]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Information thoroughness wins. List: dates of surveillance with
specific observations; intelligence basis (without burning the source); associated arrests; physical
patterns. Specify the premises down to the flat number. Persons on premises: searchable under the
warrant — name them in evidence at scene.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Sworn information, warrant, executed warrant return,
PEMS exhibits, seizure log.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Generic grounds ('I have received information'). (2) Premises
ambiguous (multi-unit building, no specific flat). (3) Searching persons not on the premises. (4)
Items seized beyond scope without plain view basis.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Lavery (consent / scope); DPP v Heaphy (plain view); s.7 CJA
2006 (plain view); Article 40.5; Damache.
STATUTE — Section 10, Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1997 Standard warrant
provisions
FRAMEWORK: Standard form, presentation, validity, and execution provisions for statutory
search warrants. The warrant must be in standard form, may be issued for a maximum 7 days validity
(or as the statute specifies), must be presented on demand, may authorise force entry, and provides
for return after execution.
ELEMENTS: Standardisation provisions across warrant types.
POWERS / EFFECT: Procedural framework.
TEST: Was the warrant in standard form, executed within validity, presented on demand?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Compliance with standard provisions is foundational.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Warrant in physical form on execution. Photocopy / photograph
for file. Returned after execution per statutory requirement.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Executing without physical warrant on person. (2) Failure to return.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Dunne; specific statutory warrants.
STATUTE — Section 23, Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 Stop, search, detain — without warrant, drugs
FRAMEWORK: Where a member of the Garda Síochána has reasonable cause to suspect that a
person is in possession of a controlled drug, the member may: (a) search the person; (b) require the
person to give name and address; (c) detain the person for the purpose of the search; (d) search any
vehicle, vessel, aircraft they suspect contains drugs or evidence.
ELEMENTS: (a) Reasonable cause to suspect; (b) drugs offence; (c) stop and / or search.
POWERS / EFFECT: Stop, search, detain for search, require name and address, search vehicle.
TEST: Articulable reasonable cause to suspect — specific facts, not hunch.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: S.23 stop-searches are heavily scrutinised. Defence challenges:
(a) what specifically gave rise to suspicion? (b) was the search proportionate? (c) was identification
required? (d) were items found correctly seized?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] at [location], I observed [specific behaviour — handover,
urgency, known dealing area, presence of associate known to me as a dealer, etc]. On the basis of
what I observed, I had reasonable cause to suspect [name] was in possession of controlled drugs. I
identified myself and stated I was searching under s.23 MDA 1977. I conducted a search of his outer
clothing and pockets. In his [pocket] I located [substance / item]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Articulation is everything. Specific facts, contemporaneous
notebook entry, witness Garda. Don't search on 'gut feeling' you can't put into words. Inner-city
Dublin patrol: known dealing locations, time of day, specific behaviours (handover, swift exchanges,
lookout posture) — all articulable. PULSE entry per stop-search standard.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Stop-search form per current practice. Notebook entry.
Witness Garda statement. CCTV if available.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'He looked suspicious' — defence destroys this. (2) Targeting based
on association without specific grounds. (3) Search beyond outer clothing without higher grounds.
(4) Failing to note name and address.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Reilly (s.23 grounds); s.8 CJA 2006 (modernised stop-search);
People (DPP) v Mallon.
STATUTE — Section 30, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Stop and search — public order
FRAMEWORK: Member may stop and search a person whom they reasonably suspect of carrying
or being in possession of an item with intent to use it as a weapon, or a stolen / unlawfully obtained
item.
ELEMENTS: (a) Reasonable suspicion; (b) item used as weapon or unlawfully obtained.
POWERS / EFFECT: Stop, search, seize.
TEST: Articulable reasonable suspicion.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Similar scrutiny to s.23 MDA — defence focuses on grounds
and scope.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I observed the suspect in [circumstances]. I had reasonable suspicion
he was carrying a weapon based on [specific facts]. I stopped him under s.30 CJ(PO)A 1994 and
conducted a search. I located [item]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Useful where weapons suspected but no specific drugs nexus. Bag
of someone leaving a fight scene; cyclist late at night with bag and bolt cutters; etc.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Confusing with s.23 MDA — wrong power cited. (2) Insufficient
grounds.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Reilly; s.9 FOWA 1990 (knives offences).
STATUTE — Section 8, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Search of person — modernised stop-search
FRAMEWORK: Member may stop, search, and detain a person where reasonable suspicion they
have committed, are committing, or are about to commit an arrestable offence and have items
relevant to it.
ELEMENTS: (a) Reasonable suspicion; (b) arrestable offence; (c) relevant items in possession.
POWERS / EFFECT: Stop, search, seize.
TEST: Reasonable suspicion of arrestable offence and item connection.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Modernised provision; standard reasonable suspicion analysis.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Broader than s.23 / s.30 — covers any arrestable offence. Use
where the offence suspected is not drugs or public order. E.g. suspicion of theft from a person.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Failure to identify the underlying arrestable offence. (2) Vague
suspicion.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.23 MDA; s.30 CJ(PO)A; DPP v O'Reilly.
STATUTE — Section 6, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Search incident to arrest
FRAMEWORK: (Already covered in Arrest section.) Power to search person and immediate
possessions following arrest.
CROSS-REFERENCES: See Arrest section, s.6 CJA 1984 entry.
STATUTE — Section 7, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Plain view seizure during lawful search
FRAMEWORK: Where a member is lawfully on premises (under warrant, statutory power,
consent, or other lawful authority) and discovers in plain view an item that is evidence of an
arrestable offence, the member may seize and retain it even though it falls outside the scope of the
original warrant or power.
ELEMENTS: (a) Lawful presence; (b) item discovered in plain view; (c) reasonable belief item is
evidence of arrestable offence; (d) accessibility.
POWERS / EFFECT: Seize and retain.
TEST: Lawful presence + plain view + obvious evidential character + accessible.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where seizure was outside the original warrant scope, defence
will examine whether plain view conditions are made out. Was the search progressing within the
scope of the warrant when the item was discovered? Was the item obviously evidential or did the
Garda have to investigate to recognise it?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I was searching the premises under warrant for drugs. In the course
of searching [room] for drugs, I observed in plain view on [location] [item]. Based on my training
and experience, I immediately recognised this as [evidential character — e.g. a sawn-off shotgun, a
stolen item, a balaclava with apparent blood]. I seized it under s.7 CJA 2006, photographed it in situ,
and sealed it as exhibit [number]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Plain view doctrine is your friend during drugs searches —
firearms, stolen property, stash of cash all often appear. Photograph in situ. Note exact location.
Don't move it before photograph. Continue search if appropriate and within scope. If the item
suggests a wider problem, consider whether further warrant needed for deeper search (e.g. a
computer in plain view — seize OK, but content search needs further authority).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Moving items to 'find' them in plain view. (2) Seizing items not
obviously evidential. (3) Going beyond scope of original warrant under cover of plain view.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Heaphy; DPP v O'Donnell (scope); s.26 MDA (drugs warrants).
STATUTE — Section 6, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Search powers — general

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FRAMEWORK: Provides search powers in connection with various offences; complements s.7
plain view.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.7 CJA 2006; specific offence statutes.

2.3 Consent Searches and Hot Pursuit
DPP v Lavery (2018) IECA
FACTS: Search conducted on basis of consent of the occupier; question of validity and scope of
consent.
PRINCIPLE: Consent searches are lawful where the consent is (a) given by a person with authority
over the premises, (b) genuinely voluntary (not under duress or implicit pressure), (c) informed (the
person knows what is being asked), (d) specific in scope, and (e) revocable. Where any element is
missing, the consent is doubtful and evidence at risk.
RATIO: Consent must be real — informed, voluntary, from the right person, with understood scope.
TEST: Was the consent given by someone with authority, freely, informedly, with understood
scope? Could the householder have refused without consequence?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will challenge whether the consent was truly voluntary.
'My client believed if he refused, things would be worse for him.' The Garda must show: how consent
was sought (request, not demand); whether refusal was made known to be an option; whether the
consenting person knew what was being searched for.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] I called at [address]. I was met at the door by [person], who
identified themselves as the occupier. I explained that I was investigating [offence] and asked if she
would consent to me searching the premises. I made clear that she was not obliged to consent and
could refuse, and that I would have to obtain a warrant in that event. She replied [verbatim consent].
I asked her to sign a consent-to-search form. I conducted the search."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Where you have grounds but no warrant, consent is an option —
but proceed carefully. Written consent form ideal. Verbal consent must be witnessed. Make refusal
an obvious option. Where person seems hesitant or coerced, get the warrant instead. Consent from
one occupier doesn't necessarily authorise search of another occupier's personal space (separate
bedroom of cohabiting adult, etc).
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Consent form signed and dated, with witness. Notebook
entry. Time of consent. Time of search.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Implicit pressure ('we can do this the easy way or get a warrant'). (2)
Consent from someone without authority (child, visitor, ex-partner who doesn't live there). (3)
Searching beyond the scope of consent. (4) No written record of consent.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.5; DPP v Dunne; warrant requirements as alternative.
DPP v O'Donnell — Hot pursuit (illustrative) (line of authority)
FACTS: Suspect pursued from scene of an immediate offence into a dwelling. Question of whether
entry was lawful without warrant.
PRINCIPLE: Hot pursuit allows entry without warrant where: (a) a serious offence has just been
committed or is being committed, (b) the suspect is fleeing immediately, (c) the entry is in active
pursuit (continuous from the offence/scene), (d) there is necessity for immediate entry to prevent
escape, harm, or evidence destruction.
RATIO: Article 40.5 yields to immediate operational necessity in narrowly-defined circumstances.
TEST: Continuous pursuit from immediate offence with operational necessity?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where entry was without warrant, the prosecution must
establish each element of hot pursuit. Time gaps in pursuit, lost contact then re-acquired contact,
second-hand information about suspect's location — all weaken the hot pursuit basis.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], I observed [suspect] commit [offence — e.g. assault on a
member of the public] at [scene]. He fled and I pursued, maintaining sight. He entered [dwelling]. I

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

entered immediately in hot pursuit, citing the immediate offence and necessity. I located him at
[position] and arrested him under [power] at [time]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Hot pursuit is narrow. Genuinely continuous pursuit, timeimmediate, from an active offence. If the suspect runs off and you find him 20 minutes later at home,
that's not hot pursuit — that's tracking down a suspect, which needs an arrest warrant or other power
and, for dwelling entry, a search warrant or consent.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating any quick follow-up as hot pursuit. (2) Continuing search
after suspect arrested — the hot pursuit authority is to enter and arrest, not to conduct an investigation
search.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.5; arrest warrant powers; DPP v Lavery.

2.4 Vehicle Search — Specific Considerations
DPP v Owens [1999] 2 IR 16
FACTS: Search of a vehicle at the roadside under s.23 MDA; scope and grounds challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Vehicle searches under s.23 require reasonable suspicion that the vehicle contains
controlled drugs. The search may include the cab, boot, under-seat areas, glove compartment, and
accessible compartments. Locked compartments require specific suspicion to extend the search.
RATIO: Vehicle searches are searches — the same standards of grounds, proportionality, and
recording apply.
TEST: Reasonable suspicion of drugs in the vehicle, with scope proportionate to suspicion.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence may challenge the basis for stop, the basis for search,
the basis for extending search to specific compartments. Each step is articulable.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I stopped the vehicle at [location] at [time]. On approach, I noted
[observations — smell of cannabis, observed handover earlier, known to me as user, etc]. I had
reasonable suspicion the vehicle contained controlled drugs. I asked the driver and passenger to step
out. I searched the vehicle under s.23 MDA. I located in [compartment] [item]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Stop legitimately — RTA grounds, breath test indication, witness
sighting, or stop and search grounds. Note observations at the stop. Develop suspicion if you can.
Get occupants out of vehicle before searching for safety and integrity of search. Photograph items
in situ before seizure. Secure vehicle if continuing search.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Stops without grounds. (2) Search without explaining grounds to
driver. (3) Searching beyond scope (e.g. locked storage without specific suspicion). (4) Failure to
document each compartment searched.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.23 MDA; s.30 CJ(PO)A; DPP v O'Reilly.
DPP v Reilly — Joint possession in vehicle [2004] IESC 109
FACTS: Multiple occupants of vehicle; drugs found accessible to more than one; each charged with
joint possession.
PRINCIPLE: Joint possession requires (a) knowledge of the substance, (b) ability to exercise
control, (c) intent to do so. Mere presence in a vehicle is insufficient. Each defendant's circumstances
must be assessed separately.
RATIO: Possession is personal, even if exercised jointly.
TEST: For each occupant: knowledge + control + intent?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will argue mere presence. Prosecution must show each
defendant's specific connection — proximity, accessibility, conduct, statements.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On stopping the vehicle, I noted: driver [A] in front, passenger [B]
in front, passenger [C] in rear. The cannabis was located [specific position — e.g. on the centre
console, equally accessible to A and B; or in the rear footwell, immediately at C's feet]. I noted at
scene: A said [verbatim], B said [verbatim], C said [verbatim]. [Phone messages / observations /
earlier conduct] supported each accused's connection."

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Document each occupant's position, accessibility, statements,
conduct at scene. Photograph the vehicle with positions marked. Each suspect's pockets and clothing
searched for connecting items. Phones examined for messages indicating shared enterprise.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Bulk-charging joint possession on mere presence. (2) Failing to note
positional details. (3) Failing to take individual statements at scene.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Joint possession line; DPP v Power (s.15A); People (DPP) v Foley.

2.5 Strip Search, Intimate Search, and Sampling
DPP v Birmingham — Strip search (illustrative) (line of authority)
FACTS: Strip search of detained person in custody; authorisation and conduct examined.
PRINCIPLE: Strip search (more than removal of outer clothing) requires Member-in-Charge or
Superintendent authorisation, specific grounds, same-sex searcher, and discrete conduct. It engages
bodily integrity (Article 40.3).
RATIO: Bodily intrusion in search requires specific authority and proportionate conduct.
TEST: Authorisation by appropriate rank + specific grounds + proportionate conduct + same-sex
searcher + dignified setting?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will challenge basis for strip search and conduct.
Where the search produced evidence, the chain must be defensible to a high standard.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Strip searches: not routine. Specific grounds (concealment
suspected based on intelligence, observation of suspect behaviour suggesting concealment, prior
history). Authorisation by Member-in-Charge. Same-sex searcher. Private setting. Two members
present (searcher + witness, same sex). Document carefully.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Routine strip-searching of arrested persons. (2) Cross-gender searches.
(3) Failure to authorise properly. (4) Public setting. (5) Inadequate documentation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.3 (bodily integrity); Custody Regulations.
Intimate search and sample regime Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act 1990; CJ(FE&DNAS)A 2014
FACTS: Statutory regime for intimate samples (blood, urine, swabs of body orifices, pubic hair,
dental impressions) and non-intimate samples (head hair, mouth swab, fingerprints).
PRINCIPLE: Intimate samples require: (a) medical practitioner to take, (b) informed consent OR
specific Superintendent authorisation, (c) for specified relevant offences. Non-intimate samples: less
formality, may be taken by a member for specified relevant offences.
RATIO: Bodily samples engage Article 40.3 personal rights; statutory regime balances investigative
need with personal integrity.
TEST: Right sample, right authority, right safeguards, right person taking it.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Sample admissibility depends on each step of the procedure.
Defence will examine: consent / authority basis; taking procedure (medical practitioner for intimate);
chain of custody to FSI; analysis; result presentation.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], I requested a [sample] from the suspect for the purpose of
investigation of [offence]. I explained: that the offence was a relevant offence under the Act; that he
had a right to refuse [for intimate samples]; the consequences of refusal [where applicable]. He
[consented / refused]. [Where consent given:] At [time], Doctor [name], a designated medical
practitioner, attended and took the sample in my presence. The sample was sealed, labelled, and
transferred to FSI with continuity statement."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Sample regime is procedure-critical. Get the form right, the
consent right, the doctor's attendance documented. For DNA, buccal swab is the standard nonintimate sample — easier. Chain of custody from suspect to FSI is the case.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Confusing intimate / non-intimate categories. (2) Failing to obtain
consent or authorisation. (3) Lay member taking intimate sample. (4) Chain of custody gaps.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v JF (DNA); s.9 CJ(FE&DNAS)A 2014; Article 40.3.

2.6 Mobile Phones and Digital Devices
DPP v Forde [2017] IECA
FACTS: Phone seized incident to arrest; content searched at scene; admissibility challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Phone seizure incident to arrest is lawful under s.6 CJA 1984. Content examination
— going beyond mere physical seizure to access data — requires specific authority: warrant,
production order, or specific statutory power. The volume and intimacy of phone data attracts
heightened protection.
RATIO: Phones are not pockets. Seizure is one act; content access is another, requiring separate
authority.
TEST: Was the phone seized lawfully? Was content accessed with separate authority?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will examine: when was the phone seized? When was
content accessed? By whom? Under what authority? Was the device handed to CCU per protocol or
browsed at the station?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], following arrest, I seized the suspect's phone (handset
model, IMEI) incident to arrest under s.6 CJA 1984. I powered off the device and placed it in a
Faraday bag. I did not browse or access content. The device was transferred to the Computer Crime
Unit on [date] under continuity statement, for forensic examination pursuant to [warrant / production
order / consent]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Power off if possible. Faraday bag. Do not browse. Do not attempt
PIN. Submit to CCU with proper authority (production order, warrant). CCU produces forensic
image and report. Content analysed by trained personnel.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Browsing the phone at the station. (2) Asking suspect for PIN under
pressure. (3) Looking through messages 'just to see'. (4) Photographing screen content informally.
(5) Failure to use Faraday bag (remote wipe risk).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.6 CJA 1984; production orders under CJA 2011; DPP v Egan; Riley v
California (US analogous reasoning).

2.7 Recurring Pitfalls in Search Powers
Cumulative practice across courts shows the following recurring problems sinking search-based
cases. Each is preventable.
Wrong premises identified (recurring theme)
FACTS: Warrant for No. 7 Flat A; executed at No. 7 Flat B. Or warrant for an apartment in a multiunit building where the warrant doesn't specify the unit.
PRINCIPLE: Premises specificity is constitutional. Wrong premises = unlawful entry = evidence
at risk.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Read the warrant. Confirm the address with what you can see. If
there's any doubt, pause and resolve before entry. Flats in inner-city Dublin often share house
numbers — name the flat / unit / floor specifically in the warrant application.
Generic grounds (recurring theme)
FACTS: Sworn information says 'I have reasonable grounds to believe that drugs are being supplied
at the premises.' No specifics.
PRINCIPLE: Generic grounds are inadequate. The DJ should refuse; sometimes they don't. When
the warrant is challenged in court, the thinness shows.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Write the information with specifics: dates, observations,
intelligence (protected as 'confidential information received from a source whose information has
previously been reliable'), associated arrests, surveillance products.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Scope creep (recurring theme)
FACTS: Drug warrant becomes a wide-ranging search of every drawer and cupboard.
PRINCIPLE: Search scope is determined by what is sought. Drugs and associated paraphernalia /
cash / phones — yes. Other items in plain view — s.7 CJA 2006. Going further requires specific
justification.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Brief the search team before entry: what we're looking for, the
scope. Stop and consider before opening anything that would not contain what we're looking for.
Continuity gaps in seized items (recurring theme)
FACTS: Item seized at scene; later challenged because exhibit number changed, seal broken, or
chain unclear.
PRINCIPLE: Continuity is sacred (see Bowes; Doolan).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Seal at scene, sign across, photograph, exhibit log, PEMS entry,
every transfer signed. Continuity statements from each handler ready for trial.

3. Detention Regimes & Custody Management
Detention is where most cases stand or fall in cross-examination. The clock, Custody Regulations, solicitor
access, interviews, medical attention — every minute recorded, every action accountable.
Detention is where most cases stand or fall in cross-examination. The clock, the Custody Regulations, solicitor
access, interviews, medical attention — every minute is recorded, every action accountable. The Member-inCharge is the gatekeeper, but every Garda involved must understand the regime well enough to spot problems
before they become defects.

3.1 The Detention Regimes Compared
Different powers, different clocks, different paperwork. Below: the major regimes a mid-level Garda
will encounter, with their distinguishing features. Always confirm the regime at the moment of arrest
and again on intake at the station.
STATUTE — Section 4, Criminal Justice Act 1984 — Detention Mechanics Standard arrestable offence
detention
FRAMEWORK: Detention for proper investigation of arrestable offence (5+ years imprisonment).
Maximum 24 hours total. Phased authorisations: 6 hours by Member-in-Charge initially; up to 12
hours total by Superintendent direction; up to 24 hours total by Chief Superintendent direction.
ELEMENTS: (a) Lawful arrest for arrestable offence; (b) Member-in-Charge satisfied detention
necessary for proper investigation; (c) extensions on grounds of continuing necessity.
POWERS / EFFECT: Detain, interview, fingerprint, photograph, sample (per separate regime),
search (per separate regime), charge or release.
TEST: At every moment: is detention currently authorised within cap, with necessity established
and recorded?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Custody record is the case foundation. Defence reads it minuteby-minute. Where it doesn't match other records (PULSE, station log, members' notebooks), the
inconsistencies are exploited.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: (See Arrest section, s.4 CJA 1984 entry, for testimony template.)
Specific to detention: "At [time] the Member-in-Charge authorised initial detention. At [time] the
Superintendent authorised first extension for [reasons]. At [time] the Chief Superintendent
authorised final extension for [reasons]. Total detention time was [hours/minutes], net of suspensions
for solicitor consultation, medical attention, and rest period."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Detention plan written from arrest forward. Interviews scheduled.
Solicitor contact made promptly. Rest period — at least 8 hours uninterrupted if detention spans an
overnight. Meal intervals strict. Member-in-Charge tracks the clock; investigating member must

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

also.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record (primary). Member-in-Charge entries.
Superintendent / Chief Superintendent directions in writing. Solicitor sign-in. Doctor visits.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Losing track of cumulative clock time across suspensions. (2) Late
extensions. (3) Rest period interrupted. (4) Meals missed. (5) Solicitor request not actioned. (6)
Inconsistencies between custody record and other records.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien (24hr cap); DPP v Finn (extension grounds); DPP v
Boylan (suspensions); DPP v Buck (Custody Regs); Custody Regulations 1987.
STATUTE — Section 30, Offences Against the State Act 1939 — Detention Mechanics Scheduled offence
detention
FRAMEWORK: Detention for scheduled offences. Maximum 72 hours total. Phases: 24 hours
initial (Member-in-Charge); 24 hours by Chief Superintendent direction; 24 hours by District Judge
order.
ELEMENTS: (a) Arrest under s.30; (b) detention necessity at each phase.
POWERS / EFFECT: Detain, interview, fingerprint, photograph, sample, search, charge or release.
TEST: Each phase: decision-maker satisfied detention necessary.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: S.30 detentions are high-stakes. The DJ phase is judicial —
application contested by defence, prosecution must justify continuing detention. Records and
authorisations must be impeccable.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] I arrested under s.30 OASA 1939 on suspicion of
[scheduled offence]. At [time] Member-in-Charge authorised initial detention. At [time] Chief
Superintendent [name] directed extension for [reasons]. On [date] application was made to District
Judge [name] for further extension. Application was granted at [time] for the period to [time]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: S.30 application paperwork prepared in advance. DJ application:
written application, evidence on oath, defence may attend and contest. Prepare evidence on the
necessity of further detention — specific investigative steps remaining.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) S.30 used where offence not scheduled. (2) DJ application thinly
grounded. (3) Failure to give defence reasonable notice of DJ application.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Heaney v Ireland (s.52 OASA — historic but illustrative); People (DPP)
v Quilligan; s.30 OASA.
STATUTE — Section 50, Criminal Justice Act 2007 — Detention Mechanics Detention for specified
offences (max 7 days)
FRAMEWORK: Power to detain for up to 7 days for specified serious offences (drug trafficking
with €10,000+ value; murder involving firearm; certain organised crime; explosives offences).
Phased authorisations: 24 hours initial; 48 hours Chief Super; further 24 hours DJ; further 48 hours
DJ; further 48 hours DJ.
ELEMENTS: (a) Arrest under s.50 for specified offence; (b) detention necessity at each phase.
POWERS / EFFECT: Standard detention powers.
TEST: Necessity for proper investigation at each phase.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: S.50 detentions are exceptional. Defence challenges
proportionality and necessity at each judicial phase.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Reserved for the most serious matters. DJ applications throughout
— extensive preparation. Investigation strategy mapped to the available time.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Using s.50 where s.4 / s.30 would suffice. (2) Insufficient grounds for
the specified-offence basis.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.4 CJA 1984; s.30 OASA; specified offences list.
STATUTE — Section 16, Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 Drug trafficking detention

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FRAMEWORK: Detention for drug trafficking offences. Maximum 7 days. Phased authorisations
similar to s.50 CJA 2007.
ELEMENTS: (a) Arrest for drug trafficking offence; (b) detention necessity.
POWERS / EFFECT: Detention powers; specific provisions for drug trafficking investigations.
TEST: Necessity for proper investigation.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Used in major drug investigations. Investigation strategy must
justify extended detention.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.50 CJA 2007; s.15A MDA; s.27A FA.

3.2 The Custody Regulations — Operational Detail
Criminal Justice (Treatment of Persons in Custody in Garda Síochána Stations) Regulations 1987 —
Overview S.I. No. 119/1987
FACTS: The Regulations establish the operational standards for treatment of persons in custody.
Promulgated under s.7 CJA 1984.
PRINCIPLE: Every person detained must be treated in accordance with the Regulations. Memberin-Charge is responsible for compliance. Specific provisions cover: information of rights; solicitor
access; family notification; medical attention; meals and refreshment; sleep; exercise; complaints;
interpreter; vulnerable persons; juveniles; records.
RATIO: The Regulations operationalise the constitutional and statutory rights of detained persons.
Non-compliance is a factor in admissibility analysis (Buck).
TEST: Has each Regulation been complied with? Where not, was the breach inadvertent and
corrected?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Custody record audited by defence against the Regulations.
Each Regulation has a corresponding entry expected.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Key provisions to memorise: (a) Information of detention basis
and rights immediately on arrival. (b) Solicitor access on request — contact promptly, allow private
consultation. (c) Family notification on request — make contact, document. (d) Medical attention as
required — designated doctor on call. (e) Meals at 3-hour intervals; refreshment between. (f) Sleep
— 8 hours uninterrupted in any 24-hour period, typically overnight. (g) Exercise — where
practicable, particularly for longer detentions. (h) Complaints — note any, escalate per procedure.
(i) Interpreter — provide where language difficulty. (j) Vulnerable persons — appropriate adult,
special considerations. (k) Juveniles — parent / guardian, special procedures. (l) Records —
contemporaneous in custody record.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record is THE record. Member-in-Charge signs
entries with time.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Custody record gaps. (2) Entries made retrospectively. (3) Memberin-Charge handing over without proper briefing of incoming MIC. (4) Failure to document refusals
(e.g. suspect refuses food — must be recorded).
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Buck; DPP v Bowes & McGrath; s.7 CJA 1984.
Member-in-Charge Role Regulations + Garda Code
FACTS: The Member-in-Charge is the Garda who has charge of the station for the period in
question. Their responsibility includes ensuring all detained persons are treated in accordance with
the Regulations.
PRINCIPLE: The MIC is the gatekeeper. The MIC authorises detention, supervises treatment,
records all significant events, handles solicitor / family / medical requests, escorts interviews, and is
the primary witness for the Custody Regs compliance.
TEST: Is the MIC's record complete, contemporaneous, accurate?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: MIC is typically called as witness on detention and treatment
issues. Their record is the defence baseline.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If you are MIC: focus is on the suspects, not on the wider station.
Contemporaneous entries — don't tidy up at end of shift. Handover to incoming MIC: walk through
each detained person, status, outstanding actions.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) MIC running the front desk simultaneously and missing custody
events. (2) Handover incomplete. (3) Entries in pencil to be 'tidied up later'. (4) Discrepancies
between MIC record and member's notebook.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Custody Regulations 1987; s.7 CJA 1984.
DPP v Madden [1977] IR 336 (SC)
FACTS: Long detention; statement challenged on grounds of oppression by length, isolation, and
conditions.
PRINCIPLE: Oppression in detention is not only physical mistreatment. Length, isolation, repeated
questioning, sleep deprivation, atmospheric pressure can amount to oppression cumulatively. The
court considers the totality of conditions.
RATIO: Voluntariness of statement requires absence of oppression — broadly understood.
TEST: Considering all conditions of detention, was the suspect's will overborne?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where defence raises oppression, the custody record and
interview records are examined for: interview duration, breaks, sleep, meals, environmental
conditions, suspect's apparent state at intervals.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Maximum 2-hour interview sessions before break. Genuine
breaks — out of interview room, refreshment, time to compose. Document suspect's apparent
condition at intervals. If signs of distress, fatigue, intoxication impairment — suspend interview.
Better to break and resume than to push.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Marathon interviews. (2) Breaks in same room without genuine
respite. (3) Failure to note suspect's apparent state.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Holland (interview duration); DPP v Ward (oppression methods).
DPP v Holland (CCA, unreported 15 June 1998)
FACTS: Lengthy interview without adequate breaks; suspect exhausted; confession excluded.
PRINCIPLE: Excessive interview duration without proper breaks producing exhaustion is
oppression. The resulting confession is inadmissible.
RATIO: Duration + lack of rest + cumulative pressure = oppression.
TEST: Considering the length of interview sessions, cumulative duration across detention, breaks
afforded, suspect's apparent condition — was the will overborne?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will tabulate interview times against breaks against
rest. Where there is no rest period of sufficient length, the case is at risk.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Maximum 2 hours per session before break. Genuine break — out
of room, refreshment, toilet, time. Document time in / out of interview room. Document offered /
taken refreshments. Document any signs of distress, fatigue, intoxication. If unfit for interview,
suspend. Mental health, physical illness, intoxication all relevant.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Pushing through fatigue. (2) Continuing interview when suspect is no
longer engaging. (3) Failure to log breaks.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Madden; DPP v Ward; DPP v Lynch.
DPP v Ward [1998] (Special Criminal Court)
FACTS: Confession in Veronica Guerin murder investigation; Ward held for extended period under
s.30 OASA. Methods of questioning challenged.
PRINCIPLE: The Special Criminal Court excluded the confession, finding that methods of
questioning amounted to oppression. Pressure is not only shouting. Repeated questioning, isolation,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

sleep disruption, atmospheric pressure, persistent confrontation — all may amount to oppression
cumulatively.
RATIO: Oppression is a cumulative concept. Many small pressures compound.
TEST: Would a person of ordinary firmness in the suspect's position have had their will overborne?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where defence makes a Ward-style argument, the detention is
examined holistically. Every aspect — environmental, conversational, procedural — is in play.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Avoid: relentless repetition of the same question, theatrical anger
or kindness, suggestion that 'we know you did it', references to family in pressurising ways, isolation
tactics beyond proper interview structure. Interview at least two-up where possible (corroboration
of conduct). Recording is your protection — both ways.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'We have evidence' suggestions when you don't. (2) Repeated
questions designed to wear down rather than elicit. (3) Manipulating breaks (short breaks during
productive moments to prevent suspect from regrouping). (4) Tone of voice and demeanour outside
professional range.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Holland; DPP v Madden; DPP v Lynch; Saunders v UK (ECHR
oppression).
DPP v Lynch [1981] ILRM 401
FACTS: Statement made by suspect who had been in custody for extended period; voluntariness
challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Where suspect makes a statement after extended detention, the prosecution must
establish voluntariness — that the statement was the product of the suspect's free will, not the result
of oppression, inducement, or hope of advantage.
RATIO: Burden on prosecution to establish voluntariness affirmatively in challenged cases.
TEST: Was the statement the product of free will, untainted by oppression or inducement?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Voluntariness is examined by reference to the whole detention
— conditions, conduct, exchanges, suspect's apparent state.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the voluntariness picture: regular breaks, meals, sleep,
solicitor access, respectful conduct. Documented offers of refreshment / breaks, even if declined.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Failure to log declined offers (looks like they weren't made). (2)
Inadequate rest periods. (3) Aggressive questioning approach.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tyndall (inducement); DPP v Holland; DPP v Ward.
DPP v Tyndall [2005] 1 IR 593
FACTS: Statement challenged on voluntariness; question of subtle inducement.
PRINCIPLE: Voluntariness requires absence of threats, oppression AND inducement — including
subtle inducement. 'It will go easier if you tell us', 'we can put in a good word', 'the judge will look
favourably' — all amount to inducement.
RATIO: Inducement need not be express to vitiate voluntariness.
TEST: Was any expression made that could reasonably be understood as offering a conditional
advantage in exchange for statement / admission?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will look for any soft language, suggestions of
leniency, references to consequences, references to family / others. Recordings are the test — if it's
not on the record, your testimony is contested.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: No promises. No suggestions of leniency. No references to family
or third parties as leverage. If the suspect raises 'what happens if I cooperate?' — answer factually:
'I can't promise you anything. Whether to make a statement is your decision. Anything you say is
evidence.' Document the exchange.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'It would be in your interest to…' (2) 'The judge will look favourably

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

on cooperation.' (3) 'Think of your children.' (4) 'If you tell us about X we won't go after Y.' (5) Any
conditional language about consequences.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Lynch; DPP v Ward; DPP v Hoey.
DPP v Hoey [1987] IR 637
FACTS: Allegations of inducement during interview; statement challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Where any inducement is alleged with credibility, the burden is on prosecution to
disprove it. Voluntariness is a prosecution burden.
RATIO: Allegations of inducement, once raised credibly, must be disproved.
TEST: Has the prosecution disproved the alleged inducement on the balance of probabilities?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Recording and contemporaneous notes are the answer. If the
inducement is not on the recording and the suspect alleges it, the recording is the prosecution's case.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Recorded interviews are your protection. Where recording is
impossible (non-designated station, equipment fault), second member present + careful notes.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Off-tape conversations that touch on substance. (2) Tea-and-chat
moments with suspect that turn into discussion of evidence.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tyndall; DPP v Mallon (recording).

3.3 Solicitor Access — Detention Phase
Gormley & White v DPP [2014] IESC 17
FACTS: (See Arrest section for full treatment.) Suspect interviewed before solicitor consultation;
admissions challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Constitutional right to reasonable access to legal advice. Where suspect has requested
solicitor, no substantive questioning until solicitor consulted (or reasonable efforts exhausted and
waiver).
RATIO: Solicitor access is gateway to fair interview.
TEST: Request made? Reasonable efforts made? Consultation occurred before interview?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Solicitor request triggers hard stop on interview. Custody record
entries: time of request, efforts made, solicitor identity, time of arrival, time of consultation, time of
resumed interview.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Continuing 'background questions' that elicit substantive material. (2)
Pressuring waiver. (3) Failing to log consultation timings.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Doyle; Murray v UK; Article 38.1.
DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1
FACTS: (See Arrest section for full treatment.) Question of solicitor presence (vs consultation)
during interview.
PRINCIPLE: Constitutional right is to reasonable access (consultation) — not absolute right to
presence during interview. Current practice facilitates presence.
RATIO: Consultation = constitutional minimum; presence = good practice.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Facilitate solicitor presence unless specific operational reason to
refuse. Brief solicitor on procedure. Allow mid-interview consultation where sought.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Refusing presence without articulable reason. (2) Allowing solicitor
to take over interview. (3) Restricting consultation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Gormley & White; Murray v UK; Article 38.1.

3.4 Vulnerable Persons in Detention
DPP v JM [2015] (vulnerable adult)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FACTS: Suspect with intellectual disability interviewed; safeguards questioned.
PRINCIPLE: Vulnerable adult suspects (mental health, intellectual disability, intoxication
impairment, age) require additional safeguards: (a) appropriate adult present, (b) plain language, (c)
frequent breaks, (d) careful pace, (e) confirmation of understanding, (f) medical opinion on fitness
for interview where doubt.
RATIO: Voluntariness and reliability depend on capacity. Capacity is assessed.
TEST: Was capacity assessed? Were appropriate safeguards in place?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where defence shows vulnerability and inadequate safeguards,
admissibility is at risk. Custody record entries on vulnerability assessment are foundational.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: On intake, Member-in-Charge screens: known medical / mental
health history (PULSE flag), behaviour observed, suspect's own disclosure, medication. Appropriate
adult — not the solicitor, a third party (family, advocate, Tusla for juveniles). Slow pace, simple
language, frequent breaks, check understanding. Designated doctor opinion on fitness if doubt.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating vulnerability as obvious or self-disclosing. (2) Family
member doubling as interpreter and appropriate adult. (3) Failure to obtain medical opinion when
doubt.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Custody Regulations 1987; DPP v Onumwere (interpreter); appropriate
adult provisions.
Children Act 2001 — Juveniles in Custody Statutory framework
FACTS: Statutory regime for treatment of under-18s in Garda custody, with additional protections.
PRINCIPLE: Juveniles require: (a) parent / guardian notification immediately, (b) appropriate adult
present for interview, (c) consideration of juvenile diversion before charge, (d) special interview
techniques, (e) age-appropriate care.
RATIO: Juveniles are constitutionally protected as developing persons.
TEST: Were juvenile-specific protections observed?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Juvenile in custody: parent / guardian contacted immediately. JLO
(Juvenile Liaison Officer) notified. Appropriate adult — typically parent / guardian or, if unavailable
/ inappropriate, social worker. Diversion considered before charge per Part 4 of the Act.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating juvenile as standard adult suspect. (2) Failure to consider
diversion. (3) Interview without appropriate adult.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Children Act 2001; Garda Juvenile Diversion Programme; Children First
Act 2015.

3.5 The Detention Clock — Suspensions and Mechanics
DPP v Boylan [1991] 1 IR 477
FACTS: (See Arrest section for full treatment.) Suspension of detention clock during specified
periods.
PRINCIPLE: Clock suspends for: solicitor consultation, medical attention, rest period (8 hours
overnight), specified other categories. Each suspension recorded with start and end.
RATIO: Suspensions are for the suspect's protection, not investigative convenience.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Log every suspension contemporaneously. Genuine activity
required (real rest, real consultation).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Recording 8 hours rest when actual rest was 4. (2) Failing to log brief
solicitor consultations. (3) Suspensions that aren't genuine.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien; DPP v Finn; Custody Regulations 1987.
Detention Clock Worked Example (operational)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FACTS: Suspect arrested at 14:00 Friday under s.4 CJA 1984. Cap is 24 hours of net detention time.
PRINCIPLE: Wall-clock and net detention time can diverge significantly with suspensions.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Example timeline: 14:00 arrest; 14:30 arrival station / MIC
authorisation (clock starts 14:00 or 14:30 — defence may argue both). 15:00 solicitor requested.
16:30 solicitor arrives, consultation 16:30-17:30 (1 hour suspension). 17:30 interview commences.
19:30 break for meal 30 mins. 20:00 interview resumes. 22:00 interview ends. 22:00-06:00 rest
period (8 hours suspension). 06:00 morning meal. 07:00 second interview commences. ETC. — net
detention time after suspensions tracks toward the 24-hour cap. Charge or release before cap is
reached.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Custody record entries timed to the minute. Suspension
entries with start and end.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Confusing wall clock with net clock. (2) Failure to record exact
suspension times. (3) Running close to cap without buffer.
Charge or Release — End of Detention (operational)
FACTS: End of detention period: either charge or release. The decision is significant and must be
timely.
PRINCIPLE: If sufficient evidence and DPP / Inspector direction supports charge: charge before
cap, bring before next court sitting (or remand in custody where applicable). If insufficient evidence
or further investigation required: release. Re-arrest under s.10 CJA 1999 only on new information.
TEST: Is there sufficient evidence to charge?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Charge file prepared during detention — CM13 (charge sheet),
brief facts, evidence list. Inspector or Sergeant signs off charge per current practice. Court bag
prepared for next appearance.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Charging without adequate evidence. (2) Releasing on bail without
proper bail conditions. (3) Failure to inform victim / complainant of release.

3.6 Recurring Pitfalls in Detention
Recurring mid-level problems in detention regimes:
Late authorisations (recurring)
FACTS: Extension that should have been at hour 6:00 is signed at hour 6:30.
PRINCIPLE: Authorisations must be timely. Retrospective authorisation is at risk of being treated
as no authorisation.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Set alarms. Track the clock. Brief the Super in advance of the
deadline.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'I'll get him to sign it later.' (2) Direction signed after the fact.
Custody record discipline (recurring)
FACTS: Entries pencilled in, tidied at end of shift, or backfilled.
PRINCIPLE: Custody record must be contemporaneous, ink (or system equivalent), and honest.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Live entries. If you missed an entry, note when you noticed and
when you entered it.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) End-of-shift tidying. (2) Inconsistencies between custody record and
other records.
Solicitor delays (recurring)
FACTS: Solicitor requested but not arriving for hours; pressure to interview without.
PRINCIPLE: Reasonable efforts to facilitate solicitor. Waiver only if genuinely informed and
voluntary.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Document calls made to solicitor's office (time, person spoken to,
outcome). Offer alternative solicitor from station list. Where suspect persists in wanting their
preferred solicitor and willing to wait, wait — but at the cap, charge or release.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Pressuring waiver. (2) 'Brief background questions' before solicitor.
Interview discipline breakdowns (recurring)
FACTS: Long interviews, no breaks, oppressive conduct, inducements.
PRINCIPLE: 2 hours maximum per session, genuine breaks, no inducement, no oppression.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Plan interview structure. Self-monitor for oppressive drift.
Witness Garda corroborates conduct.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Wearing the suspect down with repetition. (2) Going off-recording for
'chat'. (3) Verbal pressure when evidence is thin.
VOLUME 2
EVIDENCE & INTERVIEW
Building the Case from First Contact

4. Evidence Law — Admissibility, Continuity, Forensics
Evidence law is where investigation meets trial. Voluntariness, continuity, disclosure, expert testimony,
identification, hearsay — the mid-level Garda must understand how evidence is built, preserved, and presented.
Evidence law is where investigation meets trial. Voluntariness, continuity, disclosure, expert testimony,
identification, hearsay, and the exclusionary rules. The mid-level Garda is not the trial lawyer but must
understand how evidence is built, preserved, and presented — because the work is done on the ground and the
file built in real time, not by a barrister months later.

4.1 Voluntariness and the Exclusionary Rule
DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31 (SC, 7 judges)
FACTS: Evidence obtained pursuant to a search warrant later found constitutionally defective; the
Gardaí had relied on it in good faith without knowledge of the defect. The Supreme Court
reconsidered Kenny v DPP [1990] which had established an absolute exclusionary rule.
PRINCIPLE: Evidence obtained in breach of constitutional rights is presumptively inadmissible.
Prosecution may rebut by establishing the breach was INADVERTENT — Garda acted in good faith
without recognition of the breach. Deliberate or reckless breaches still result in exclusion. Onus is
on prosecution to establish inadvertence.
RATIO: Inadvertent breach + good faith may save evidence; deliberate or reckless breach excludes
it.
TEST: Was the breach inadvertent (not realised), in good faith (no recklessness), and not
undermining overall fairness?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial explores state of knowledge at the time. Did Gardaí know?
Should they have known? What checks were made? Was the breach inherent in the action or
peripheral?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At the time of [act], I had checked [what was checked]. I had no
reason to believe there was any defect. I was unaware of [the defect] until [point in time]. The checks
I made before acting included [list]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: JC is a safety net, not a strategy. Proceed always as if the strict
rule applies — check warrants, verify powers, document grounds. Where you discover a defect after
the fact, disclose it honestly. Cover-up converts inadvertence into bad faith.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Notebook entries showing checks. Supervisor briefings.
Pre-action consultations.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating JC as licence for sloppiness. (2) Concealing recognised
defects. (3) Failing to articulate checks made at the time.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Kenny (overruled); Damache v DPP; DPP v Quirke; Article 38.1;
Article 40.3.
DPP v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110 (SC) — overruled by JC
FACTS: Pre-JC absolute exclusionary rule for unconstitutionally obtained evidence.
PRINCIPLE: Constitutional breach = exclusion regardless of good faith. The law for 25 years until
JC.
RATIO: Constitutional rights protected by absolute deterrent rule.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Kenny mindset — that constitutional rights are robustly protected
— should still inform practice. JC saves inadvertent breaches; does not licence carelessness.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v JC; Damache.
DPP v Tyndall [2005] 1 IR 593
FACTS: Statement challenged on voluntariness; question of subtle inducement.
PRINCIPLE: Voluntariness requires absence of threats, oppression AND inducement — including
subtle inducement. Promises of leniency, hopes of advantage, references to family — all
inducement.
RATIO: Inducement need not be express to vitiate voluntariness.
TEST: Was any expression made that could reasonably be understood as offering a conditional
advantage?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Recordings are the test. Where inducement alleged, recording
is primary evidence.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: No promises. No suggestions of leniency. No conditional
language. If suspect asks 'what happens if I cooperate?' — answer: 'I can't promise you anything.
The decision whether to make a statement is yours. Anything you say is evidence.'
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Soft inducement language. (2) Off-tape conversations. (3) Tea-andchat moments touching on substance.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Hoey; DPP v Lynch; DPP v Madden.
DPP v Hoey [1987] IR 637
FACTS: Allegations of inducement; voluntariness challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Where inducement alleged credibly, burden on prosecution to disprove it. Recording
is the answer.
RATIO: Voluntariness is a prosecution burden.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Recorded interviews discipline yourself accordingly. Where
recording impossible, second member + careful notes.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tyndall; DPP v Mallon.
DPP v Mallon [2011] IECCA 29
FACTS: Interview not properly recorded; statement challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Electronic Recording of Interviews Regulations 1997 require recording in designated
stations for arrestable offences. Failure may render statement inadmissible. Exceptions: equipment
malfunction, second member with note, suspect refusal — all documented.
RATIO: Recording protects both ways.
TEST: Was recording made? If not, was the exception properly handled?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Test equipment before interview. If malfunction, get technical

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

attention or use second-member-with-note method. Document malfunction in custody record. Where
suspect refuses recording, document refusal on tape before stopping.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Equipment not tested. (2) Malfunction not documented. (3)
Inconsistencies between members' notes.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Electronic Recording of Interviews Regulations 1997; DPP v Hoey.
Saunders v UK (1996) 23 EHRR 313 (ECtHR)
FACTS: Compelled DTI inquiry statement used in subsequent criminal trial; Article 6 ECHR
challenge.
PRINCIPLE: Right against self-incrimination prohibits use of compelled statements in criminal
proceedings against the maker.
RATIO: Compelled statements are not voluntary; their use in criminal proceedings breaches Article
6.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Where compelled material is in the investigation, segregate it. Use
for intelligence / lead purposes; build separate evidence for criminal proceedings.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating compelled answers as direct evidence against maker. (2)
Cross-pollination between regulatory and criminal investigations.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Heaney v Ireland; Article 6 ECHR; Article 38.1 Bunreacht.

4.2 Identification and Recognition Evidence
DPP v Cooney [1997] 3 IR 205 (CCA)
FACTS: Garda gave evidence identifying accused from CCTV; defence challenged as inadmissible
opinion.
PRINCIPLE: Identification of a stranger and recognition of a familiar person are distinct cognitive
processes. Recognition by witness with prior familiarity admissible — basis established by witness's
own evidence.
RATIO: Familiarity is the foundation for recognition evidence.
TEST: (1) Prior familiarity? (2) Basis articulated? (3) Viewing conditions reasonable?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence will probe: how long known? How well? Specific
identifying features? Were features visible on CCTV? Trial judge gives Cooney/Turnbull direction.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I have known [accused] for approximately [period]. I have had
regular interactions with him in my role on the Mountjoy beat, including approximately [number]
direct encounters over this period — examples include [encounters]. I am familiar with his
appearance, gait, customary clothing, and the following distinguishing features: [list]. When I
