viewed the CCTV of the incident, I immediately recognised the person at [position in footage] as
[accused] by reason of [specific features visible]. I have no doubt as to the identification."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the recognition foundation BEFORE the identification act
in the statement. Length of acquaintance, nature of interactions, identifying features. Then state the
recognition with reasons. Avoid 'I know him to be X' without foundation.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Statement structure: foundation first, then identification,
then basis. Multiple Gardaí recognising independently strengthens the case.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Conclusory identifications. (2) Coaching multiple Gardaí to identify
the same features. (3) Failure to record uncertainty.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Casey; DPP v Boyce; Turnbull warning.
DPP v Casey [2018] IESC 53
FACTS: Recognition evidence by Garda; basis of familiarity scrutinised.
PRINCIPLE: Recognition admissible where foundation established. Cooney principles reaffirmed.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city beats produce recognition evidence routinely. Daily
interactions, named encounters, characteristic clothing / build / gait — all build the foundation.
Record encounters as they happen.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Cooney; DPP v Boyce.
DPP v Boyce [2008] IESC 62
FACTS: Photo identification challenged on quality of array, conduct of witness viewing.
PRINCIPLE: Identification by photo array requires: (a) array of similar persons (8+); (b) witness
not coached; (c) record of process; (d) no prior viewing of suspect's photo; (e) caution that suspect
may or may not be present.
RATIO: Identification evidence is dangerous evidence; procedure protects against suggestion.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Where ID parade not feasible, photo array — 8+ similar persons
(age, build, ethnicity, hair). Witness brought separately, cautioned, asked to look carefully.
Recording of process. Witness's confidence noted.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Array with obvious differences. (2) Witness coaching. (3) Multiple
witnesses viewing together. (4) Failure to caution.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Cooney; Turnbull; VIPER procedure.
Turnbull Warning R v Turnbull [1977] QB 224 — applied in Irish practice
FACTS: Trial judge guidance for ID evidence direction.
PRINCIPLE: Trial judge directs on ID evidence: dangerous because honest mistakes happen;
consider conditions of observation (lighting, distance, time, obstruction); prior familiarity; quality
of identification; risks of mistaken identity.
TEST: Conditions + quality + risk acknowledged?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I observed the person from approximately [distance]. Lighting was
[conditions — street lights operational, dusk, daylight, etc]. The observation lasted approximately
[duration]. There were no obstructions to my view. I had a clear view of [features]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the observation foundation. Distance, lighting, duration,
obstruction, features visible — all in the statement.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Cooney; DPP v Boyce.
DPP v O'Brien (CCTV) [2011] IECCA
FACTS: CCTV evidence central to robbery prosecution; authentication challenged.
PRINCIPLE: CCTV admissible where: (a) statement from operator/keeper proving system
reliability and time accuracy; (b) chain from capture to court via master copy preserved; (c) where
Garda identification offered, basis of recognition established separately; (d) fair representation (no
manipulation).
RATIO: CCTV is documentary evidence requiring authentication.
TEST: Provenance + integrity + fair representation?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence examines: who recorded? What system? Time stamp
accuracy? Master preservation? Process to court? Garda recognition foundation?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The CCTV was recovered from [premises] on [date] at [time]. I
obtained a statement from [keeper], who confirmed [system details, time accuracy method,
retention]. The master copy was preserved on [media]. The working copy in the case file is a faithful
reproduction. I have viewed the full sequence and the segment accurately represents the events at
[time range]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Capture within 24-72 hours (overwrite risk 7-30 days). Written
preservation request. Keeper statement covers system, accuracy, access. Master copy preserved
separately. Working copy with format playable on standard equipment.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Late capture — footage overwritten. (2) Keeper statement thin on time
accuracy. (3) No master copy preserved. (4) Working copy edited. (5) Garda recognition without
separate foundation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Cooney; Braddish v DPP (active preservation); DPP v O'Carroll.

4.3 Forensic Evidence and Chain of Custody
DPP v Bowes [2004] 4 IR 223 (CCA)
FACTS: Drugs prosecution; chain of custody for seized substance challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Chain of custody must be unbroken from seizure to laboratory to court. Each handler
is a link. Break may render evidence inadmissible.
RATIO: Continuity is foundational to forensic reliability.
TEST: Can the prosecution prove the item examined and produced in court is the item seized?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence examines: who seized? Who transported? Who logged
in property? Who transferred to FSI? Who at FSI received? Each link contested.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date] at [time] I seized [item] from [location] in [premises]. I
sealed it in tamper-evident exhibit bag [number] and signed across the seal at [time]. I retained it in
my exclusive custody until [time] when I transferred it to [property store/next handler] at [location].
I made a contemporaneous entry in PEMS [reference]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Seal at scene, sign across, photograph, exhibit log, PEMS entry,
every transfer signed. Continuity statements ready for trial.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: PEMS records, property log, continuity statements from
each handler, FSI receipt.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Gap in chain. (2) Seal broken without explanation. (3) Exhibit number
changed without reason recorded. (4) Inconsistent records between PEMS and notebooks.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Doolan; DPP v JF; FSI standards.
DPP v Doolan [2007] IECCA
FACTS: Chain of custody broken; sample examined cast into doubt.
PRINCIPLE: Chain break may lead to exclusion. Prosecution must establish each link.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Where continuity gap occurs: prompt assessment, can
independent evidence repair? Disclose to defence and DPP. Don't paper over.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Concealment of gaps. (2) Retrospective tidying of continuity
statements.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Bowes.
DPP v JF [2010] 1 IR 199
FACTS: DNA evidence; statistical interpretation challenged; low template DNA issues raised.
PRINCIPLE: DNA admissible subject to: (a) proper sample collection per statutory regime; (b)
chain of custody; (c) FSI laboratory standards; (d) expert testimony on match probability; (e) caution
where low template DNA (contamination, indirect transfer, stochastic effects).
RATIO: DNA is powerful but not infallible; context and corroboration matter.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence probes: contamination opportunities, mixed sample
interpretation, statistical methodology, sample quality (low template), indirect transfer.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Scene contamination avoidance: PPE before inner cordon, singleuse suits, no cross-contamination. Suspect samples per statutory regime. FSI expert briefed on full
case context.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Contamination at scene (Garda PPE failures). (2) Cross-contamination
between exhibits. (3) Sample below threshold quality. (4) Failure to consider indirect transfer.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: CJ(FE)A 1990; s.9 CJ(FE&DNAS)A 2014; DPP v Bowes.
DPP v Brady (Fingerprints) [2005] IECCA
FACTS: Fingerprint identification challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Fingerprint identification is expert evidence. ACE-V methodology (Analysis,
Comparison, Evaluation, Verification). Peer verification.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Lift latent prints with care. Recover items where prints on textured
surfaces. PPE to avoid Garda print contamination. Elimination prints from those legitimately at
scene.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Fingerprint Section procedures; DPP v JF.
DPP v Allen (Footprints) [2003] 4 IR 295
FACTS: Footprint impression evidence admitted; methodology challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Footprint evidence admissible with expert testimony — class characteristics (make,
size), individual characteristics (wear patterns).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Photograph impression with scale and oblique lighting. Cast
where possible. Avoid contamination. Submit suspect's footwear with continuity.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Brady (fingerprints).
DPP v Tomkins [2012] IECCA
FACTS: Crime scene examination methodology challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Crime scene examination must be disclosed in detail — sequence, photographs
sequenced and dated, exhibits accounted for, methodology recorded.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Scene log discipline: first responder records state, secures scene,
calls Scenes of Crime. Common Approach Path single route in/out. Inner / outer cordons.
Photograph sequence: overall, mid-range, close-up. Sketch with measurements. Exhibits
photographed in situ before recovery. Each Garda entering logged.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Scene preservation protocols.
DPP v Egan (Mobile phones) [2010] IECCA
FACTS: Mobile phone evidence; question of search, examination, content extraction.
PRINCIPLE: Phone seizure on arrest permissible under s.6 CJA 1984. Examination of content
requires further authority (warrant or specific statutory power). Phones attract heightened protection
— volume and intimacy of data.
RATIO: Phones are not pockets — separate authorisation regime for content.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Power off if possible. Faraday bag during transport. Do NOT
browse content at station. Submit to Cyber Crime Unit / Computer Crime Unit with warrant or
production order. PIN/passcode request under caution — refusal noted. CCU produces forensic
image; analysis follows.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Browsing the phone at station. (2) Asking suspect for PIN under
pressure. (3) Looking through messages 'just to see'. (4) Photographing screen content informally.
(5) Failure to use Faraday bag (remote wipe risk).
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Forde; s.6 CJA 1984; CJA 2011 production orders.
DPP v Forde [2017] IECA
FACTS: Phone seized incident to arrest; content searched at scene; admissibility challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Seizure incident to arrest lawful; content access requires separate authority.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Seize, power off, Faraday bag, CCU referral. Don't browse.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Egan.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

4.4 Expert Evidence
DPP v Buckley [2007] 3 IR 745
FACTS: Expert evidence challenged; qualifications, methodology, basis examined.
PRINCIPLE: Expert evidence admissible where: (a) field requires expertise; (b) witness qualified
by training and experience; (c) methodology recognised and properly applied; (d) basis disclosed
(data, assumptions); (e) opinion within field of expertise.
RATIO: Expert opinion must be evidence-based and transparent.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges: qualifications (CV scrutinised);
methodology (alternatives?); data (sufficient? reliable?); limitations (acknowledged?).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Brief expert with full case context, not selected material. Disclose
report in advance. Anticipate defence expert and prepare for cross.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Expert CV, methodology described, data disclosed,
alternatives addressed, limitations acknowledged.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Briefing only with prosecution-favourable material. (2) Failing to
disclose underlying data. (3) Expert testifying beyond field.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Smyth (cell-site); accident reconstruction; medical evidence.
DPP v Smyth (Cell-site) [2010] IECCA 34
FACTS: Drug supply prosecution; phone evidence central — CDR, cell-site analysis, content of
messages.
PRINCIPLE: Phone evidence admissibility requires: (a) lawful acquisition; (b) authentication; (c)
chain of custody through CCU to court; (d) expert testimony for cell-site interpretation.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: CDR via production order to carrier. Cell-site expert statement
explains tower mapping, sector geometry, accuracy/limitations. Content extraction documented
from CCU.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Phone seized but not promptly to CCU. (2) Cell-site relied on without
expert. (3) Authentication thin.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Egan; CJA 2011 production orders.

4.5 Hearsay and Statutory Exceptions
Hearsay Rule — Overview Common law + statutory exceptions
FACTS: Out-of-court statement tendered for truth of contents is hearsay; presumptively
inadmissible unless exception applies.
PRINCIPLE: Hearsay rule preserves cross-examination as test of evidence. Exceptions: dying
declarations, statements against interest, res gestae, business records, statutory exceptions (s.16 CJA
2006 etc), confessions (against maker), expert opinion.
TEST: Is the statement hearsay? If yes, does an exception apply?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Direct evidence preferable. Where hearsay arises, identify the
exception. PULSE entries, official documents, business records — various exceptions.
CROSS-REFERENCES: ss.16-17 CJA 2006; documentary evidence rules.
Section 16, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Prior inconsistent statements as substantive evidence
FACTS: Statutory exception allowing prior witness statements to be evidence of truth in certain
circumstances.
PRINCIPLE: Where witness at trial refuses to give evidence, gives evidence materially inconsistent
with prior statement, or denies making the prior statement, the prior statement may be admitted as
evidence of truth where: (a) court satisfied of voluntariness; (b) reliability; (c) made in circumstances
where the witness understood the seriousness; (d) it is in the interests of justice.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

RATIO: Witness retraction does not destroy prior evidence — statement may stand on its own
merits.
TEST: Was the statement made voluntarily, reliably, with understanding, and is admission in
justice?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Where witness hostile at trial or retracting, prosecution applies
under s.16. Court hears evidence on circumstances of statement. Statement may be admitted with
appropriate trial direction.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: DV cases especially: take prior statement in best possible
conditions. Sworn statement (s.16 format) before peace commissioner at first opportunity. Detail
surrounding context. Conditions noted: location, time, who present, whether suspect / abuser nearby,
whether intoxicated, whether under any pressure. The quality of the statement at the time determines
its later admissibility.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Statement signed and dated. Peace commissioner
attestation. Notebook entry on circumstances of taking. Member's evidence on conditions.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Cursory statement when full one possible. (2) Failure to swear at the
time. (3) No record of circumstances. (4) Statement taken in suspect's presence.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Yates; DV practice; s.17 CJA 2006.
DPP v Yates [2010] IECCA
FACTS: Prosecution witness gave evidence inconsistent with prior statement; application to treat
as hostile and admit prior statement.
PRINCIPLE: Where witness inconsistent at trial, court may rule hostile, permitting crossexamination by calling party. Prior statement may be admitted under s.16 CJA 2006.
RATIO: Witness retraction managed via hostile witness procedure and s.16.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Prepare for the possibility — every DV statement, every gangrelated witness statement, every vulnerable witness. Best evidence at the time.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.16 CJA 2006; DV practice.

4.6 Disclosure — The Continuing Duty
DPP v Special Criminal Court (Ward) [1999] 1 IR 60 (SC)
FACTS: Disclosure dispute in Guerin murder trial; range of material at issue.
PRINCIPLE: Prosecution must disclose all material relevant to guilt or innocence — including
material relevant to defence even if seemingly unhelpful to prosecution. Disclosure managed by
court where privilege/PII issues arise.
RATIO: Fair trial requires defence access to relevant material; secrecy is exception managed by
court.
TEST: Is the material relevant to any issue likely to arise? If yes, disclose (or apply for PII).
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Disclosure schedule maintained from investigation outset.
Every statement, exhibit, PULSE entry, notebook, CCTV, expert report, surveillance product (with
PII flag), intelligence document — each assessed for relevance.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build disclosure schedule live, not at end. Anything potentially
relevant flagged. For sensitive material: PII application via DPP and counsel, not unilateral
withholding.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Disclosure schedule on file. Decision log entries on
relevance assessments.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Withholding 'unhelpful to prosecution' material — defence assesses
relevance. (2) Late disclosure. (3) Unilateral PII decision (always via DPP).
CROSS-REFERENCES: Braddish v DPP; Dunne v DPP; DPP v Wilson; DPP v Marley.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Braddish v DPP [2001] 3 IR 127 (SC)
FACTS: Robbery charge; CCTV from scene not retained by Garda investigator; defence sought stay
on basis of lost evidence.
PRINCIPLE: Prosecution duty is not merely to disclose what they have, but to SEEK OUT AND
PRESERVE evidence relevant to guilt OR innocence. Failure may ground stay where real risk of
unfair trial.
RATIO: Disclosure has an active dimension — investigation must look for, not just preserve,
evidence.
TEST: Was reasonable effort made to seek out and preserve material relevant to the case (in any
direction)?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence may apply for stay where material lost. Court
considers: efforts made, significance of material, availability of alternative evidence.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Active preservation: from first attendance, identify all potential
evidence (CCTV in area, witnesses passing, forensic opportunities, mobile phone location data,
social media). Approach within 24-72 hours (CCTV overwrites 7-30 days). Written preservation
requests to keepers.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Statements from preservation requests. Logs of CCTV /
witness enquiries.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Failing to canvass area for CCTV. (2) Not approaching potential
witnesses promptly. (3) Allowing forensic opportunities to lapse.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Dunne v DPP; Ludlow v DPP; DPP v Wilson.
Dunne v DPP [2002] 2 IR 305 (SC)
FACTS: Item potentially relevant to defence lost during investigation.
PRINCIPLE: Preservation duty continues even where evidence appears favourable to prosecution.
Defence may find use in material the prosecution thinks unhelpful.
RATIO: The defence assesses relevance, not prosecution.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Property store discipline: don't dispose because 'we don't need
them'. Retention: case + appeal period + (serious offences) extended retention. Forensic samples per
FSI protocols.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Disposing of items because prosecution doesn't need. (2) Returning
items to victim without alternative evidence preserved.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Braddish v DPP; Ludlow v DPP.
Ludlow v DPP [2009] 1 IR 640 (SC)
FACTS: Evidence lost; defence sought stay.
PRINCIPLE: Test for lost evidence is whether there is real risk of unfair trial — considering nature
of missing material, potential significance, what other evidence exists, whether trial directions can
cure prejudice. Not every loss grounds stay.
RATIO: Balancing exercise — risk of unfairness against ability to manage at trial.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: When material lost: document efforts to retain, circumstances of
loss, alternative evidence. Open disclosure of loss to defence and DPP early. Trial judge may give
specific direction on missing material.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Concealing loss. (2) Failing to document retention efforts.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Braddish; Dunne; DPP v Wilson.
DPP v Wilson [2017] IESC 53
FACTS: Material relevant to defence emerged mid-trial; question of ongoing disclosure.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRINCIPLE: Prosecution duty of disclosure is CONTINUING throughout trial. New material must
be disclosed promptly. Failure to disclose can vitiate conviction or ground retrial.
RATIO: Fair trial is a continuing requirement, not pre-trial threshold.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Disclosure schedule lives during trial. Anything new — witness
statement, exhibit, intelligence, change in evidence — assessed for relevance and logged. Disclose
via prosecutor.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Closing disclosure file at start of trial. (2) Failing to escalate new
material. (3) Holding back unhelpful new evidence.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Ward; Braddish; DPP v Meleady.
DPP v Marley [1985] ILRM 17 (SC)
FACTS: PII application for sensitive material.
PRINCIPLE: Public Interest Immunity applies where disclosure would damage public interest
(source protection, future operational capability, national security). Court balances PII against
accused's fair trial interest. Court inspects material if necessary.
RATIO: PII is court-managed exception to disclosure — not unilateral withholding.
TEST: (1) Real public interest in non-disclosure? (2) Outweighs accused's interest? (3) Can lesser
steps (redaction, summary) suffice?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: PII applications: identify sensitive material early, brief DPP,
prepare confidential schedule. Categories: source identity, source methods, future operational
capability, third-party privacy. Court may order partial disclosure, full disclosure, or non-disclosure.
If material is sole evidence of guilt and cannot be disclosed: case may not proceed.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Unilateral withholding. (2) Inadequate PII application. (3) Failing to
anticipate PII issues.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Ward; informer privilege; surveillance product disclosure.
DPP v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268 (SC)
FACTS: (See Detention section.) Custody Regulations breaches; question of effect on admissibility.
PRINCIPLE: Custody Regs breaches do not automatically exclude evidence. Discretionary
considering nature, deliberateness, effect on suspect, effect on reliability, overall fairness.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Procedural compliance always preferred. Where breach occurs,
document honestly.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Custody Regulations 1987; DPP v Bowes & McGrath.
DPP v Meleady (No 3) [2001] 4 IR 16 (SC)
FACTS: Material not disclosed to defence; conviction. On appeal, undisclosed material assessed.
PRINCIPLE: Failure to disclose relevant material can vitiate conviction even where prosecution
acted in good faith. Test is effect on fairness, not Garda intent.
RATIO: Disclosure is an objective requirement — non-compliance has consequences regardless of
motive.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Better to disclose disputed material and let DPP decide than
withhold. Where uncertainty about relevance — flag to DPP.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Filtering disclosure on prosecution view of relevance. (2) Late
realisation of relevance.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Ward; Wilson.
DPP v Cronin (No 2) [2006] 4 IR 329 (SC)
FACTS: Defence point not raised at trial; sought to ground appeal.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRINCIPLE: Points not raised at trial generally cannot ground appeal — they must be ventilated
below to allow trial court to consider. Narrow exceptions (manifest injustice).
RATIO: Appeal is not second chance for tactical decisions.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Trial preparation: ensure every relevant matter is on the record.
Procedural defects, admissibility arguments, disclosure issues — get them before the trial judge.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Wilson.
DPP v O'Keeffe [2018] IECA
FACTS: Garda notebook lost; defence challenged conviction on basis of unavailable
contemporaneous record.
PRINCIPLE: Notebooks of Gardaí are disclosable; loss is a serious matter requiring explanation.
Where loss is reasonable and other evidence covers, conviction may stand; otherwise risk to fairness.
RATIO: Contemporaneous Garda records are evidence with disclosure consequences.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Notebook discipline: contemporaneous entries, preserved per
force policy. Don't lose them. Where lost: prompt notification, search efforts documented.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Loss undisclosed. (2) Reconstructed notes presented as
contemporaneous.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tuite (notebook standards); DPP v Wilson.
DPP v Tuite (1983) 2 Frewen 175 (CCA)
FACTS: Garda evidence quality challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Garda evidence must be contemporaneous, accurate, reliable. Embellishment,
contradictions, retrospective notes all undermine credibility.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Notebook on the day. Statement drawn from notebook, not
memory months later. Where discrepancies between notebook and statement — address openly.
Court testimony anchored in notebook; 'I don't recall' is honest and acceptable; speculation is not.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Keeffe.

5. Interview Law & Strategy
Interview law is where the case is built or lost. Caution, solicitor access, inference warnings, PEACE model,
vulnerable suspects, recording regime. Get the interview right and the case is built; get it wrong and admissions
are excluded.
Interviews are the moment where the case lives or dies. Strong investigation can be undone by a sloppy
interview; weak evidence can be built into a sustainable case by a disciplined one. The mid-level Garda must
master not just the legal mechanics but the techniques — PEACE model, question structure, evidence
deployment, and the careful navigation of the right to silence.

5.1 The Right to Silence and Inference Regime
Heaney v Ireland [1996] 1 IR 580 (SC); (2000) ECtHR
FACTS: Constitutional challenge to s.52 OASA 1939 (compelled answers during s.30 detention).
Supreme Court upheld; ECtHR subsequently found Article 6 ECHR breach.
PRINCIPLE: Restrictions on silence must be proportionate. The Heaney test: (a) rational
connection between restriction and objective, (b) restriction impairs the right as little as possible, (c)
effect proportionate to objective.
RATIO: Restrictions on silence must satisfy strict proportionality.
TEST: Necessary + minimal + balanced?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: S.52 OASA effectively unused post-Heaney ECtHR. Right to
silence dominates unless statutory inference regime properly invoked.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Don't expect to compel answers. Legitimate pathways for adverse
consequence are inference regimes (ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984) with all safeguards.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Relying on s.52 OASA. (2) Pressure on suspect to answer beyond
inference regimes.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Murray v UK; DPP v Connolly; ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984.
Murray v UK (1996) 22 EHRR 29 (ECtHR)
FACTS: Inferences from silence under NI equivalent provisions; Article 6 ECHR challenge.
PRINCIPLE: Inferences compatible with Article 6 PROVIDED: (a) solicitor access prequestioning; (b) court warned of dangers of relying solely on silence; (c) trial judge directs
appropriately; (d) other evidence supports inference.
RATIO: Inference regimes permissible with safeguards. Solicitor access is gateway.
TEST: All safeguards present?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Solicitor consultation BEFORE inference warning is the gateway.
Without it, the inference is gone. Document consultation properly — time arrival, time consultation,
time concluded, solicitor identity.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Deploying inference warning without prior solicitor consultation. (2)
Vague documentation of consultation timing.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Heaney; DPP v Connolly; Gormley & White.
DPP v Connolly [2003] 2 IR 1 (CCA)
FACTS: Inferences sought from suspect's failure to answer questions; statutory warning procedure
under ss.18/19 CJA 1984 challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Strict compliance with statutory warning procedure required: (a) solicitor consulted
(or genuine waiver); (b) warning given verbatim in prescribed form; (c) suspect afforded opportunity
to understand; (d) specific question asked on specific matter (item/mark for s.18; presence for s.19;
fact for s.19A).
RATIO: Inference provisions infringe right to silence — strict compliance is the safeguard.
TEST: ALL conditions met for each inference sought?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial judge examines interview record for each step. Any failure
invalidates inference for that question.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], following solicitor consultation completed at [time], I
administered the warning under section [18/19/19A] CJA 1984 in the prescribed terms: '[verbatim
from station form]'. I asked the suspect 'Do you understand?' He replied [verbatim]. I offered further
solicitor consultation. He [accepted/declined]. I then asked: '[specific question]'. The suspect
responded: '[verbatim — or remained silent]'."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Sequence: (1) Confirm solicitor consultation on record with time.
(2) Read prescribed warning VERBATIM from station-issued form. (3) Pause. Ask 'Do you
understand?' Note response. (4) Offer further solicitor consultation. (5) Ask specific question. (6)
Note response verbatim. (7) Re-caution before continuing standard interview.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Paraphrasing the warning. (2) Wrong section warning (s.18 for
presence question). (3) Not pausing for understanding. (4) Vague rather than specific question. (5)
Failure to re-caution before continuing.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Murray v UK; ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984; Heaney; Gormley & White.
Section 18, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Inference from failure to account for marks/substances
FACTS: Where suspect has on his person, in or on his clothing or footwear, or in any place in which
he is at time of arrest, an object, substance, or mark, and the Garda reasonably believes the
object/substance/mark may be attributable to participation in the offence, the Garda may inform the
suspect of the belief and request account. Failure or refusal to account may result in inferences at

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

trial.
TEST: Specific item, specific belief, specific request, specific failure — all present and
documented?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial judge directs that inference is permissible only if all
conditions satisfied. Wrong item, wrong belief basis, vague question = no inference.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], following solicitor consultation, I administered the s.18
warning. I asked: 'How do you account for [specific item — e.g. white powder residue on your right
hand] found [location — e.g. on your right hand at the time of your arrest at 14:32]?' The suspect
[response]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Be specific: name the item, the location, the time, the basis of
your belief. 'How do you account for the gunshot residue on your right index finger detected at
16:00?' — good. 'How do you account for things found?' — bad.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Generic question. (2) Item not properly identified by belief. (3) Item
not 'on person / in clothing / in place' at time of arrest.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Connolly; s.19; s.19A; Murray v UK.
Section 19, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Inference from failure to account for presence
FACTS: Where suspect was found at or near the place where, and at or about the time when, the
offence is alleged to have been committed, the Garda may request account of presence.
TEST: Specific place, specific time, specific request — all documented?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "You were observed at [place] at [time]. How do you account for your
presence at that place at that time?"
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Place specific, time specific. Tie to observation evidence (Garda
observation, CCTV, witness).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Vague location. (2) Time range too broad.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Connolly; s.18; s.19A.
Section 19A, Criminal Justice Act 1984 Inference from failure to mention fact later relied on
FACTS: Where suspect later relies on a fact at trial that was not mentioned during interview when
reasonably to be expected, inference may be drawn.
TEST: Warning given properly, with explanation of the provision, before relevant interview phases?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I administered the s.19A warning at [time]: 'If you fail or refuse to
mention any fact which you later rely on in your defence, a court may draw such inferences as appear
proper. Anything you do say may be given in evidence.' Suspect understood per [response]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Deploy early in interview to invite the suspect to give a full
account. Doesn't require specific question — it's a general warning.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Connolly; ss.18, 19.

5.2 Solicitor Access — Interview Phase
Gormley & White v DPP [2014] IESC 17
FACTS: (See Arrest section.) Suspects interviewed before solicitor consultation; admissions
challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Constitutional right to reasonable access to legal advice. Where suspect has requested
solicitor, no substantive questioning until consultation has occurred or reasonable efforts exhausted
with informed waiver.
RATIO: Solicitor access is gateway to fair interview.
TEST: Request made? Reasonable efforts made? Consultation before substantive questioning?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Solicitor request triggers hard stop on interview. Continue

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

administrative matters but no substantive questioning.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Doyle; Murray v UK.
DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1 (SC, 7 judges)
FACTS: (See Arrest section.) Solicitor presence vs consultation during interview.
PRINCIPLE: Constitutional right is to reasonable access (consultation) — not absolute right to
presence. Current practice facilitates presence.
RATIO: Consultation = constitutional minimum; presence = good practice.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Facilitate solicitor presence unless specific operational reason.
Brief solicitor on procedure. Allow mid-interview consultation where sought.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Gormley & White; Murray v UK.
DPP v Cromie [2017]
FACTS: Pre-interview disclosure to solicitor challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Pre-interview disclosure to solicitor should be sufficient to enable informed legal
advice without compromising the investigation. Practice has converged on balanced briefing.
TEST: Did the solicitor receive sufficient information for meaningful advice?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Pre-interview disclosure typically: nature of arrest, offence,
general categories of evidence, broad timeframe. Not typically: specific evidence about to be put,
witness names, detailed forensic results. Document what was disclosed.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Bare denial of all information. (2) Burning the case by over-disclosing
strategy.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Gormley & White; Doyle.

5.3 PEACE Model — Best Practice Methodology
PEACE Interview Model Best practice methodology (UK origin, adopted internationally)
FACTS: Structured interview methodology developed to produce better evidence than confessionseeking approaches.
PRINCIPLE: PEACE: Planning & Preparation; Engage & Explain; Account; Closure; Evaluation.
Each phase has specific aims and techniques.
RATIO: Structured interview produces better evidence than confession-seeking — better quality
evidence, more sustainable on appeal.
TEST: Is the interview structured to elicit information, not to extract confession?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: PLAN & PREPARE — read all evidence, identify topics in
sequence, plan question structure, anticipate accounts, identify evidence to deploy when. Written
interview plan before entering room. ENGAGE & EXPLAIN — introductions on record, caution
administered, purpose stated, ground rules. ACCOUNT — get the account first (open questions:
'Tell me about your day on [date]'). Let suspect speak. Active listening, minimal interruption.
PROBE — open questions before closed. 'Tell me more about...' before 'Did you...?' CHALLENGE
— with evidence, not assertion. 'CCTV shows you at [place] at [time]. Can you explain that?' Never
'we know you did it'. CLOSURE — summarise account, give opportunity to add/correct, end
formally on tape. EVALUATION — review interview, identify gaps, plan further session.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Interview plan on file. Recording captures execution.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Leading questions on key facts. (2) Multi-part questions (gives suspect
choice which to answer). (3) Repeated questions on same point (oppression). (4) Promises or
inducements. (5) Interview as monologue (suspect doesn't speak enough).
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Ward (oppression); DPP v Tyndall (inducement); DPP v Holland
(duration).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

DPP v Bowes & McGrath [2004] 4 IR 223 (CCA)
FACTS: Multiple Custody Regulations and interview breaches; question of cumulative effect.
PRINCIPLE: Court distinguishes bona fide procedural slips from deliberate/reckless breaches. Bad
faith breaches treated significantly worse. Honest acknowledgment in custody record protects the
case.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Honest custody record always. Tidy entries always; backfilled
tidy entries are exposed.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Buck; Custody Regulations 1987.
DPP v Madden [1977] IR 336
FACTS: Long detention; statement challenged on oppression.
PRINCIPLE: Oppression cumulative — length, isolation, repeated questioning, sleep deprivation,
atmospheric pressure.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Maximum 2-hour interview sessions. Genuine breaks. Document
suspect's apparent condition. Suspend if signs of distress/fatigue/intoxication.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Holland; DPP v Ward.
DPP v Holland (CCA, 1998)
FACTS: Lengthy interview without adequate breaks; suspect exhausted; confession excluded.
PRINCIPLE: Excessive interview duration without breaks producing exhaustion is oppression.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Max 2 hours per session. Genuine break — out of room,
refreshment, time. Document time in/out, refreshment offers, signs of distress.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Madden; DPP v Ward.
DPP v Ward [1998] (SCC)
FACTS: Confession in Guerin murder investigation; methods of questioning challenged. SCC
excluded confession on oppression grounds.
PRINCIPLE: Pressure is not only shouting. Repeated questioning, isolation, sleep disruption,
persistent confrontation — cumulative oppression.
TEST: Would a person of ordinary firmness have had will overborne?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Avoid: relentless repetition, theatrical anger or kindness, 'we
know you did it' suggestions, family references as leverage. Recording is protection both ways. Twoup interviews.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'We have evidence' when you don't. (2) Repeated questions to wear
down. (3) Tone outside professional range. (4) Manipulating breaks to prevent regrouping.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Holland; DPP v Madden; Saunders v UK.

5.4 Interpreters and Vulnerable Persons
DPP v Onumwere [2007] 3 IR 396
FACTS: Limited English suspect; statement challenged on inadequate understanding.
PRINCIPLE: Where suspect's English insufficient to understand caution/rights/questioning,
interpreter must be provided. Statements without adequate language support at risk of exclusion.
TEST: Did suspect actually understand?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Assess language at intake by reciprocal conversation. Don't ask
'do you understand English?' (answer always yes). Garda Síochána interpreter service. Interpreter
present throughout interview. Translates verbatim.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Cursory language assessment. (2) Family member as interpreter. (3)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Other suspects as interpreters. (4) Interpreter not present for full interview. (5) Caution in English
with no interpretation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 6 ECHR; EU Directive 2010/64; DPP v Avadenei.
DPP v Avadenei [2017] IESC 77
FACTS: Suspect in RTA matter not provided interpretation of specimen procedure.
PRINCIPLE: Interpretation rights extend to technical procedures (RTA specimen, fingerprinting,
sampling).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: RTA specimen: standard demand wording, interpreter where
needed. 20-minute observation maintained throughout interpretation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Onumwere; s.12 RTA 2010.
DPP v JM (Vulnerable adult) [2015]
FACTS: Intellectual disability suspect interviewed; safeguards questioned.
PRINCIPLE: Vulnerable adults require: (a) appropriate adult present; (b) plain language; (c)
frequent breaks; (d) careful pace; (e) confirmation of understanding; (f) medical opinion on fitness
where doubt.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Member-in-Charge screens at intake. PULSE flags. Behaviour
observed. Appropriate adult — not solicitor — family/advocate/Tusla for juveniles. Slow pace,
simple language, check understanding.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Vulnerability not assessed. (2) Family member doubling as interpreter
and appropriate adult. (3) No medical opinion when doubt.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Custody Regulations; appropriate adult provisions.
Children Act 2001 — Interviews of Juveniles Statutory framework
FACTS: Special protections for under-18s.
PRINCIPLE: Juveniles require parent/guardian notification immediately; appropriate adult for
interview; consideration of diversion before charge; special interview techniques; age-appropriate
care.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: JLO notified. Parent/guardian contacted. Appropriate adult —
typically parent/guardian or, if unavailable, social worker. Diversion considered before charge.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Children Act 2001; Garda Juvenile Diversion Programme; Children First
Act 2015.

5.5 Multi-Suspect Interview Considerations
DPP v Quinlivan [2005]
FACTS: Multiple suspects interviewed; question of cross-contamination between interviews.
PRINCIPLE: Each suspect interviewed separately. Information from one suspect's interview not
put to another except through proper procedure.
TEST: Each interview independent? Cross-references properly framed?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Separate interview teams where possible. Brief each team on
matrix — what each suspect told, what to put. Where putting Suspect A's statement to Suspect B:
structured — 'It has been alleged by [Suspect A] that [allegation]. What do you say?' Don't
paraphrase as Garda allegation. Document chronologically.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Same team interviewing all. (2) Leaking statement content informally.
(3) Failing to attribute properly.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Joint enterprise line; co-accused statements.

5.6 Recurring Pitfalls in Interview

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Recurring mid-level problems in interview rooms:
Off-tape conversations (recurring)
FACTS: Conversation with suspect outside formal interview that touches on substance.
PRINCIPLE: Anything elicited or volunteered after caution is potentially evidence. Off-tape
conversations create voluntariness and reliability issues.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If suspect speaks substantively outside interview, get back on the
record. 'You just told me X. I'd like to record that on the formal interview. Would you say it again?'
Don't carry information forward informally.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Tea and chat that becomes confession. (2) Corridor conversations. (3)
Booking-in admissions not properly cautioned.
Leading questions (recurring)
FACTS: Questions that contain the answer.
PRINCIPLE: Open questions before closed. 'What happened?' before 'Did you do X?'
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Open: who, what, where, when, why, how. Closed for
clarification: 'You said X — was that at 3pm or later?'
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'You assaulted him, didn't you?' (2) 'You went there to deal drugs.' (3)
'Tell me how you broke in.'
Repetitive questioning (recurring)
FACTS: Same question asked many times to wear down suspect.
PRINCIPLE: Oppression (Ward, Holland).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If you've asked a question 3 times and got the same answer, move
on. Return to it later in a different way if necessary.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Standing on a question for 15 minutes. (2) Re-asking in different
words to disguise repetition.
Mishandling 'no comment' (recurring)
FACTS: Suspect goes 'no comment'; interviewer becomes hostile or tries to extract.
PRINCIPLE: Silence is silence. Right to silence is constitutional unless inference regime properly
invoked.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If suspect goes 'no comment': continue your questions, note
responses, deploy inference warnings where appropriate per procedure. Don't argue, don't pressure,
don't editorialise. Each question stands as part of the record.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 'You're not helping yourself.' (2) Extended editorialising on silence.
(3) Suggesting silence = guilt.
