OFFENCES
Drugs, Organised Crime, Violence, Property, Domestic Violence, RTA

6. Drugs Offences
Drugs are the daily bread of inner-city policing. From simple possession (s.3 MDA) to wholesale-level supply
(s.15A MDA), the case is built from quantity, packaging, scales, cash, phones, lifestyle, and associations.
Drugs offences are the daily bread of inner-city policing. From simple possession (s.3 MDA) to wholesalelevel supply triggering presumptive 10-year sentences (s.15A MDA), the case is built from quantity, packaging,
scales, cash, phones, lifestyle, and associations. Each strand independently established; the cumulative picture

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

establishing the offence.

6.1 Possession — Section 3 MDA
STATUTE — Section 3, Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 Possession of controlled drug
FRAMEWORK: Possession of a controlled drug is an offence. Summary: District Court, up to 12
months / fine. Cannabis simple possession (first offence) typically diversion / fine. Repeat or larger
quantities escalate to indictment.
ELEMENTS: (a) Possession — physical custody OR control with knowledge; (b) controlled drug
(Schedule to Act + Regulations); (c) absence of authorisation (medical prescription etc).
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984 (where indictable) or arrest under s.23 MDA for
search purpose. Summary charge or direct charge.
TEST: Knowledge + control + drug identity?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges: (a) possession (was it actually his?); (b)
knowledge (did he know what it was?); (c) drug identity (FSI analysis); (d) chain of custody.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date] at [location], I conducted a search of [accused] under s.23
MDA 1977. In his [pocket/location], I located [quantity, description]. I asked: 'What is this?' He
replied: '[verbatim]'. I cautioned him and seized the item as exhibit [number]. FSI subsequently
confirmed the substance as [drug] of [weight]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city possession: small bags of cannabis, cocaine, MDMA,
occasional heroin. Field-test indicates; FSI confirms. Quantity matters — small amounts = simple
possession; larger = consider s.15 supply.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: FSI analysis cert. PEMS chain. Search evidence (s.23
grounds, witness Garda).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Inadequate s.23 grounds. (2) Chain of custody gaps. (3) Field-test
relied on without FSI confirmation. (4) Quantity not weighed properly.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15 MDA; s.15A MDA; s.23 MDA stop-search; DPP v Foley.
DPP v Foley [1995] 1 IR 267
FACTS: Possession case; question of knowledge.
PRINCIPLE: Possession requires knowledge — knowing one has the item, or having reason to
know. Mere physical proximity without knowledge is not possession.
TEST: Did the accused know they had the item, or have reason to know?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: In multi-occupancy contexts (vehicle, flat), each accused's
knowledge must be established separately. Statement on what was said at scene crucial.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Charging all occupants without individual knowledge evidence. (2)
Failing to take statements at scene.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Reilly (joint possession); s.3 MDA.
DPP v Reilly [2004] IESC 109
FACTS: Multiple occupants of vehicle; drugs found accessible to more than one.
PRINCIPLE: Joint possession requires knowledge + ability to control + intent to control. Mere
presence insufficient.
TEST: Each accused: knowledge + control + intent?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Photograph positions. Document each accused's statement.
Search results — drugs on specific person = sole possession of that person; drugs in common area =
joint possession arguable. Phone messages for shared enterprise.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Charging joint possession on mere presence. (2) Missing positional
detail.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Foley; vehicle search.

6.2 Sale/Supply — Section 15 MDA
STATUTE — Section 15, Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 Possession with intent to supply / supply
FRAMEWORK: Possession of controlled drug for the purpose of selling or otherwise supplying to
another. Indictable; up to 14 years (or life for some drugs).
ELEMENTS: (a) Possession; (b) of controlled drug; (c) intent to supply.
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984. Detention up to 24 hours; or s.16 CJ(DT)A 1996
for drug trafficking up to 7 days where threshold met.
TEST: Intent to supply — inferred from quantity, packaging, paraphernalia, cash, phones, lifestyle.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'personal use'. Prosecution: aggregated evidence
beyond personal threshold. Each strand established and weighed cumulatively.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date], I obtained search warrant under s.26 MDA from DJ
[name]. I executed at [address] at [time]. I located: [quantity of drug — weight, packaging detail];
[scales]; [bags]; [cash — denomination breakdown]; [phones]; [other indicia]. In interview, the
accused said [verbatim]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Building the case: quantity beyond personal use; packaging in
deal units (€20/€50/€100 equivalents); paraphernalia (scales digital or balance, bags, cling film,
cutting agents); cash denomination breakdown (€20s and €50s in quantity); phones (dealer line
separate from personal; messages re supply); lifestyle (declared income vs observed expenditure).
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: FSI analysis. FSI weight cert. Search warrant + grounds.
Photos of seizures in situ. PEMS chain. CCU phone analysis. Cash photograph + count witness.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Personal use range not exceeded clearly. (2) No paraphernalia. (3)
Cash not denominated. (4) Phone not properly examined. (5) Charge file thin on supply indicia.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15A MDA; s.3 MDA; DPP v Power; DPP v Foley.
DPP v Foley (Supply indicia) [1995]
FACTS: Indicia of supply: quantity, packaging, paraphernalia, cash.
PRINCIPLE: Intent to supply inferred from cumulative indicia. No single indicator determinative.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the picture: quantity + packaging + paraphernalia + cash +
phones + observation. Each strand independently proven.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15 MDA.

6.3 Aggravated Supply — Section 15A MDA
STATUTE — Section 15A, Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 (as amended) Possession with intent to supply —
€13,000 threshold
FRAMEWORK: S.15 offence where market value of drugs is at least €13,000 at date of offence.
Presumptive minimum 10-year sentence (subject to exceptional and specific circumstances
reducing).
ELEMENTS: (a) S.15 offence elements; (b) market value €13,000+ at date of offence.
POWERS / EFFECT: Standard search and arrest. Detention up to 7 days under s.16 CJ(DT)A
1996.
TEST: Market value at offence date (FSI valuation cert).
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: FSI valuation cert is the essential document. Defence may
challenge valuation methodology. Defence may argue exceptional circumstances reducing
presumptive sentence.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I seized [quantity, description]. The FSI valuation cert confirms
market value at €[amount] at the date of seizure, which exceeds the €13,000 threshold under s.15A

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

MDA."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: FSI valuation cert immediately on quantity that may exceed
threshold. Drugs photographed with standard scale and rigid ruler. Packaging units noted. Multiple
substances aggregated to total. The €13,000 threshold drives charge selection — just below: still
serious s.15; just above: presumptive 10-year minimum applies.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: FSI valuation, FSI analysis, photo evidence with scale,
weight cert.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Valuation not requested promptly. (2) Photo without proper scale. (3)
Packaging units not recorded. (4) Multiple substances not aggregated.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15 MDA; DPP v Power.
DPP v Power [2007] 2 IR 509 (SC)
FACTS: S.15A prosecution; market value at threshold.
PRINCIPLE: S.15A presumptive 10-year minimum subject to exceptional and specific
circumstances. Court considers individual circumstances; mitigation can reduce but only for specific
reasons.
RATIO: Major statutory aggravation for wholesale-level drug offences.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: FSI valuation date-specific to offence date. Document quantities
precisely.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15A MDA.

6.4 Stop-Search and Other Drugs Provisions
Section 23, MDA 1977 (See Search section.) Stop-search-detain power
FACTS: Power to stop, search, detain a person reasonably suspected of drugs offence.
PRINCIPLE: Articulable reasonable cause required.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Articulate grounds before stop. Specific facts: observation,
behaviour, location, time of day. Witness Garda. Notebook contemporaneous. PULSE entry.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Search section, s.23 MDA entry.
Section 26, MDA 1977 (See Search section.) Search warrant for drugs
FACTS: DJ warrant for drugs offence on sworn information.
PRINCIPLE: Reasonable grounds + specific premises + articulable intelligence/observation basis.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Application thorough. Premises specific. Information dated and
detailed.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Search section, s.26 MDA entry; Damache.
STATUTE — Section 17, MDA 1977 Cultivation of cannabis
FRAMEWORK: Cultivation of cannabis plants is offence. Indictable. Penalties scale with quantity.
ELEMENTS: (a) Cultivation (planting, tending, harvesting); (b) cannabis (plant).
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984.
TEST: Cultivation activity + cannabis plants?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On execution of warrant at [premises], I located in [room] [number]
cannabis plants in [stage of growth]. Equipment included [grow lights, ventilation, hydroponics,
etc]. FSI confirmed the plants as cannabis."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Grow houses common in inner-city flats and houses. Photograph
entire setup. FSI samples from plants. Equipment evidence (lights, ventilation, timers, fertilisers).
Electricity bypass often present — Networks Ireland involvement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Not photographing setup before dismantling. (2) Failing to sample
each plant for FSI. (3) Missing electricity bypass evidence.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15 MDA; s.15A MDA.
STATUTE — Section 21, MDA 1977 Aiding/abetting/conspiring
FRAMEWORK: Aiding, abetting, counselling, procuring, conspiring to commit drugs offence.
ELEMENTS: (a) Auxiliary conduct; (b) in respect of drugs offence.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Captures couriers, runners, lookouts, financiers. Build the
auxiliary role: communications, surveillance, financial flows.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.15 MDA; joint enterprise line.

6.5 Drug Trafficking Detention
Section 16, Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 (See Detention section.) Extended detention
FACTS: Detention up to 7 days for drug trafficking offences.
PRINCIPLE: Phased authorisations; major investigation context.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Used for s.15A/larger operations. DJ applications throughout.
Investigation strategy mapped to available time.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Detention section; s.50 CJA 2007.

7. Organised Crime, Firearms & Explosives
S.71-74 CJA 2006 — membership, participation, direction. Firearms offences with presumptive minimums.
Belief evidence regime. The case-law that supports complex inner-city operations.
Organised crime offences address persistent criminal structures — drug networks, feuds, professional theft
crews, firearms enterprises. The mid-level Garda typically supports DOCB/NBCI-led operations but must
understand the framework — particularly s.71 membership and s.71A belief evidence, which underpin many
inner-city investigations.

7.1 Definitions and Membership
STATUTE — Section 70, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Definitions
FRAMEWORK: 'Criminal organisation' = structured group, however organised, of three or more
persons, established over a period of time, acting in concert with primary objective of committing
or facilitating serious offences for material benefit. 'Serious offence' = 4+ years imprisonment.
ELEMENTS: Statutory definitions framework.
TEST: (1) Three or more persons? (2) Structure (not random)? (3) Period of time? (4) Concerted
action? (5) Primary objective of serious offences for material benefit?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Definition deliberately broad — captures loose networks as
well as hierarchical structures.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city feuds and dealing networks typically meet definition.
Build: persistent membership (phone analysis, surveillance log, social media), command structure
(runners, lookouts, enforcers, principals), repeated serious offences (drug seizures, firearms,
assaults), material benefit (cash, property, lifestyle).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.71; s.72; s.74; CAB.
STATUTE — Section 71, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Membership of criminal organisation
FRAMEWORK: Membership of criminal organisation. Max 15 years. Elements: (a) defendant is
member; (b) of criminal organisation per s.70; (c) knowing the nature.
ELEMENTS: Membership + knowledge.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

TEST: Member + criminal organisation + knowledge?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Distinct from specific crimes — membership prosecution stands
independent. Build via: surveillance, communications (encrypted apps, burners), social media
analysis, statements from co-accused (s.16 CJA 2006 if hostile), informant material (privileged).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.70; s.71A; s.72; DOCB/NBCI.
STATUTE — Section 71A, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Belief evidence (inserted by CJ(A)A 2009)
FRAMEWORK: Statement by Chief Superintendent that he believes accused was at material time
member of criminal organisation is admissible as evidence. Court considers basis; opinion not
conclusive.
TEST: Honestly held belief + sufficient basis + court weighs in overall picture.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Chief Super belief supported by surveillance, intelligence
reports, prior incidents, financial analysis, communications data, associate mapping.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Chief Super belief evidence is supported, not standalone.
Investigating member: assemble supporting material, brief Chief Super. Opinion must be Chief
Super's own, formed on material, articulable in cross. Document briefing trail.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Chief Super opinion without supporting material. (2) Briefing trail not
documented. (3) Chief Super unable to articulate basis in cross.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Walsh (belief evidence in SCC); s.71; SCC certification.
DPP v Walsh (Belief evidence) [2014] IECCA
FACTS: Walsh convicted of membership; Chief Super belief evidence challenged.
PRINCIPLE: S.71A compatible with fair trial provided: (a) belief genuinely held; (b) supporting
material exists; (c) defence has reasonable opportunity to test; (d) trial court directs appropriately on
weight.
RATIO: Belief evidence is a tool, not a presumption.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Prepare Chief Super for cross. Detailed briefing memo:
surveillance dates, intelligence sources (protected), incidents tied to organisation. Have non-belief
evidence in court too.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.71A; s.71.
STATUTE — Section 72, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Enhanced penalty — gang nexus
FRAMEWORK: Committing serious offence for benefit of/at direction of/in association with
criminal organisation. Up to 15 years consecutive to underlying offence.
ELEMENTS: (a) Underlying serious offence; (b) gang nexus; (c) knowledge.
TEST: Underlying offence proven + gang nexus proven?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Drivers, weapon-holders, financiers, drug runners — caught even
below membership threshold. Build nexus: who tasked? Who benefits? Phone traffic before and
after. Cell-site analysis, voice ID, money trail.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.71; s.74; DOCB.
STATUTE — Section 74, Criminal Justice Act 2006 Directing criminal organisation
FRAMEWORK: Directing activities of criminal organisation. Max life. Reserved for top-tier
figures.
ELEMENTS: (a) Directs; (b) activities of criminal organisation.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Top targets — Kinahan/Hutch principals, importation principals.
DOCB/NBCI/CAB joint. International co-operation (Europol, Interpol).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.70-72; SCC certification.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

7.2 Special Procedures for OC Cases
STATUTE — Section 7, Criminal Justice (Amendment) Act 2009 SCC certification
FRAMEWORK: DPP may certify that ordinary courts inadequate — case transferred to Special
Criminal Court. Used principally where jury intimidation risk.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: SCC trial: three-judge panel, no jury, more formalised.
Investigation file to higher disclosure standard. Witness intimidation considerations — anonymity,
special measures, s.16 statements at outset.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Offences Against the State Act 1939.
DPP v Special Criminal Court (Ward) — Informer privilege [1999] 1 IR 60 (SC)
FACTS: Defence sought disclosure of informant identity in Guerin murder trial.
PRINCIPLE: Informer privilege presumptive — protects sources, encourages reporting, maintains
operational capability. Exception: innocence at stake.
RATIO: Source protection presumptive; innocence-at-stake narrow exception.
TEST: (1) Privilege properly claimed? (2) Defence shown realistic prospect that disclosure helps
innocence? (3) Lesser steps possible?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: CHIS handling: source file separate from investigation file.
Restricted access. Investigating member rarely knows source identity. Never volunteer source
information — describe resulting product. 'As a result of confidential information received...' rather
than naming.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Volunteering source detail. (2) Source file co-mingled with
investigation. (3) Disclosure decisions made unilaterally.
CROSS-REFERENCES: PII; DPP v Marley; surveillance product.

7.3 Surveillance and Covert
STATUTE — Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009 Surveillance authorisation regime
FRAMEWORK: S.4 — Superintendent authorisation up to 72 hours. S.5 — DJ authorisation up to
3 months, renewable. S.7 — tracking devices. S.8 — urgent cases, post-event ratification.
ELEMENTS: Authorisation per category.
POWERS / EFFECT: Surveillance product admissible where regime complied.
TEST: Right authorisation for right type, properly grounded, product handled?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Application drafting: necessity (less intrusive means
considered/excluded), proportionality (intrusion vs offence seriousness), specificity (subject,
premises, duration). Product log, exhibit chain, disclosure (with PII for sensitive operational detail).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Surveillance outside authorisation scope. (2) Late renewal. (3) Product
chain gaps.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Reilly; DPP v Brennan; PII.
DPP v O'Reilly (Surveillance) [2009] 3 IR 196 (SC)
FACTS: Surveillance product central to drugs case; authorisation challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Surveillance Act 2009 strict regime. Authorisations grounded, time-limited, specific.
Product chain demonstrable.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Authorisation paperwork, deployment briefing, daily product log,
exhibit register, debrief notes.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Surveillance Act 2009; DPP v Brennan.
DPP v Brennan (Surveillance scope) [2015] IECCA

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FACTS: Surveillance product; authorisation timeline and scope challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Authorisation must be valid at time of each act, within scope (subject, premises,
vehicle, duration), properly extended.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Deployment log entries tied to authorisation reference. Timestamped product. Any deviation — pause, re-authorise.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Surveillance Act 2009.
STATUTE — Interception of Postal Packets and Telecommunications Messages (Regulation) Act 1993
Intercept regime
FRAMEWORK: Most intrusive tool. Ministerial authorisation on application by Garda
Commissioner. Strict procedures: application content, authorisation duration (3 months typical),
product handling, restricted access, destruction. Designated Judge oversight.
TEST: Application meets grounds + product handled per regime?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Reserved for most serious — murder, serious OC, terrorism.
Material sensitive at every level. Limited officer access. Disclosure via transcription; original under
seal. PII applications routine.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Application thin. (2) Product handling breach. (3) Disclosure beyond
authorised.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Holland (intercept admissibility); PII.
DPP v Holland (Intercept) [1998] CCA
FACTS: Intercept product critical; admissibility challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Intercept evidence admissible where statutory regime followed precisely; defects
fatal.
RATIO: Strict compliance — no judicial discretion to cure breaches.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Paperwork is the case. Authorisation file, product log,
transcription notes, voice ID statements.
CROSS-REFERENCES: 1993 Act.
DPP v Reid (Test purchase) [2002] IESC
FACTS: Test purchase by undercover Garda; entrapment / agent provocateur argument.
PRINCIPLE: Distinction: (a) providing opportunity for predisposed offender (lawful) vs (b)
inducing offence that would not otherwise happen (agent provocateur — exclusion).
TEST: Would offence have occurred without state involvement?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Target selection based on existing intelligence, not random.
Officer passive (responding to offer) better than active (soliciting). Brief / debrief recorded. Officer
anonymity often.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Undercover operations; informer privilege.

7.4 Proceeds of Crime
STATUTE — Proceeds of Crime Act 1996 (as amended) Civil seizure regime
FRAMEWORK: Civil regime allowing seizure and forfeiture of property representing proceeds of
crime. Balance of probabilities. Three-step: interim order, interlocutory order, disposal order. CAB
administers.
TEST: Property represents proceeds of criminal conduct on balance?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city dealing: cash seizures €5,000+, vehicles, property,
jewellery, watches all CAB referable. Build lifestyle case: declared income (Revenue), benefits
claimed, expenditure observed, assets. CAB intelligence officer joins early. Source-of-wealth

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

questions in interview.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Failing to refer to CAB. (2) Not building lifestyle picture. (3) Sourceof-wealth not put in interview.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CJ(MLTF)A 2010; CAB.
STATUTE — Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010 ML offences
FRAMEWORK: Handling, conversion, possession of criminal property knowing/suspecting it is
proceeds of crime.
ELEMENTS: (a) Property; (b) proceeds of crime; (c) knowledge / suspicion; (d) handling /
conversion / possession.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Charges run parallel to underlying offences. Cash carried = ML
possession. Cash deposited = ML conversion. Lifestyle disconnect from income = ML.
CROSS-REFERENCES: POCA 1996; CAB.

8. Violence, Public Order & Use of Force
The assault ladder (s.2/3/4 NFOAPA), threats, harassment, endangerment, public order, Garda use of force
under s.18 NFOAPA. Volume work for inner-city Gardaí.
Violence offences are the most common inner-city Garda work after public order and drugs. The Non-Fatal
Offences Against the Person Act 1997 codifies the assault regime — the ladder from common assault (s.2) to
assault causing harm (s.3) to assault causing serious harm (s.4). Charge selection is driven by the level of harm
caused, documented by injury evidence (photographs at scene + 48 hours later).

8.1 The Assault Ladder — Sections 2, 3, 4 NFOAPA
STATUTE — Section 2, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 Common assault
FRAMEWORK: Application of force / causing apprehension of force / impeding free movement.
Summary; up to 12 months / fine.
ELEMENTS: (a) Direct or indirect application of force OR causing apprehension of immediate
force OR impeding free movement; (b) intentionally or recklessly; (c) without lawful excuse; (d)
without consent (where required for the conduct).
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984 (arrestable offence at threshold of charge).
TEST: Force / apprehension / impeding + intent/recklessness + no excuse?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Witness evidence on conduct + injuries (if any) + accused
statement.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] at [location] I observed [conduct]. The injured party
showed me [injuries]. He stated [verbatim of complaint]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Common assault: pushes, slaps, threats with immediate
apprehension. No harm threshold required. CCTV / witnesses corroborate conduct. Photograph any
minor injuries.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Charging s.2 where s.3 evidence supports. (2) Failing to photograph
minor injuries. (3) Not capturing apprehension element (witness statement on fear).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.3 NFOAPA; s.4 NFOAPA; s.5 NFOAPA (threats).
STATUTE — Section 3, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 Assault causing harm
FRAMEWORK: Assault causing harm. 'Harm' includes pain, unconsciousness, any impairment of
physical condition. Indictable; up to 5 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Assault (per s.2 elements); (b) causing harm.
TEST: Assault + harm caused?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Medical evidence on harm. Often A&E examination.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Photographs day-of and 48 hours after.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The injured party attended [hospital] at [time]. Medical report by Dr
[name] documents [injuries — cuts, bruising, swelling, etc]. Photographs taken at [time] and again
at [+48 hours] show [injury development]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city bar/club assaults, DV assaults with visible injuries,
pub-stop assaults. Photograph immediately at scene/station, again 24-48 hours later (bruising
develops). Medical attendance encouraged for victim.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) S.3 charged without medical evidence. (2) No 48-hour photo. (3) Harm
element not articulated.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.2 NFOAPA; s.4 NFOAPA; medical evidence.
STATUTE — Section 4, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 Assault causing serious harm
FRAMEWORK: Assault causing serious harm. 'Serious harm' = injury creating substantial risk of
death OR causing serious disfigurement OR substantial loss/impairment of mobility, function of any
body part/organ. Indictable; up to life.
ELEMENTS: (a) Assault (per s.2); (b) causing serious harm.
TEST: Assault + serious harm?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Medical evidence on serious harm threshold. Often consultant
report. Long-term effects relevant.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Major assaults — knife wounds, severe blunt force, glassing,
baseball bat assaults. Medical report from consultant essential. Long-term injury follow-up.
Photograph at scene and through recovery.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Charging s.4 where harm is not 'serious' per statutory definition. (2)
Medical evidence thin on long-term impact.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.3 NFOAPA; medical evidence; mens rea.
DPP v Cagney (Assault mens rea) [2007] IESC 46
FACTS: Assault charge; question of mens rea required.
PRINCIPLE: S.3 / s.4 require subjective recklessness — accused must have foreseen risk of
harm/serious harm. Objective foreseeability not enough.
TEST: Did the accused foresee harm/serious harm?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the mens rea: nature of attack, weapon used, force applied,
accused's apparent knowledge.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Charging s.4 on objective foreseeability. (2) Failing to address
recklessness specifically.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.3/4 NFOAPA.

8.2 Threats and Harassment
STATUTE — Section 5, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 Threats to kill or cause serious
harm
FRAMEWORK: Threat by any means intending the other to believe it will be carried out.
Indictable; up to 10 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Threat to kill or cause serious harm; (b) made by any means (verbal, written,
gesture, online); (c) intention that other believes it.
TEST: Specific threat + intent to be believed?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Threat wording / content important. Means of communication
preserved (text, social media, voice message). Recipient's reaction relevant.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time], the accused said to the injured party: '[verbatim threat]'.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The injured party responded [verbatim] and stated to me [verbatim of fear/belief]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: DV cases: threats to victim recorded (texts, voice messages).
Other contexts: threats during disputes, gangland threats. Preserve digital evidence — screenshot +
extraction via CCU. Witness statement on perception of seriousness.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Generic 'threatening' description without verbatim. (2) Digital
evidence not preserved. (3) Recipient's belief element not captured.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.10 NFOAPA (harassment); s.39 DVA 2018 (coercive control).
STATUTE — Section 10, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 Harassment
FRAMEWORK: Persistently, without lawful authority/reasonable excuse, follows, watches,
pesters, besets or communicates with another, intentionally or recklessly, seriously interfering with
their peace and privacy or causing alarm, distress or harm. Indictable; up to 7 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Persistence (two or more incidents typically); (b) conduct of specified type; (c)
intent/recklessness; (d) serious interference / alarm.
TEST: Persistence + conduct + serious interference?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Pattern is the offence. Each incident documented and
chronologically presented.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The injured party reports incidents on [date 1] [conduct]; [date 2]
[conduct]; [date 3] [conduct]. Each documented in [PULSE/notebook]. Effect on her: [statement on
alarm, distress, change in routine, etc]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: DV / stalking cases: chronological log of incidents. Each incident
— calls, texts, social media, in-person, third-party messages, observation, visits — documented.
Pattern matters. Two or more typically required. Restraining order on conviction.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Single incident charged as harassment. (2) Pattern not documented
chronologically. (3) Effect on victim not captured.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.5 NFOAPA; s.39 DVA 2018; DV practice.
STATUTE — Section 13, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 Endangerment
FRAMEWORK: Intentional or reckless conduct creating substantial risk of death or serious harm.
Indictable; up to 7 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Conduct; (b) substantial risk of death/serious harm; (c) intent/recklessness.
TEST: Conduct + substantial risk + mens rea?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Drive-by shootings (no specific target identifiable), weapons
brandished, throwing objects from heights, dangerous driving at Gardaí, exposure of vulnerable
persons. Captures gravity distinct from result.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.4 NFOAPA; firearms offences.
STATUTE — Section 15, Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 False imprisonment
FRAMEWORK: Intentionally or recklessly takes / detains another, or causes them to be taken or
detained, without consent. Indictable; up to life.
ELEMENTS: (a) Taking or detaining; (b) without consent; (c) intent/recklessness.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Kidnap, false detention in DV (preventing leaving), tiger kidnaps,
taking children without authority. Photograph any restraint. Document time and conditions.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DV practice; kidnap line.

8.3 Domestic Violence
STATUTE — Domestic Violence Act 2018 Comprehensive DV regime
FRAMEWORK: Codifies orders (safety, protection, barring, emergency barring). s.30 — arrest for

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

breach. s.39 — coercive control offence.
ELEMENTS: Varies by section; structured around protection orders and pattern offending.
POWERS / EFFECT: Positive arrest for breach. Coercive control as pattern offence.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city DV: routine callouts. Positive arrest where evidence
supports — don't mediate. Photograph injuries day-of and 48 hours later. S.16 CJA 2006 sworn
statement at outset insulates against retraction. Coercive control captures pattern of behaviour,
timeline, controlling conduct, isolation, financial control, monitoring.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.16 CJA 2006; s.10 NFOAPA; Children First.
DPP v O'Reilly (DV) [1990] 2 IR 415 (SC)
FACTS: Spousal compellability in family violence context.
PRINCIPLE: Pre-CEA 1992 position narrowed by Act. Spouse of accused now generally
compellable for offences involving violence/threat against family member, sexual offences against
family, offences against child under 17.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Don't accept 'I'm his wife, I can't testify'. Explain compellability
— sympathetically. Build case independent of victim — photographs, 999 call, neighbours, prior
PULSE.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CEA 1992; s.16 CJA 2006.
Section 39, Domestic Violence Act 2018 — Coercive Control (Statutory framework)
FACTS: Offence of coercive control — pattern of controlling/coercive behaviour in intimate or
family relationship having serious effect on victim.
PRINCIPLE: Captures the pattern, not single incidents. Behaviour: isolating from friends/family,
monitoring, financial control, sexual coercion, threats, surveillance, repeated criticism,
manipulation.
TEST: Pattern + serious effect?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Schedule full follow-up statement covering: timeline of
relationship, controlling conduct (with examples), isolation tactics, financial control, monitoring
(phone, social media, GPS), sexual aspects, threats, third-party witnesses. Document the cumulative
effect on victim — changed routine, lost friends, depression, anxiety, fear.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating as series of separate s.2 assaults rather than pattern. (2)
Timeline not built. (3) Effect on victim not captured.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DVA 2018; s.10 NFOAPA; s.16 CJA 2006.

8.4 Public Order Offences
STATUTE — Section 4, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Intoxication in public place
FRAMEWORK: Person who is in public place while intoxicated to such extent as would give rise
to reasonable apprehension that he might endanger himself or others. Summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Public place; (b) intoxicated; (c) extent giving reasonable apprehension of
endangerment.
TEST: Public + intoxicated + endangerment apprehension?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Falling over, stepping into traffic, unable to walk safely, drunk in
charge of children. Where conduct includes disorder — charge s.6 instead (better captures element).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) S.4 where s.6 fits better. (2) Endangerment element not specified.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.5; s.6; DPP v Cagney; DPP v Forsey.
STATUTE — Section 5, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Disorderly conduct
FRAMEWORK: Person in public place engaging in offensive conduct (between midnight and 7am,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

or in such manner as gives rise to reasonable apprehension of breach of peace). Summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Public place; (b) offensive conduct; (c) time of day or apprehension of breach.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city night-time disorder: shouting, fighting starts, drunken
behaviour. Often charged with s.4/s.6 in alternative.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.4; s.6.
STATUTE — Section 6, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Threatening, abusive, insulting
behaviour
FRAMEWORK: Person who in public place uses or engages in threatening, abusive or insulting
words or behaviour, with intent to provoke breach of peace or being reckless as to whether such
breach might occur. Summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Public place; (b) threatening/abusive/insulting words or behaviour; (c) intent or
recklessness as to breach of peace.
TEST: Conduct + intent/recklessness?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Witness evidence on conduct + audience reaction. Recklessness
sufficient (Heeney).
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] at [public place], I observed the accused [specific verbatim
conduct — words used, gestures]. [Number] members of the public were present, [number] of whom
[reaction — moved away, stopped, exchanged looks]. The conduct in my opinion was such that a
breach of the peace was likely or threatened."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Capture conduct precisely. Verbatim words. Specific gestures
(raised fist, pointed finger, square-on body language). Audience reaction (others stopping, looking,
moving away). Time of day. Type of premises.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Vague conduct description. (2) No witness Garda corroboration. (3)
Audience reaction not documented.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Heeney; DPP v Forsey; s.4/5.
DPP v Heeney (Recklessness for s.6) [2001] 1 IR 736 (SC)
FACTS: S.6 conviction; recklessness sufficient as mens rea.
PRINCIPLE: Recklessness as to breach of peace sufficient for s.6 — doesn't require specific intent
to provoke.
TEST: Reasonable person foresee breach as likely?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Don't over-prove intent. Recklessness easier to establish from the
conduct itself.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.6 CJ(PO)A.
DPP v Forsey (Public place definition) [2018] IECA
FACTS: What constitutes 'public place'.
PRINCIPLE: Public place = any place to which the public have access, whether as of right or by
permission. Includes streets, parks, public transport, premises licensed to public, common areas of
buildings open to public.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Outside dwelling = generally public. Inside pub during opening =
public. Inside private flat = not public. Common hallways of apartment building — case-by-case.
CROSS-REFERENCES: All s.4-6 public order offences.
STATUTE — Section 8, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Failure to comply with direction
FRAMEWORK: Person fails to comply with direction of Garda given under power. Summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Garda gave lawful direction; (b) failure to comply.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Move on, disperse, leave area. Lawful direction must have basis
(preventing breach of peace, public safety).
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Direction without legal basis. (2) Direction not communicated clearly.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.6; common law direction.
STATUTE — Section 9, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Obstruction of peace officer
FRAMEWORK: Person obstructs Garda in execution of duty. Summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Garda in execution of duty; (b) obstruction.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Physical interposition, crowd interference, verbal obstruction
reaching threshold. Caution often given first ('move on or you'll be arrested for obstruction').
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.19 (assault on Garda); s.6.
STATUTE — Section 19, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Assault on peace officer
FRAMEWORK: Assault on Garda acting in execution of duty. Indictable; up to 7 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Garda in execution of duty; (b) assault.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Critical that duty element established — Garda was acting
lawfully. Often follows lawful stop / arrest / direction. Photograph injuries to Garda. Statement from
Garda + any witness members.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Duty element not established. (2) Witness Garda statement missing.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.2/3/4 NFOAPA; s.9 (obstruction).
STATUTE — Section 13, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 Trespass on building/curtilage
FRAMEWORK: Trespass on building or curtilage in manner causing or likely to cause fear in
another.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Squatter / anti-social occupation, persons creating fear in
tenants/owners. Engagement first; charge if necessary.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Burglary (Theft Act); s.11 FOWA (armed trespass).

8.5 Weapons Offences
STATUTE — Section 9, Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990 Knives/blades in public
FRAMEWORK: S.9(1) — having in public place knife or other article with blade or sharp point,
without good reason or lawful authority. S.9(4) — possession of other offensive weapon. Indictable;
up to 5 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Public place; (b) possession; (c) article meeting description; (d) absence of good
reason/lawful authority (defence burden after prosecution shows possession).
TEST: Article description met? Public place? Has accused given credible account?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Common defences: 'work tool' (employment context, time of
day), 'forgot it was there' (rarely credible), 'protection from others' (NOT lawful authority).
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On searching the accused under [power], I located in his [waistband
/ pocket] a [type of knife / blade]. The blade measured [length] using a rigid ruler. I photographed
the knife at [time] with [scale reference]. The accused stated [verbatim]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city knife crime: bread and butter. Photograph blade with
rigid scale ruler. Measure precisely. Document where carried. Search context: was search lawful
(s.30 OASA / s.23 MDA / s.30 CJ(PO)A / arrest)?
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) No scale in photo. (2) Length not measured. (3) Carriage location not
specified. (4) Search context uncertain.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.10 FOWA; s.11 FOWA; weapons line.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

STATUTE — Section 10, Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990 Production of weapon to intimidate
FRAMEWORK: Production in public place, in manner likely to intimidate, of article capable of
inflicting serious injury. Indictable; up to 5 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Public place; (b) production (showing, brandishing); (c) manner intimidatory; (d)
article capable of serious injury.
TEST: Article shown in manner calculated/likely to put person in fear?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Knife produced in dispute even if not used to strike. CCTV often
determinative. Witness statements on manner. Photograph article. Charge alongside s.6 CJ(PO)A
and s.2 NFOAPA if applicable.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.9 FOWA; s.2/3 NFOAPA.
STATUTE — Section 11, Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990 Armed trespass in dwelling
FRAMEWORK: Trespass in dwelling armed with firearm or offensive weapon. Indictable; up to
10 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Trespass; (b) dwelling; (c) armed (carrying for use as weapon).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Aggravated burglary (s.13 CJ(T&FO)A 2001) often more
appropriate where theft intent. S.11 covers armed trespass without theft intent — entry to threaten
or assault.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.13 CJ(T&FO)A 2001; FOWA line.

8.6 Firearms Offences
STATUTE — Section 27A, Firearms Act 1964 (as amended) Possession in suspicious circumstances
FRAMEWORK: Possession of firearm or ammunition in suspicious circumstances. Presumptive
5-year minimum (subject to exceptional circumstances).
ELEMENTS: (a) Possession; (b) firearm/ammunition; (c) circumstances giving rise to reasonable
inference not for lawful purpose.
TEST: Reasonable person infer not for lawful purpose?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Circumstances: concealment, location, loaded state,
accessibility, association with OC, lack of certificate.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Forensic examination: ballistics, serial number trace (often
obliterated — that itself probative), DNA, fingerprints. Photograph in situ with scale. Chain of
custody from scene to lab.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.27B; Firearms Acts 1925-2009; FOWA; SCC.
STATUTE — Section 27B, Firearms Act 1964 Possession with intent to endanger life
FRAMEWORK: Possession with intent to endanger life or to enable another to endanger life. Max
life.
ELEMENTS: (a) Possession; (b) intent (own or facilitating) to endanger life.
TEST: Intent inferred from circumstances — readiness to use against persons?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Loaded firearm in waistband during drive to known feud location.
Surveillance product placing weapon at dispute scenes. Phone evidence of plans.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.27A; conspiracy to murder.
DPP v Conroy (Joint possession of firearm) [2018] IECA
FACTS: Multiple occupants of vehicle; firearm recovered accessible to more than one.
PRINCIPLE: Joint possession requires knowledge + ability to control + intention (even briefly).
Mere presence insufficient.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

TEST: Each occupant: knowledge + control + intent?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Photograph each occupant's position. Forensic recovery on each
person (gunshot residue, DNA, prints). Statements at scene noted.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Reilly (joint possession drugs); s.27A.

8.7 Explosives
STATUTE — Explosive Substances Act 1883 (s.3, s.4) Manufacture / possession
FRAMEWORK: S.3 — unlawful manufacture / possession with intent to endanger life or property.
S.4 — possession of explosive substance under suspicious circumstances. Max life (s.3), 14 years
(s.4).
ELEMENTS: (a) Explosive substance; (b) intent (s.3) or suspicious circumstances (s.4).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Pipe bombs, IEDs, firework conversions, precursors. Scene
management: cordon, EOD callout (Defence Forces), no Garda movement near device until cleared.
Forensic recovery after clearance.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CT Investigations Unit / NBCI; SCC.

9. Property — Theft, Burglary, Robbery, Handling
Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 codified most of this regime. Theft, burglary (with Bowe
extension), robbery, handling, possession of stolen property.
Property offences — theft, burglary, robbery, handling stolen property — are codified in the Criminal Justice
(Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001. Charge selection turns on the elements: dishonesty for theft, entry-astrespasser for burglary, force-at-time-of-theft for robbery. Each element separately established and articulated.

9.1 Theft Offences
STATUTE — Section 4, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Theft
FRAMEWORK: Person guilty of theft if he dishonestly appropriates property without consent of
owner with intention of depriving owner permanently. Indictable; up to 10 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Dishonest appropriation; (b) of property; (c) without consent of owner; (d) intent
permanently to deprive.
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest under s.4 CJA 1984.
TEST: Dishonesty + appropriation + lack of consent + intent to deprive?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'I thought I could take it / borrow it / it was mine'.
Mens rea probed.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "On [date] at [premises], CCTV shows the accused [action — taking
item from shelf and concealing in bag]. He left the premises without paying. At interview he stated
[verbatim]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Shoplifting: CCTV is the case. Statement from store security.
Recovery of item from accused. Receipt non-payment evidence.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Dishonesty not articulated. (2) CCTV not recovered. (3) Item not
recovered/identified.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.12 burglary; s.14 robbery; DPP v Hayes.
DPP v Hayes (Dishonesty) (illustrative)
FACTS: Theft case; dishonesty element challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Dishonesty = objective standard with subjective element — accused knew conduct
was dishonest by standards of reasonable people.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Common defences: 'I thought it was mine', 'I was going to bring

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

it back', 'I was owed money'. Each addresses dishonesty. Build counter-narrative.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.4 Theft Act.

9.2 Burglary and Aggravated Burglary
STATUTE — Section 12, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Burglary
FRAMEWORK: Person guilty of burglary if he enters any building or part of a building as
trespasser with intent to commit arrestable offence OR having entered as trespasser commits or
attempts to commit arrestable offence. Indictable; up to 14 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Entry; (b) as trespasser (without consent / beyond permission); (c) building or
part of building; (d) intent to commit arrestable offence at entry OR commission after entry.
TEST: Entry + trespasser status + building + intent/commission?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'I had permission / went in by mistake / didn't intend
any offence'. Trespasser element explored.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The accused entered [premises] at [time]. He had no consent or
permission from [owner/occupier] to do so. He proceeded to [conduct — searched drawers, took
items, etc]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Domestic burglaries, commercial burglaries, entering staff-only
areas of shops (Bowe — staff-only burglary). Photograph point of entry (broken window, forced
lock, climbed wall). Recovery of items. Forensic — fingerprints, DNA from blood drops at entry,
footprints.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Trespasser status not established. (2) 'Part of building' (staff-only) not
articulated. (3) Intent at entry not built.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.13 (aggravated); s.4 (theft); DPP v Bowe; common law burglary line.
DPP v Bowe (Staff-only burglary) [2017] IECA 215
FACTS: Customer entered staff-only area to commit theft; charged with burglary rather than theft.
PRINCIPLE: 'Enters as trespasser' includes entering a part of building from which person is
excluded. Customer in staff-only = trespasser in that part.
RATIO: Burglary captures escalation when accused goes where they're not permitted.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Mountjoy district shoplifting: where suspect enters stock room,
behind-counter area, manager's office — burglary, not just theft. Photograph signage ('Staff Only').
Statement from manager confirming exclusion. CCTV showing entry to restricted area.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.12 burglary.
STATUTE — Section 13, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Aggravated burglary
FRAMEWORK: Burglary armed with firearm or other weapon of offence at time of burglary.
Indictable; up to life.
ELEMENTS: (a) Burglary (per s.12); (b) armed at time.
TEST: Burglary + armed?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Home invasions, tiger kidnaps, armed commercial burglary.
Photograph weapon recovered. Statement from witnesses on weapon visibility/use.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.12; s.14 robbery; s.11 FOWA.

9.3 Robbery
STATUTE — Section 14, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Robbery
FRAMEWORK: Person guilty of robbery if steals AND immediately before or at time of stealing,
and in order to steal, uses force on any person or puts any person in fear of immediate force.
Indictable; up to life.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

ELEMENTS: (a) Theft (per s.4); (b) force or threat of immediate force; (c) at time of or immediately
before theft; (d) in order to steal.
TEST: Theft + force/threat + temporal connection + purpose connection?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'force was after theft / not connected'. Temporal and
purposive links scrutinised.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "The accused approached [victim] at [location]. He [verbatim threat
or force used]. He took [items] from [victim]. The threat / force was used at the time of taking and
in order to take."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Mugging, knife-point robberies, distraction theft with force,
handbag snatches with push. Photograph injuries to victim. Recover item. Witness statements.
CCTV.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Force after theft (not robbery — theft + assault). (2) No connection
between force and purpose of theft.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.4 theft; s.13 aggravated burglary; assault offences.

9.4 Other Property Offences
STATUTE — Section 15, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Possession of article for
theft
FRAMEWORK: Possession of any article made or adapted for use in course of theft / burglary /
fraud, with intent so to use.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Bolt cutters, screwdrivers, glass cutters, cloning equipment. Intent
inferred from possession + circumstances + suspect's connection to incidents.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.4/12/14.
STATUTE — Section 17, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Handling stolen property
FRAMEWORK: Person who, knowing or believing it to be stolen, dishonestly receives or
undertakes/assists in retention/removal/disposal of stolen property. Indictable; up to 10 years.
ELEMENTS: (a) Property stolen; (b) accused knew or believed stolen; (c) dishonest
receipt/handling.
TEST: Stolen + knowledge/belief + handling?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Fences, recipients, retailers of stolen goods. Knowledge built
from: circumstances of acquisition (price, place), accused's history, statements at scene.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.18 possession suspected stolen.
STATUTE — Section 18, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Possession of suspected
stolen property
FRAMEWORK: Person in possession of property reasonably suspected to be stolen, who does not
give satisfactory account.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Used where handling not provable but possession + circumstances
clearly suspect. Suspect's account at interview crucial.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.17.
STATUTE — Section 6, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Deception
FRAMEWORK: Person who dishonestly, by deception, obtains property or makes gain or causes
loss to another.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Fraud, scams, false pretences. Documentary evidence often
central — bank records, communications, false documents.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.9 computer fraud.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

STATUTE — Section 9, Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 Computer fraud
FRAMEWORK: Dishonest use of computer to make gain or cause loss.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Online fraud, identity theft, account takeover. CCU involvement.
Production orders to platforms.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.6 deception; CCU.

10. Road Traffic — Procedure-Critical Offences
RTA is procedure-critical. The form is the case. Every box ticked, every demand verbatim, every observation
period strict. Drunk driving, drug driving, dangerous driving, failure to stop.
Road Traffic Act prosecutions are procedure-critical. Strict compliance regime — the form is the case. Miss a
box, lose the case. 20-minute observation period, prescribed wording for demands, certified statement form
(s.17 RTA 2010), designated doctor for blood/urine. The mid-level Garda must master this regime because
RTA work is daily.

10.1 Drink Driving
STATUTE — Section 49, Road Traffic Act 1961 (as amended) Drunk driving offences
FRAMEWORK: (1) Driving / attempting to drive / in charge of MPV in public place while
intoxicated to extent of inability to have proper control. (2)-(4) Driving over prescribed alcohol/drug
limits (specific concentration thresholds).
ELEMENTS: (a) Drive / attempt / in charge; (b) MPV; (c) public place; (d) intoxication / over
limits.
POWERS / EFFECT: Arrest s.49(8) without warrant. Specimen demand s.12 RTA 2010.
TEST: Each element established + procedure complied with?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Procedure-critical. Defence challenges: grounds for opinion,
20-minute observation, demand wording, machine calibration, form completion.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "At [time] at [location], I observed [driving behaviour — weaving,
slow at lights, etc]. On approach, I noted [smell of intoxicant, glassy eyes, slurred speech, unsteady
on feet]. I formed opinion of intoxication. I administered roadside breath test on calibrated device
[serial]. Result: fail. At [time] I arrested under s.49(8) RTA 1961."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build the opinion: driving observation + physical signs + roadside
test. Each adds weight. Note time of each observation. Calibrated device. Note device serial.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Opinion thinly grounded. (2) Roadside test not noted. (3) Device
calibration not available.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.12 RTA 2010; DPP v Avadenei; DPP v McNiece.
STATUTE — Section 12, Road Traffic Act 2010 Specimen demand at station
FRAMEWORK: Demand for specimen of breath (evidential breath machine), blood, or urine, on
arrest under s.49(8) RTA 1961. Prescribed wording. Designated doctor for blood/urine.
ELEMENTS: (a) Lawful arrest under s.49(8); (b) demand in prescribed terms; (c) sample provided
per procedure.
TEST: Demand prescribed + 20-min observation + sample procedure?
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: "I required the suspect under s.12 RTA 2010 to provide
[breath/blood/urine] specimen. I read the prescribed wording: '[verbatim]'. He responded [verbatim].
20-minute observation commenced at [time] — nothing in mouth, no smoking, eating, vomiting.
Specimen provided at [time] via [machine type/doctor name]."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: 20-minute observation: nothing in mouth — no smoking, eating,
drinking, vomiting. Restart clock if anything happens. Demand wording read verbatim from
prescribed form. Designated doctor for blood/urine. Chain of custody for blood/urine to MBRS lab.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

COMMON PITFALLS: (1) 20-min observation interrupted not noted. (2) Demand wording
paraphrased. (3) Form not completed. (4) Chain to MBRS broken.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.17 form; designated doctor; DPP v McNiece.
DPP v McNiece (Specimen procedure) [2003] 2 IR 614
FACTS: Specimen procedure challenged; multiple elements.
PRINCIPLE: Strict compliance. Each step matters: 20-minute observation, demand wording,
machine state, form completion.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Use prescribed forms. Tick every box. Sign and time.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.12 RTA 2010; s.49 RTA 1961.
DPP v Avadenei (Interpretation) [2017] IESC 77
FACTS: Limited English suspect; interpretation of specimen procedure.
PRINCIPLE: Interpretation rights extend to specimen procedure.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Interpreter where needed. 20-min observation continues during
interpretation. Demand wording translated verbatim.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Onumwere.
DPP v Moorehouse (Driving evidence) [2006] 1 IR 421
FACTS: Driving offence; evidence of driving.
PRINCIPLE: Driving evidence: who was driving? Garda observation strongest. Admission from
driver. Other occupants' statements.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: At stop, ask 'who was driving?' before getting answer or moving
people. Photograph keys location (ignition / in driver's possession).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.49 RTA.
DPP v Cabot (Form filling) [2004]
FACTS: Specimen form completion challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Form must be completed contemporaneously and accurately.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Don't leave to end of shift. Complete in real time. Every box filled
or NA.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.17 RTA 2010.

10.2 Drug Driving
STATUTE — Section 4 / 10, Road Traffic Act 2010 Drug driving
FRAMEWORK: Driving with impairing drug or specified drug above limit. Roadside drug test
(oral fluid) introduced 2017.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Roadside oral fluid test on stop. Positive = arrest, station blood
test via designated doctor. FSI MBRS analysis.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.49 RTA 1961; s.12 specimen procedure.

10.3 Other RTA Offences
STATUTE — Section 52, RTA 1961 Careless driving
FRAMEWORK: Driving without due care and attention. Summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Driving; (b) without due care.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Below dangerous threshold. Substandard driving observed.
Witness Garda or witness motorist statement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: s.53; s.49.
STATUTE — Section 53, RTA 1961 Dangerous driving
FRAMEWORK: Driving in manner dangerous to public having regard to all circumstances.
Indictable where causing death/serious injury; otherwise summary.
ELEMENTS: (a) Driving; (b) manner dangerous to public.
TEST: Manner objectively dangerous?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Speed, weaving, ignoring signals, driving on wrong side, racing.
Witness statements + Garda observation. Where causing death — careful investigation, accident
reconstruction expert.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.52; DPP v Quinlan.
STATUTE — Section 106, RTA 1961 Failure to stop / report
FRAMEWORK: Driver involved in accident must stop and give particulars / report to Gardaí
within reasonable time.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Hit-and-run. CCTV / witness recovery to identify driver. Damage
to vehicles.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Accident investigation.
STATUTE — Section 112, RTA 1961 Unauthorised taking of MPV (UTMV)
FRAMEWORK: Taking MPV without owner's consent, with intent to drive.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Joyriding, professional theft for chop shops. Recovery of vehicle.
CCTV. Forensic on vehicle (interior prints, DNA).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.53 dangerous driving; pursuit cases.
STATUTE — Section 41, RTA 1994 Uninsured driving
FRAMEWORK: Using vehicle without insurance.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Insurance check on stop. Section 41 charge typical for uninsured.
Often coupled with other RTA offences.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Insurance regime.
VOLUME 4
CONSTITUTIONAL & PROCEDURE
Rights, Bail, Charging, Court

11. Constitutional & ECHR Rights
Articles 38.1, 40.3, 40.4, 40.5 plus ECHR Articles 5, 6, 8. Proportionality. Damache. JC. The constitutional
architecture that underlies every policing act.
Constitutional and ECHR foundations underpin everything. Articles 38, 40.1, 40.3, 40.4, 40.5 of the Bunreacht,
alongside Articles 5, 6, 8 of the ECHR. The mid-level Garda doesn't need to be a constitutional lawyer but must
know where these foundations meet operational practice — fair trial, liberty, dwelling, privacy, equality, selfincrimination.

11.1 The Constitutional Rights
Article 38.1 Bunreacht na hÉireann Fair trial
FACTS: 'No person shall be tried on any criminal charge save in due course of law.'
PRINCIPLE: Foundational right. Encompasses procedural fairness, legal representation,

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

disclosure, presumption of innocence, fair hearing, impartial tribunal.
TEST: Has the trial process complied with due course of law?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Every procedural shortcut risks Article 38.1 challenge. Document
the procedural path: arrest, caution, detention, interview, charge, file, court.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 6 ECHR; Healy v Donoghue (legal aid); fair trial line.
Article 40.1 Bunreacht na hÉireann Equality
FACTS: 'All citizens shall, as human persons, be held equal before the law.' Permits distinctions of
capacity, physical/moral and social function.
PRINCIPLE: Equality baseline subject to legitimate distinctions.
TEST: Differential treatment objectively justified?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city policing: heightened sensitivity to perceived
discriminatory enforcement. Document objective grounds for every action. Even-handed
enforcement: similar conduct, similar response.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 14 ECHR; community trust.
Article 40.3 Bunreacht na hÉireann Personal rights
FACTS: State guarantees in laws to respect, defend and vindicate personal rights of citizens.
PRINCIPLE: Personal rights protected including enumerated and unenumerated (bodily integrity,
fair procedures, privacy, communicate, marital privacy, work).
TEST: Does the action respect personal rights?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Every policing action engages personal rights. Search engages
bodily integrity and privacy. Detention engages liberty. Questioning engages self-incrimination
right. Proportionality is the constant test.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 8 ECHR; Heaney v Ireland.
Article 40.4.1 Bunreacht na hÉireann Liberty
FACTS: 'No citizen shall be deprived of his personal liberty save in accordance with law.'
PRINCIPLE: Every detention requires statutory basis. No general power to detain.
TEST: Specific statutory authority for deprivation of liberty?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Every detention has a section you can name. Court testimony: 'I
detained the suspect under section [x] of the [Act] on grounds of [stated grounds].' Habeas corpus is
the remedy.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 5 ECHR; Dunne v Clinton; Re Ó Laighléis.
Article 40.4.2 Bunreacht na hÉireann Habeas corpus
FACTS: Foundational right to immediate judicial inquiry into lawfulness of detention.
PRINCIPLE: High Court may inquire into lawfulness of any detention and order release where
unlawful.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: If habeas corpus threatened, file must be presentable in minutes.
Custody record, authorisations, briefings — all to hand. Weak foundation: charge or release before
application is heard.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 5(4) ECHR; Re Ó Laighléis.
Article 40.5 Bunreacht na hÉireann Dwelling
FACTS: 'The dwelling of every citizen is inviolable and shall not be forcibly entered save in
accordance with law.'
PRINCIPLE: Highest constitutional protection for home. Forcible entry requires specific legal
